|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+

Key idea:

  All proof methods reduce to demonstrating that a certain
  object lies within nested sets.

  To prove Q = to show a path of inclusions leading to Q.


 Geometric interpretation of proof methods

 Every proof method has a simple visualization through
 nested sets on a Venn diagram. This makes logic intuitive.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Modus ponens: "If P, then Q. P is true. Therefore, Q is true."
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Formula: P -> Q,  P  |-  Q

   Geometrically:
   If P sub Q (circle P entirely inside circle Q)
   and object x in P
   then x in Q (obvious)

       +================================+
       |            Q                   |
       |      +------------+            |
       |      |     P      |            |
       |      |   * x      |            |  <-- x in P, but P inside Q
       |      +------------+            |    => x automatically in Q
       +================================+

   Example: "All students have a student ID."
            "Masha is a student."
            => "Masha has a student ID."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Reductio ad absurdum: "Assume the opposite. We get absurdity."
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Formula: Assume ~Q. Derive contradiction. Therefore, Q.

   Geometrically:
   Suppose P sub Q (P inside Q).
   Assume: object x belongs to P and simultaneously does not belong to Q.
   But this is a contradiction. (x cannot be in P and outside Q if P sub Q)
   Therefore, if x in P, then x in Q.

       +================================+
       |            Q          ######## | <-- Q^c (complement)
       |      +------------+   # ~Q  ## |
       |      |     P      |   ######## |
       |      |            |   ######## |
       |      +------------+   ######## |
       +================================+

   Attempting to place a point simultaneously in P and in Q^c is a contradiction.
   These regions do not intersect.

   Example: "Prove sqrt(2) is irrational"
            Assume sqrt(2) in Q => get contradiction => sqrt(2) not in Q

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Philosophical remark
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Proofs are not the pinnacle of mathematics, but a crutch to compensate
 for losses during linguistic transmission. In an ideal world of telepaths,
 where one could directly "show" the geometry of inclusions, formal proofs
 would not be needed.

 Formalization of mathematics is not rigor for its own sake, but a forced
 measure when transitioning to linguistic description. Mathematics itself
 exists at a pre-linguistic level of manipulating images and set inclusions.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Unity: logic = sets = order
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Preliminary: What is an order?

  A partial order <= on a set X is a relation that is:
    * Reflexive: x <= x for any x
    * Antisymmetric: x <= y and y <= x => x = y
    * Transitive: x <= y and y <= z => x <= z

  Examples: sub on sets, <= on numbers, "divides" on naturals.

 Logic, sets, and order are three languages describing one structure.

 This is not an analogy. It is an identity. Stone's theorem (1936) proves:
 Boolean algebra iso algebra of sets iso propositional logic


 One object — three languages
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
| Logic            | Sets                 | Order / Lattice    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| Proposition P    | Set A                | Element a          |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| P ^ Q  (and)     | A cap B (intersect.) | a ^ b  (meet, inf) |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| P v Q  (or)      | A cup B (union)      | a v b  (join, sup) |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| ~P  (not)        | A^c  (complement)    | ~a  (complement)   |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| P => Q (implies) | A sub B (inclusion)  | a <= b  (order)    |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| truth T          | Universe U           | Maximum 1          |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+
|                  |                      |                    |
| falsehood F      | Empty set            | Minimum 0          |
|                  |                      |                    |
+------------------+----------------------+--------------------+

 The laws are the same in all three languages


 De Morgan:  ~(P^Q) = ~Pv~Q        |  (AcapB)^c = A^c cup B^c  |  likewise
             ~(PvQ) = ~P^~Q        |  (AcupB)^c = A^c cap B^c  |

 Distributivity: P^(QvR)=(P^Q)v(P^R) | Acap(BcupC)=(AcapB)cup(AcapC) |
                 Pv(Q^R)=(PvQ)^(PvR)  | Acup(BcapC)=(AcupB)cap(AcupC) |

 Excluded middle: P v ~P = T       |  A cup A^c = U        |  a v ~a = 1
 Contradiction:   P ^ ~P = F       |  A cap A^c = empty    |  a ^ ~a = 0

 These are not "similar laws" — they are one law written three ways.


 Lattice and Boolean algebra


 Lattice = partial order where any two elements have:
   a v b = sup{a, b}  (least upper bound, join)
   a ^ b = inf{a, b}  (greatest lower bound, meet)

 Boolean algebra = lattice with complement:
   For every a there exists ~a:  a v ~a = 1  and  a ^ ~a = 0

+--------------------+-------------+-----------+
| Example            | v (join)    | ^ (meet)  |
+--------------------+-------------+-----------+
| Subsets 2^X        | A cup B     | A cap B   |
| Divisors of n      | lcm(a, b)  | gcd(a, b) |
| Propositions       | P v Q (or)  | P ^ Q(and)|
| Open sets          | U1 cup U2   | U1 cap U2 |
+--------------------+-------------+-----------+

 Note: Divisors of 12 form a lattice, but not Boolean (no complement for 2,3,4,6).
       Power set of subsets is Boolean (complement A^c always exists).

 Intuitionistic logic and topology

 The table above holds for classical logic (Boolean algebra).
 But there is intuitionistic logic, where ~~P != P and P v ~P is not always true.

 +---------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Classical           | Intuitionistic                 |
 +---------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Boolean algebra     | Heyting algebra                |
 | Subsets             | Open sets of a topology        |
 | P v ~P = T (always) | P v ~P != T (not always)       |
 | ~~P = P             | ~~P != P                       |
 | A cup A^c = U       | U cup Int(U^c) != X in general |
 +---------------------+--------------------------------+

 Why open sets:
   * ~U = Int(U^c) = interior of complement (not the complement itself)
   * U cup ~U = U cup Int(U^c) may not equal X
   * Example: U = (0,1) on R. Then ~U = Int((-inf,0] cup [1,inf)) = (-inf,0)cup(1,inf)
     and U cup ~U = R \ {0,1} != R.

 Connection with constructivism:
   In intuitionistic logic "exists" = "can be constructed."
   The law of excluded middle (P v ~P) is not valid, because
   the impossibility of constructing a counterexample does not
   imply the existence of an example.
===============================================================================
The axiomatic method — rules of the mathematical game
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 What is an axiom
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 An axiom is a statement accepted without proof.

 Why without proof? Because any proof relies on some prior statements.
 If we demand proving everything, we get an infinite chain or a closed loop.
 We need a foothold.

Analogy: Rules of a board game

   * You do not "prove" that in chess the knight moves in an L-shape
   * It is a rule of the game — we accept it in order to play
   * Axioms = rules of the mathematical game
   * Theorems = everything that can be derived from the rules

 Important: Axioms are not "true" or "false" in any absolute sense.
 They are conventions. Different sets of axioms yield different mathematics.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why the axiomatic method is needed
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 History: Until the late 19th century, mathematicians worked "intuitively."
 Then paradoxes appeared — logical contradictions.

 Russell's paradox (1901):

   Let R = {x : x not-in x} — "the set of all sets that do not contain themselves"

   Question: R in R or R not-in R?

   * If R in R, then by definition R must be R not-in R — contradiction
   * If R not-in R, then by definition R must be R in R — contradiction

 Conclusion: One cannot simply create "the set of everything satisfying
 a condition." Restrictions are needed — axioms defining which sets
 are "legal."

 Solution: The ZFC axiom system (Zermelo-Fraenkel with the Axiom of Choice)


===============================================================================
ZFC axioms — the foundation of modern mathematics
===============================================================================

ZFC = Zermelo-Fraenkel + Choice

This is a system of ~9 axioms on which nearly all modern mathematics is built.

 Notation

   forall x — "for all x" (universal quantifier)
   exists x — "there exists x" (existential quantifier)
   ->  — "implies"
   <-> — "equivalent," "if and only if"
   ^   — "and" (conjunction)
   v   — "or" (disjunction)

 ZFC axioms — summary table
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| Axiom         | Formula                    | What it gives                  |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 1. Extension- | (forall x: x in A <->     | A set = its elements.          |
|    ality      |            x in B) -> A=B  | {1,2,3} = {3,1,2}             |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 2. Empty Set  | exists empty:              | The "zero" of set theory.      |
|               |   forall x: x not-in empty | Starting point for all         |
|               |                            | constructions                  |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 3. Pairing    | forall a,b exists P:       | We can create {a,b}.           |
|               |   x in P <-> (x=a v x=b)  | Consequence: {a}={a,a}         |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 4. Union      | forall A exists U:         | We can merge sets.             |
|               |  x in U <-> exists B       | cup{{1,2},{3}} = {1,2,3}       |
|               |  (B in A ^ x in B)        |                                |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 5. Power Set  | forall A exists P:         | Set of all subsets.            |
|               |   B in P <-> B sub A       | P({1,2}) = {empty,{1},{2},     |
|               |                            |             {1,2}}             |
|               |                            | |P(A)| = 2^|A|                |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 6. Infinity   | exists I: empty in I ^     | Infinite sets exist.           |
|               |  (x in I -> x cup {x}in I)| The minimal such I = N         |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 7. Separation | forall A exists B:         | {x in A: phi(x)} is legal.    |
|               |  x in B <-> (x in A ^     | {x: phi(x)} is not legal.     |
|               |              phi(x))      | (avoids Russell's paradox)     |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 8. Replace-   | The image of a set under   | {f(a): a in A} is a set.      |
|    ment       | a function is a set        | Needed for large cardinals     |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+
| 9. Foundation | A != empty ->              | No x in x, no cycles a in     |
|    (Regularity)|  exists x in A: x cap A   | b in a. Everything is built    |
|               |  = empty                   | "from below" out of empty      |
+---------------+----------------------------+--------------------------------+

 Important subtlety: axiom schemas

 Axioms 7 (Separation) and 8 (Replacement) are not single axioms but schemas:
 for each formula phi(x) we get a separate axiom.

   * "Separation with phi(x) = (x is even)" — one axiom
   * "Separation with phi(x) = (x is prime)" — another axiom
   * ...and so on for every possible formula

 Therefore ZFC formally contains infinitely many axioms.
 This is not a problem — we can still verify any specific proof in finite time.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Russell's paradox and how ZFC resolves it
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Paradox: Let R = {x : x not-in x}. Then R in R <=> R not-in R. Contradiction.

 Resolution in ZFC:
   The Separation axiom forbids constructing {x : phi(x)}.
   Only {x in A : phi(x)} is allowed — a subset of an already existing A.

   To build R = {x : x not-in x}, one would first need a set A
   containing R. But no such A exists (by Foundation, x not-in x always).

 Conclusion: ZFC is "freedom with responsibility." One cannot create
 sets "from thin air" — only from already constructed ones.


 Philosophical meaning of the axioms
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Axiom         | Philosophical interpretation                             |
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Empty set     | Formalization of "void" — we postulate its existence     |
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Infinity      | Infinity is not obvious but a choice to play this kind   |
|               | of mathematics. One can build math without this axiom    |
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Separation    | Restriction on "naive" set creation — lesson from        |
|               | Russell's paradox. Freedom with responsibility           |
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Foundation    | Everything is built hierarchically from empty. No        |
|               | "hanging in mid-air" or self-referential constructions   |
+---------------+----------------------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Visualization: the cumulative hierarchy — "tower of sets"
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The ZFC axioms generate all of mathematics from nothing (the empty set).
 This happens level by level — the cumulative hierarchy V_alpha.


   V_{w+1} | P(V_w) — power set of an infinite set
            | Includes R, all functions N -> N, ...
            | Already uncountable.
   ---------+--------------------------------------------------------------
            |
   V_w      | V_0 cup V_1 cup V_2 cup ... — first infinite level
            | Includes N, all finite sets, all finite structures
   ---------+--------------------------------------------------------------
    ...     | ...
   V_3      | P(V_2) = all subsets of V_2
            | |V_3| = 2^2 = 4 elements
   ---------+--------------------------------------------------------------
   V_2      | P(V_1) = {empty, {empty}}
            | 2 elements — can identify with {0, 1}
   ---------+--------------------------------------------------------------
   V_1      | P(V_0) = P(empty) = {empty}
            | 1 element — this is "1" in the construction of naturals
   ---------+--------------------------------------------------------------
   V_0      | empty — the empty set, the beginning of everything
            | 0 elements — this is "0"
   =========+==============================================================

 Growth of sizes:

   |V_0| = 0
   |V_1| = 2^0 = 1
   |V_2| = 2^1 = 2
   |V_3| = 2^2 = 4
   |V_4| = 2^4 = 16
   |V_5| = 2^16 = 65536
   |V_6| = 2^65536 ~ 10^19728 — already unimaginable.
   ...
   |V_w| = aleph_0 (countable infinity)
   |V_{w+1}| = 2^{aleph_0} = |R| (the continuum)

 Conclusion: All of mathematics "grows" from empty by applying one operation P.


 Where familiar objects live

 +----------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Object               | First level where it appears   |
 +----------------------+--------------------------------+
 | empty = 0            | V_0                            |
 | 1 = {empty}          | V_1                            |
 | 2 = {empty, {empty}} | V_2                            |
 | n (any finite)       | V_n                            |
 | N (as a set)         | V_w                            |
 | Function f: N -> N   | V_{w+1} (as subset of N x N)   |
 | R (as Dedekind set)  | V_{w+1}                        |
 | Function f: R -> R   | V_{w+2}                        |
 | Space C[0,1]         | V_{w+2}                        |
 +----------------------+--------------------------------+

 Nearly all "working" mathematics lives in V_{w+w} — relatively low.
 Large cardinals (inaccessible, measurable, ...) require very high V_alpha.


===============================================================================
The Axiom of Choice — special status
===============================================================================
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Axiom of Choice (AC)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Formula: forall A: (forall A in A: A != empty) -> exists f: forall A in A: f(A) in A

 Meaning: For any family of nonempty sets there exists a function
          that picks one element from each.

 The word "simultaneously" is psychological, not mathematical.
    Mathematically: a choice function f exists.
    There is no "process of choosing" — the function simply is (or is not).

        +---+  +---+  +---+  +---+
        | * |  | ^ |  | # |  | + |  ...  infinitely many boxes
        | o |  | v |  | = |  | - |
        +---+  +---+  +---+  +---+
          |      |      |      |
          *      ^      #      +    <-- choice function f exists

 Without AC in some models of ZF: the product of nonempty sets is empty.
 This is not "we cannot choose" but "a choice literally does not exist."


 Why the Axiom of Choice is not obvious
+------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Case                   | Situation                                      |
+------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Finitely many boxes    | Obvious — just enumerate (AC not needed)       |
+------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Countably many         | The countable axiom (AC_w) suffices             |
|                        | — weaker than full AC                          |
+------------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Uncountably many       | How to choose? No algorithm. AC asserts that   |
|                        | a function exists, even without explicit        |
|                        | construction                                   |
+------------------------+------------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Consequences of the Axiom of Choice
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Good consequences:

   * Every vector space has a basis
   * Every ideal is contained in a maximal ideal
   * Tychonoff's theorem: the product of compact spaces is compact
   * Hahn-Banach theorem: extension of linear functionals
   * Any two cardinals are comparable: |A| <= |B| or |B| <= |A|

 Paradoxical consequences:

   * Banach-Tarski paradox:
     A ball can be cut into 5 pieces and reassembled into two identical balls.
     (the pieces are not "ordinary" fragments but pathological sets of points)
   * Non-measurable sets (in the sense of Lebesgue) exist

 Status:
   The Axiom of Choice is independent of ZF — it can be neither proved
   nor disproved. One can work with it (ZFC) or without it (ZF).
   Most mathematicians accept AC.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 "Choice" in philosophy vs "choice" in the axiom
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 In the philosophical foundation, "choice" = the subject's ability to
 distinguish, to draw boundaries, to single out objects from the void.
 This is ontological choice.

 In the Axiom of Choice, "choice" = the existence of a function assigning
 an element to each set. This is a technical capability.

 Connection:
   Philosophical choice is primary (without it there is no mathematics at all).
   The Axiom of Choice is a specific rule of the game, which one can accept
   or reject.

 Difference:
   Philosophical choice: "I can single out an object"
   Axiom of Choice: "For any (even uncountable) family there exists
                      a way of simultaneous selection"

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why all this is needed
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The ZFC axioms are not "truths about the world."
 They are rules of the game that:

   1. Are strong enough to build all known mathematics
   2. Are sufficiently restricted to avoid paradoxes
   3. Are (as far as we know) consistent
   4. Are incomplete (Goedel's theorem, 1931): there exist true statements
      about natural numbers that are unprovable in ZFC

 Incompleteness is not a bug but a fundamental property of any sufficiently
 rich formal system. ZFC cannot prove its own consistency from within.

 When later we write "there exists a set X" or "choose an element from Y,"
 we are relying on these axioms. Mathematics is the consequences of these rules.

 Now, with the foundation in place, we can build numbers.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Goedel's theorems — limits of formal systems
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Context:

 Goedel's theorems (1931) are not abstract logic. They are fundamental
 results about what mathematics can and cannot do:

   * Why certain problems are in principle undecidable
   * Why a machine cannot fully replace a mathematician
   * Why "truth" and "provability" are different things
   * Why we cannot be sure of the consistency of mathematics

 For the engineer: if a problem is undecidable, it is not our fault —
 it is a boundary of the theory.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Goedel's first incompleteness theorem
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Statement (simplified):

   In any consistent formal system rich enough to describe the arithmetic
   of natural numbers, there exists a statement G that is:
     * true (in the standard interpretation)
     * unprovable in that system

 Intuition — the "liar's paradox":

   Consider the sentence: "This sentence is false."
   If it is true — then it is false (contradiction).
   If it is false — then it is true (contradiction).
   Paradox. This sentence has no truth value.

   Goedel constructed a mathematical analogue:
   "This statement is unprovable in system S."

   If it is provable — we proved a falsehood (the system is inconsistent).
   If the system is consistent — it is unprovable.
   But then it is true (since it asserts its own unprovability).

 Proof idea:

   1. Goedel encoded formulas as numbers (Goedel numbering)
      Each symbol gets a number; a formula becomes a product of prime powers

   2. Proofs also became numbers
      "x is a proof of formula y" is an arithmetic relation.

   3. He constructed a formula G that speaks about itself:
      G = "There is no number encoding a proof of formula G"

 Consequence: ZFC (and any "reasonable" system) is incomplete.
   There exist true statements about numbers that ZFC will not prove.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Goedel's second incompleteness theorem
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Statement:

   A consistent system rich enough for arithmetic
   cannot prove its own consistency.

 Intuition:

   The statement Con(S) = "System S is consistent" can be written
   as an arithmetic formula (via Goedel numbering).

   Goedel showed: Con(S) -> G
   (If S is consistent, then G is true)

   If S could prove Con(S), it would prove G.
   But G is unprovable (by the first theorem).
   Therefore Con(S) is also unprovable in S.

 Philosophical meaning:

   Mathematics cannot guarantee its own reliability.
   We cannot prove that ZFC will not lead to a contradiction.
   We simply trust it (and so far no contradictions have been found in 100 years).

   This is similar to bootstrapping: you cannot verify a compiler with itself.


 What this means in practice

 +----------------------------------+---------------------------------+
 | Incompleteness does not mean     | Incompleteness means            |
 +----------------------------------+---------------------------------+
 | "Mathematics is useless"         | There are principled limits     |
 | "Nothing can be proved"          | Some questions are undecidable  |
 | "Computers cannot prove things"  | Full automation is impossible   |
 | "We should abandon formalism"    | Intuition remains important     |
 +----------------------------------+---------------------------------+

 Examples of undecidable statements:

   * Continuum hypothesis: |R| = aleph_1?  — independent of ZFC
   * Axiom of Choice — independent of ZF
   * Certain combinatorial statements (Paris-Harrington)

 For the engineer:

   99.9% of practical problems are not affected by incompleteness.
   Goedel's theorems matter for understanding limits, not for daily work.
   If an algorithm does not find a solution — maybe the problem is undecidable,
   but usually you just need to search harder.

 Unifying idea: There exist questions that a formal system cannot answer.
 This is not weakness — it is a theorem.
===============================================================================
Relations — how structure arises from sets
===============================================================================

"All of mathematics = the study of relations between objects"

 Relation = a way to say "these objects are somehow connected"

 In everyday life we constantly use relations:

   * "Ivan is the father of Peter"      (relation "being a father")
   * "Moscow is larger than Tver"       (relation "larger by population")
   * "5 is less than 7"                 (relation "less than")
   * "These two triangles are similar"  (relation "similarity")
   * "A and B are friends"              (relation "friendship")

 Key idea: A relation is simply a set of pairs (or triples, etc.)
 that we consider "connected."

   "less than" on N = {(1,2), (1,3), (2,3), (1,4), (2,4), (3,4), ...}

   Writing "3 &lt; 5" simply means "(3, 5) in R," where R is "less than."

 Why formalize?
   * We can prove properties of relations
   * We can combine relations (composition)
   * We can classify relations by properties
   * Foundation for order, equivalence, functions


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Formal definition
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Cartesian product

 A x B = {(a, b) | a in A, b in B}  — the set of all ordered pairs

 Example: {1, 2} x {a, b} = {(1,a), (1,b), (2,a), (2,b)}

 Formal definition of a pair:
   (a, b) := {{a}, {a, b}}

   This allows distinguishing (a,b) from (b,a): {{a},{a,b}} != {{b},{a,b}} when a!=b
   Important: the ordered pair is built from unordered sets.

 Generalization to n sets:
 A_1 x A_2 x ... x A_n = {(a_1, a_2, ..., a_n) | a_i in A_i}  — n-tuples

 Example: R x R x R = R^3 — three-dimensional space

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 What is a relation
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 An n-ary relation on sets A_1, ..., A_n is a subset:

   R sub A_1 x A_2 x ... x A_n

 A tuple (a_1, ..., a_n) in R means "the elements are connected by R."

 +-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Arity     | Examples                                                    |
 +-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------+
 |           |                                                             |
 | Unary     | R sub A  — simply a subset (property)                       |
 | (n = 1)   | "even" sub Z = {x in Z | x divisible by 2}                 |
 |           |                                                             |
 | Binary    | R sub A x B  — connection between two elements              |
 | (n = 2)   | "less than" sub R x R = {(x,y) | x &lt; y}                |
 |           | "divides" sub N x N = {(a,b) | a | b}                      |
 |           |                                                             |
 | Ternary   | R sub A x B x C  — connection between three elements        |
 | (n = 3)   | "between" sub R x R x R = {(x,y,z) | x &lt; y &lt; z}      |
 |           | "sum" = {(a,b,c) | a + b = c}                               |
 |           |                                                             |
 | n-ary     | A table in a database with n columns.                       |
 |           | Each row is a tuple; the table is a relation                |
 |           |                                                             |
 +-----------+-------------------------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Key binary relations
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Below is a table of binary relations from which all of mathematics is built.

+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
| Relation         | Properties           | Examples / Visualization         |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 1. membership    | Basic relation       | * 3 in N                         |
|    x in A        | Not reflexive        | * pi in R                        |
|                  | (x not-in x usually) | * {1} in P(N)                    |
| "element of      |                      |                                  |
|  a set"          | Foundation of set    |    A                             |
|                  | theory               |   +-----+                        |
|                  |                      |   |  *  | <-- x in A             |
|                  |                      |   +-----+                        |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 2. inclusion     | * Reflexive:         | * N sub Z sub Q sub R sub C      |
|    A sub B       |   A sub A            | * {1,2} sub {1,2,3}             |
|                  | * Transitive:        | * empty sub A for any A          |
| "subset"         |   AsB, BsC => AsC    |                                  |
|                  | * Antisymmetric:     |   +---------+                    |
|                  |   AsB, BsA => A=B    |   |    B    |                    |
|                  |                      |   |  +---+  |                    |
|                  | Partial order on P(X)|   |  | A |  |                    |
|                  |                      |   |  +---+  |                    |
|                  |                      |   +---------+                    |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 3. equivalence   | * Reflexive:         | * Equality of numbers (=)        |
|    x ~ y         |   x ~ x             | * Similarity of triangles        |
|                  | * Symmetric:         | * Congruence modulo n:           |
| "indistinguish-  |   x~y => y~x        |   a = b (mod n)                  |
|  able w.r.t.     | * Transitive:        | * Isomorphism of structures      |
|  a property"     |   x~y, y~z => x~z   |                                  |
|                  |                      | Partitions X into classes:       |
|                  | Gives factorization: |  +---+---+---+                   |
|                  | X/~ (quotient set)   |  |[x]|[y]|[z]|                   |
|                  |                      |  +---+---+---+                   |
|                  |                      |  equivalence classes             |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 4. total order   | * Reflexive:         | * <= on R                        |
|    x <= y        |   x <= x             | * Lexicographic on strings       |
|                  | * Antisymmetric:     | * Chronological (time)           |
| "no greater      |   x<=y, y<=x => x=y |                                  |
|  than"           | * Transitive:        |      a <= b <= c <= d            |
|                  |   x<=y, y<=z => x<=z |      *---*---*---*               |
|                  | * Totality:          |                                  |
|                  |   forall x,y:        |  Any two elements are comparable |
|                  |    x<=y or y<=x      |                                  |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 5. partial order | Like total order,    | * sub on P(X)                    |
|    x <= y        | but without totality | * Divisibility | on N            |
|                  |                      | * Implication => on propositions  |
| "no greater, but | exists x,y:          |                                  |
|  not always      | incomparable         |      +--d--+                     |
|  comparable"     |                      |      |     |                     |
|                  | Hasse diagram:       |      b     c  <-- incomparable   |
|                  |                      |      |     |                     |
|                  |                      |      +--a--+                     |
|                  |                      |                                  |
|                  |                      | Example: {1,2} and {1,3}         |
|                  |                      | are incomparable under sub       |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 6. function      | * Total:             | * f(x) = x^2                     |
|    f: A -> B     |   forall a in A:     | * sin: R -> [-1,1]               |
|                  |     exists b         | * det: M_n -> R                  |
| "rule of         | * Single-valued:     |                                  |
|  correspondence" |   f(a) is uniquely   |   A = {1, 2}    B = {a, b, c}    |
|                  |   determined         |                                  |
|                  |                      |     1 --> a                      |
|                  | Subset of A x B with |     2 --> b                      |
|                  | conditions above     |                                  |
|                  |                      |   To each element of A           |
|                  |                      |   corresponds exactly one        |
|                  |                      |   element of B                   |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 7. injection     | Function with an     | * f(x) = 2x                     |
|    f: A >-> B    | additional property: | * exp: R -> R+                   |
|                  |                      | * embedding N into Z             |
| "one-to-one"     | f(x1) = f(x2)       |                                  |
|                  |   => x1 = x2        |   A = {1, 2}    B = {a, b, c}    |
|                  |                      |                                  |
|                  | Different inputs ->  |     1 --> a                      |
|                  | different outputs    |     2 --> b                      |
|                  |                      |           c  (unused)            |
|                  | Preserves            |                                  |
|                  | distinctions         |   |A| <= |B|, B may have         |
|                  |                      |   "extra" elements               |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 8. surjection    | Function with an     | * f: R -> R, f(x) = x^3         |
|    f: A ->> B    | additional property: | * projection R^2 -> R: (x,y)->x |
|                  |                      |                                  |
| "onto"           | forall b in B:       |   A = {1, 2, 3}   B = {a, b}    |
|                  |  exists a in A       |                                  |
|                  |  such that f(a) = b  |     1 --> a                      |
|                  |                      |     2 --> a  (both to one point) |
|                  | Covers all of B      |     3 --> b                      |
|                  |                      |                                  |
|                  | Every element of B   |   |A| >= |B|, every element of B |
|                  | has a preimage       |   is reached by at least one     |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+
|                  |                      |                                  |
| 9. bijection     | Function that is     | * f: R -> R, f(x) = x           |
|    f: A <-> B    | simultaneously:      | * exp: R -> R+                   |
|                  | * Injection          | * any isomorphism                |
| "one-to-one      | * Surjection         |                                  |
|  correspondence" |                      |   A = {1, 2, 3}   B = {a, b, c} |
|                  | Inverse exists       |                                  |
|                  | f^{-1}: B -> A       |     1 <--> a                     |
|                  |                      |     2 <--> b                     |
|                  | |A| = |B|           |     3 <--> c                     |
|                  | (equinumerous)       |                                  |
|                  |                      |   Perfect correspondence:        |
|                  |                      |   each a to exactly one b,       |
|                  |                      |   and vice versa                 |
|                  |                      |                                  |
+------------------+----------------------+----------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Morphism — a function preserving structure
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now that we know what a function is, we can define a morphism.

 Key concept: Morphism


 Function (= Map) — a rule f: A -> B assigning to each element a in A
 a unique element f(a) in B.

 Morphism — a function between structures that preserves structure.

 Isomorphism — an invertible morphism (bijection preserving structure).
               Means "the structures are the same up to renaming elements."

+-----------------+------------------------+------------------------------+
| Structure       | Morphism is called     | What it preserves            |
+-----------------+------------------------+------------------------------+
| Sets            | Function               | (nothing — base case)        |
| Groups          | Homomorphism           | Operation: phi(ab)=phi(a)phi(b)|
| Rings           | Ring homomorphism      | Both operations: +, x        |
| Vector spaces   | Linear map             | Linearity: T(au+bv)=aTu+bTv |
| Topologies      | Continuous map         | Open sets                    |
| Manifolds       | Smooth map             | Smoothness                   |
| Partial orders  | Monotone map           | Order: a<=b => f(a)<=f(b)    |
+-----------------+------------------------+------------------------------+

 Formula != Function (a common mistake)


 Formula: a way to compute, e.g. f(x) = x^2
 Function: a triple (domain A, codomain B, rule of correspondence)

 Why these are different things:

 1. One function — many formulas:
    f(x) = x^2 = (x+1)^2 - 2x - 1 = x*x = ...  (different writings of one)

 2. One formula — different functions (depends on domain):
    +-----------+---------+-----------+-----------------------------+
    | Formula   | Domain  | Codomain  | Properties                  |
    +-----------+---------+-----------+-----------------------------+
    | f(x) = x^2| R      | R         | not injective: f(2)=f(-2)  |
    | g(x) = x^2| R+     | R+        | injective and surjective    |
    | h(z) = z^2| C      | C         | entirely different object   |
    +-----------+---------+-----------+-----------------------------+

 3. Functions without formulas:
    D(x) = 1 if x in Q, else 0  (Dirichlet function — no "formula.")

 Conclusion: Function = triple (A, B, f: A -> B).
             A formula is merely one way to specify the rule.


 Image and preimage of a set

 For a function f: A -> B and subsets S sub A, T sub B:

 +-----------------+---------------------------------------+
 | Concept         | Definition                            |
 +-----------------+---------------------------------------+
 | Image of a set  | f(S) = {f(x) : x in S}               |
 | f(S)            | = all values of f on elements of S    |
 +-----------------+---------------------------------------+
 | Preimage of     | f^{-1}(T) = {x in A : f(x) in T}     |
 | a set f^{-1}(T) | = all points that f sends into T     |
 +-----------------+---------------------------------------+

 Important: f^{-1}(T) is not the inverse function.
    The preimage is always defined, even if f is not invertible.
    The inverse function f^{-1}: B -> A exists only for bijections.

 Examples:
   f(x) = x^2, f: R -> R
   f({1, 2, 3}) = {1, 4, 9}                 — image of a set
   f^{-1}({4}) = {-2, 2}                    — preimage of a point
   f^{-1}({y : y &gt; 0}) = R \ {0}           — preimage of an interval
   f^{-1}({-1}) = empty                     — preimage can be empty

 Properties:
   * f(S1 cup S2) = f(S1) cup f(S2)            — image of union
   * f^{-1}(T1 cup T2) = f^{-1}(T1) cup f^{-1}(T2)  — preimage of union
   * f^{-1}(T1 cap T2) = f^{-1}(T1) cap f^{-1}(T2)  — preimage of intersection
   * f(S1 cap S2) sub f(S1) cap f(S2)          — for image only sub!

 Application: Topology uses preimages to define continuity:
   f is continuous iff the preimage of every open set is open


Key patterns:

Checking properties of a relation R:
  1. Reflexivity: xRx for all x?
  2. Symmetry: xRy => yRx?
  3. Transitivity: xRy, yRz => xRz?
  4. Antisymmetry: xRy, yRx => x=y?

Combinations determine the type:
  * Reflexivity + Symmetry + Transitivity = Equivalence (partition into classes)
  * Reflexivity + Antisymmetry + Transitivity = Partial order
  * + Totality = Linear order

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Equivalence relations and equivalence classes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: Equivalence = "identical from some viewpoint"

 Equality (=) is too strict a requirement. Often what matters is not full
 identity but "sameness" by some criterion:

   * Two people are "equivalent" by age (both are 25)
   * Two triangles are "equivalent" by shape (similar)
   * Two fractions are "equivalent" by value (1/2 = 2/4 = 3/6)
   * Two numbers are "equivalent" by remainder on division by 3

 Formally: a relation ~ is called an equivalence if it is:

   1. Reflexive:  a ~ a      (each is equivalent to itself)
   2. Symmetric:  a ~ b => b ~ a  (order does not matter)
   3. Transitive: a ~ b, b ~ c => a ~ c  (equivalence "propagates")

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Equivalence class = all who are "the same" as a given element
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 If ~ is an equivalence on a set X, then the equivalence class
 of element a is the set of all elements equivalent to a:

   [a] = {x in X | x ~ a}

 Key fact: Equivalence classes partition the set X:

   * Every element x in X lies in exactly one class
   * Distinct classes do not intersect
   * The union of all classes = X

 Visualization:

   Set X:                        After partitioning X/~:
   +-----------------------+     +-----------------------+
   | * *   o o o    # #    |     |  [*]   [o]   [#]      |
   |   *   o        # # # |  -> |                       |
   |       o o          # |     | 3 classes             |
   +-----------------------+     +-----------------------+
   (elements of same "type"      (each class is one element
    marked identically)           of the quotient set)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Concrete examples of equivalence classes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Example 1: Remainders (modular arithmetic)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set: Z (all integers)
   Relation: a ~ b iff a - b is divisible by 3  (written: a = b (mod 3))

   Equivalence classes:
     [0] = {..., -6, -3, 0, 3, 6, 9, ...}   — numbers with remainder 0
     [1] = {..., -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, 10, ...}  — numbers with remainder 1
     [2] = {..., -4, -1, 2, 5, 8, 11, ...}  — numbers with remainder 2

   Quotient set: Z/3Z = {[0], [1], [2]} — just 3 classes.
   (This is the ring of residues — the basis of cryptography)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Example 2: Fractions (rational numbers)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set: Z x Z* = {(a, b) | a,b in Z, b != 0} — pairs "numerator/denominator"
   Relation: (a,b) ~ (c,d) iff a*d = b*c  (fractions are equal)

   Equivalence classes:
     [(1,2)] = {(1,2), (2,4), (3,6), (-1,-2), ...}  — all forms of
     [(1,3)] = {(1,3), (2,6), (-1,-3), ...}          "one half"

   A rational number is an equivalence class, not a specific fraction.
   Q = (Z x Z*)/~ — the set of equivalence classes of pairs.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Example 3: Similarity of triangles
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set: all triangles in the plane
   Relation: T1 ~ T2 iff angles of T1 equal angles of T2

   Equivalence class: all triangles of the same "shape" (different sizes)

         +-+              +---+
        /   \            /     \
       / 60  \          /  60   \
      /       \        /         \          All three are in one
     / 60  60  \      /  60  60   \         equivalence class
    +-----------+    +-------------+        (equilateral)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Example 4: Real numbers (Cauchy construction)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set: all Cauchy sequences of rationals
   Relation: {a_n} ~ {b_n} iff |a_n - b_n| -> 0

   A real number is an equivalence class of sequences.
   Example: sqrt(2) = [{1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, 1.4142, ...}]

   (Infinitely many sequences converge to sqrt(2) — they are all
    equivalent and form one class, which we call the number sqrt(2))

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Example 5: Homotopy classes of paths (topology)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set: all paths from A to B in space X
   Relation: gamma_1 ~ gamma_2 iff gamma_1 can be continuously deformed
             into gamma_2

   The fundamental group pi_1(X) = equivalence classes of closed paths.
   This construction is the foundation of algebraic topology.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Quotient set X/~ — "gluing" equivalent elements together
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The quotient set X/~ is the set of equivalence classes:

   X/~ = {[a] | a in X}

 Intuition: We "glue" all equivalent elements into a single point.

 Projection: The canonical map pi: X -> X/~, where pi(a) = [a].
   It "forgets" differences between equivalent elements.

 Main theorem (Universal property):

   Any function f: X -> Y that is constant on equivalence classes
   (i.e. a ~ b => f(a) = f(b)) factors uniquely through the quotient:

           X ------f-------> Y
           |                 /
         pi|            f-bar  (exists uniquely)
           v            /
          X/~ ---------/

 Where this is used:
   * Z_n = Z/nZ          (modular arithmetic, cryptography)
   * Q = (Z x Z*)/~      (construction of rationals)
   * R = Cauchy/~         (construction of reals)
   * C = R[x]/(x^2+1)    (construction of complex numbers)
   * Projective space P^n = (R^{n+1} \ 0)/~
   * Tori, Klein bottles, and other topological objects

 Intuition: from clocks to complex numbers

   Z/12Z is clock arithmetic. We "wind" integers around the dial:
   13 o'clock = 1 o'clock, because 13 = 1 (mod 12). We declared 12 = 0.

   R[x]/(x^2+1) is the same, but with polynomials. We "wind"
   polynomials by declaring x^2+1 = 0, i.e. x^2 = -1.
   The result: x is a "new number" whose square equals -1.
   This is the imaginary unit i. Complex numbers a + bi are
   polynomials of degree <= 1 with arithmetic modulo (x^2+1).


 Why does the engineer need equivalence classes?

 1. Modular arithmetic (Z_n):
    Hash functions, checksums, cryptography — all work
    with residue classes.

 2. Finite automata:
    Minimizing an automaton = merging "equivalent" states
    (those indistinguishable by input data).

 3. Dimensional analysis:
    Physical quantities with the same dimension form one class.
    "Meters per second" is a class of all ways to express speed.

 4. Coordinates:
    A tensor is an equivalence class of arrays of numbers (coordinates),
    where two arrays are equivalent if related by the transformation law.

 5. Clustering:
    Partitioning data into clusters is essentially building equivalence
    classes by a "similarity" criterion.


Philosophy:

  Mathematics studies not objects themselves but relations between them.
  A function f: A -> B matters more than A and B individually.
  Modern (categorical) mathematics is the "mathematics of arrows."
===============================================================================
Maps — how structures connect
===============================================================================

Note: This section is a survey of types of maps. The terms "group",
"topology", "manifold" are defined in Part II.
Here they are used to illustrate the general pattern.

In the "Relations" section we defined function and morphism. Here are detailed examples.

"A good morphism preserves structure"

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Preliminary remark on categories
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 All examples below are special cases of one idea: morphism.

   * Objects (sets, groups, spaces, ...)
   * Arrows between them (functions, homomorphisms, continuous maps, ...)
   * Composition of arrows (apply one, then another)

 This is a category — a language unifying all of mathematics.
 For now it suffices to know: the different types of maps in the table below
 are morphisms in different categories.

   Category of sets:          morphisms = functions
   Category of groups:        morphisms = homomorphisms
   Category of topol. spaces: morphisms = continuous maps
   Category of vector spaces: morphisms = linear operators


+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
| Transformation       | Structure                | Examples                 |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 1. function          | Set -> Set               | * f(x) = x + 1          |
|    f: A -> B         |                          | * projection (x,y) -> x |
|                      | Preserves nothing        | * any map                |
| Basic map            | (no extra structure)     |                          |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 2. homomorphism      | Group -> Group           | * exp: (R,+) -> (R+,x)  |
|    phi: G -> H       |                          |   exp(a+b)=exp(a)exp(b) |
|                      | phi(g1 * g2) =           | * det: (GL(n),x)->(R*,x)|
| Preserves operation  |   = phi(g1) . phi(g2)    |   det(AB) = det(A)det(B)|
|                      |                          |   (GL(n) = invertible   |
|                      |                          |    n x n matrices)      |
|                      |                          |                          |
| Automorphism if      | Isomorphism if bijection | * complex conjugation    |
| G = H                | (structures "the same")  |   z -> z-bar on C       |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 3. linear operator   | Vec.space -> Vec.space   | * Rotation in R^2        |
|    T: V -> W         |                          | * Projection onto subsp.|
|                      | T(av + bw) =             | * Differentiation        |
| Preserves linear     |   = aT(v) + bT(w)       |   D: C^1 -> C^0          |
| structure            |                          |   D(af + bg) =           |
|                      | Matrix in a basis        |   = aD(f) + bD(g)        |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 4. continuous map    | Top.space -> Top.space   | * f: R -> R continuous   |
|    f: X -> Y         |                          | * Rubber deformation     |
|                      | Preimage of open is open:| * Any f: M -> N (smooth) |
| Preserves "closeness"| U in tau_Y =>            |                          |
|                      |   f^{-1}(U) in tau_X     | Homeomorphism if         |
|                      |                          | bijection + f^{-1} cont. |
|                      |                          | (topologically "same")   |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 5. diffeomorphism    | Manifold -> Manifold     | * Any smooth invertible  |
|    f: M -> N         |                          |   map                    |
|                      | f and f^{-1} smooth      | * R iso (0,1) via        |
| Smooth + invertible  |                          |   f(x) = x/(1+|x|)      |
|                      | Preserves smooth         |                          |
|                      | structure                | Shows geometric          |
|                      |                          | equivalence              |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 6. derivative        | Function -> Function     | * D(x^2) = 2x           |
|    d/dx              |                          | * D(sin) = cos           |
|                      | Linear operator:         | * D(e^x) = e^x          |
| Instantaneous rate   | D(af + bg) = aD(f)+bD(g) |                          |
| of change            |                          | Geometry: tangent line   |
|                      | Local approximation      | to the graph             |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 7. integral          | Function -> Number       | * int_0^1 x^2 dx = 1/3  |
|    int_a^b f(x)dx    |                          | * int sin x dx=-cos x+C |
|                      | Linear functional:       |                          |
| Accumulation         | int(af+bg)= a*int f +    | Geometry: area under     |
| (area, mass)         |           + b*int g      | the graph                |
|                      |                          |                          |
|                      | Inverse of derivative    |                          |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 8. tangent space     | Manifold -> Vec.space    | * T_p S^2 — plane        |
|    T_p M             |                          |   tangent to sphere at p |
|                      | Curved -> Flat           | * T_p M is where velocity|
| "Plane at a point"   |                          |   vectors "live"         |
|                      | Functor: M -> T_p M      |                          |
|                      |                          | Linearization of geometry|
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 9. fundamental group | Topology -> Algebra      | * pi_1(S^1) = Z          |
|    pi_1: Top -> Grp  |                          |   (circle -> integers)   |
|                      | Space -> Group           | * pi_1(S^2) = {e}        |
| Functor              |                          |   (sphere is trivial)    |
|                      | Loops with homotopies    | * pi_1(T^2) = Z x Z     |
| Geometry -> Algebra  |                          |   (torus -> two holes)   |
|                      | Turns "holes" into       |                          |
|                      | algebraic structure      | Computing topology       |
|                      |                          | through groups           |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+
|                      |                          |                          |
| 10. Fourier          | Function -> Function     | * cos(wt) <-> delta(w-w0)|
|     transform        |                          | * Convolution <-> Mult.  |
|   F: L^2 -> L^2     | F(f)(w) =               |                          |
|                      |  int f(t)e^{-iwt} dt    | Applications:            |
| Functor              |                          | * Signal processing      |
| Time <-> Frequency   | Unitary operator         | * Quantum mechanics      |
|                      |                          | * Differential equations |
|                      | Turns diff. eqs. into    |                          |
|                      | algebraic ones           |                          |
|                      |                          |                          |
+----------------------+--------------------------+--------------------------+

General pattern:

  A good transformation preserves what matters:
    * Homomorphism preserves the operation
    * Continuous map preserves closeness
    * Linear operator preserves linearity
    * Isometry preserves distance

  Functor — transformation between categories (in detail):
    Maps objects to objects and arrows to arrows, preserving composition.
    Example: pi_1 maps topological spaces to groups,
    and continuous maps to homomorphisms.

Why this matters:

  1. Connection between fields: pi_1 turns topology into algebra
  2. Simplification: Fourier turns diff. eqs. into algebraic ones
  3. Understanding: tangent space makes geometry flat
  4. Computation: isomorphism lets us work in a convenient structure
===============================================================================
Numbers — how all numbers arise from the void
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Construction of natural numbers
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Construction of natural numbers from the empty set (von Neumann)

All of mathematics arises from a single primitive: the empty set

+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
| Number| Definition            | Visualization                      |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
|       |                       |                                    |
| 0     | empty                 | { }                                |
|       | The empty set         | Nothing here                       |
|       |                       |                                    |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
|       |                       |                                    |
| 1     | {empty}               | { { } }                            |
|       | Set containing the    | A box with an empty box            |
|       | empty set             |                                    |
|       |                       |                                    |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
|       |                       |                                    |
| 2     | {empty, {empty}}      | { { }, { { } } }                   |
|       | = {0, 1}              | A box with: (empty) and (a box     |
|       |                       | with empty)                        |
|       |                       |                                    |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
|       |                       |                                    |
| 3     | {empty, {empty},      | { { }, { { } }, { { }, { { } } } } |
|       |  {empty, {empty}}}    |                                    |
|       | = {0, 1, 2}           | A box with 0, 1, and 2             |
|       |                       |                                    |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+
|       |                       |                                    |
| n     | {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}   | The set of all preceding numbers   |
|       |                       |                                    |
|       | n = {k : k &lt; n}    | n contains within itself all       |
|       |                       | numbers less than itself           |
|       |                       |                                    |
+-------+-----------------------+------------------------------------+

Recursive definition:

  0 := empty
  n+1 := n cup {n}   (next number = previous + set containing previous)

Deeper meaning:

  * From nothing (empty) everything arises
  * Each number contains all previous ones: 3 sup 2 sup 1 sup 0
  * A number = "memory" of all preceding steps
  * Construction of numbers = successive drawing of boundaries in the void

From naturals to the rest:

  N = {0, 1, 2, 3, ...}     (defined above)
        |
  Z = N x N / ~             (pairs (a,b) as "a - b", with equivalence)
        |
  Q = Z x Z* / ~            (pairs (p,q) as "p/q", q!=0)
        |
  R = {Dedekind cuts}       (or Cauchy sequences in Q)
        |
  C = R x R                 (pairs (a,b) as "a + bi")

Everything is built from empty by successive acts of drawing boundaries.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Dedekind cuts — creating irrational numbers from rationals
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: Q has "holes"
   The equation x^2 = 2 has no solution in Q (proof by contradiction).
   But on the number line there should be a point sqrt(2).

 Dedekind's idea (1858): define a number through a cut of the line

 Definition:
   A cut (A, B) of Q is a partition of Q into two nonempty classes:

   * A — the "lower class": all rationals to the left of the cut
   * B — the "upper class": all rationals to the right of the cut
   * A cup B = Q,  A cap B = empty
   * Every element of A is less than every element of B
   * A has no greatest element (the key condition)

 Visualization:

   The rational number line Q:
   <--*--*--*--*--*--|--*--*--*--*--*-->
         A          X         B
                 (the cut)

   Cut for sqrt(2):
   A = {q in Q : q &lt; 0  or  q^2 &lt; 2}     (everything "left of sqrt(2)")
   B = {q in Q : q &gt; 0  and  q^2 >= 2}    (everything "right of sqrt(2)")

   A has no greatest element. For any q in A with q^2 &lt; 2, there exists
   a rational r &gt; q with r^2 &lt; 2.

 Definition of real number:
   R := {all cuts (A, B) of Q}

 Two types of cuts:
   1. B has a least element q in Q -> the cut defines the rational q
   2. B has no least element -> the cut defines an irrational number

 Arithmetic of cuts:
   (A1, B1) + (A2, B2) := (A1 + A2, ...) where A1 + A2 = {a1 + a2}
   Multiplication, ordering, etc. are defined analogously.

 Deeper meaning:
   * An irrational number is not an object but a boundary between objects
   * sqrt(2) is not a specific fraction but the location of a cut
   * Once again, the act of drawing a boundary creates a new entity

 Alternative — Cauchy sequences:
   R can also be defined as equivalence classes of fundamental
   sequences in Q (sequences where |a_n - a_m| -> 0 as n, m -> inf)

   Example: 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, 1.4142, ... -> sqrt(2)

   Both approaches yield the same set R.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Cauchy sequences — convergence without knowing the limit
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: How can we tell that a sequence converges
          if we do not know what it converges to?

 Cauchy's idea: A sequence converges if its terms become
                arbitrarily close to each other.

 Definition:
   A sequence {a_n} is called a Cauchy sequence
   (or fundamental) if:

   forall eps &gt; 0  exists N in N:  forall n,m &gt; N  =>  |a_n - a_m| &lt; eps

   In words: from some index N onward, the distance between
   any two terms is less than any prescribed eps.

 Visualization:

   Ordinary sequence:
   "All terms are close to the limit a"

         a1    a2  a3 a4a5
    --*---*----*--****------------->
                   ^
                   a (limit)

   Cauchy sequence:
   "All terms are close to each other"

         a1      a2    a3  a4 a5 a6 a7.
    --*-------*-----*---*--*******-------->
                        +--+
                         &lt;eps
                    "clumping together"

 Examples:

   ok Cauchy: a_n = 1/n
     |a_n - a_m| = |1/n - 1/m| <= 1/n + 1/m -> 0
     Terms: 1, 0.5, 0.33, 0.25, 0.2, ...  (clumping around 0)

   ok Cauchy: a_n = 1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + ... + 1/2^n
     Partial sums of geom. series: 1, 1.5, 1.75, 1.875, ...
     Converges to 2, terms ever closer to each other

   no Not Cauchy: a_n = n
     |a_{n+1} - a_n| = 1 — does not tend to 0, terms drift apart

   no Not Cauchy: a_n = (-1)^n
     |a_{n+1} - a_n| = 2 — terms jump between -1 and 1

 Theorem (Cauchy's criterion):
 +-------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                       |
 | In R (real numbers):                                  |
 |                                                       |
 | A sequence converges <=> it is a Cauchy sequence      |
 |                                                       |
 +-------------------------------------------------------+

 Important: In Q this is not the case.

   The sequence 1, 1.4, 1.41, 1.414, 1.4142, ... is Cauchy in Q,
   but does not converge in Q, because sqrt(2) not-in Q.

   This is called the incompleteness of Q.

 Completeness of R:
   R is called complete because in it every Cauchy sequence
   has a limit. This is the key property distinguishing R from Q.

 Cauchy construction of R:
   R = {equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences in Q}

   Two sequences {a_n} ~ {b_n} are equivalent if |a_n - b_n| -> 0

   Example: (1, 1.4, 1.41, ...) ~ (1.5, 1.42, 1.415, ...) — both "represent" sqrt(2)

 Deeper meaning:
   Cauchy's criterion allows us to speak about convergence
   intrinsically — through the terms of the sequence themselves,
   without invoking the notion of "limit" from outside.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Cardinalities — different sizes of infinity
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Two sets have the same cardinality if there is a bijection between them.
 For finite sets this is obvious: |{a,b,c}| = |{1,2,3}| = 3.

 For infinite sets? Cantor showed: there are different infinities.

 Countable sets (cardinality aleph_0):
   A set is countable if there exists a bijection with N.
   That is, the elements can be "enumerated": a_1, a_2, a_3, ...

   * N is countable (trivially)
   * Z is countable. Bijection: 0, 1, -1, 2, -2, 3, -3, ...
   * Q is countable. (surprising — "more" numbers, same cardinality)
   * N x N is countable (diagonal traversal)

 Uncountable sets (cardinality &gt; aleph_0):
   * R is uncountable
   * (0,1) is uncountable (bijection with R via tan)
   * P(N) is uncountable (set of all subsets of N)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Cantor's diagonal argument — why R is uncountable
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Theorem: R (or even the interval [0,1]) is uncountable.

 Proof (by contradiction):

 Suppose [0,1] is countable. Then all numbers can be enumerated:

   x_1 = 0. a_11 a_12 a_13 a_14 ...
   x_2 = 0. a_21 a_22 a_23 a_24 ...
   x_3 = 0. a_31 a_32 a_33 a_34 ...
   x_4 = 0. a_41 a_42 a_43 a_44 ...
   ...        \     \     \     \
                  diagonal

 Construct y = 0. b_1 b_2 b_3 b_4 ..., where:

   b_n != a_nn  (differs from the n-th number in the n-th digit)

 Then y != x_1 (differs in the 1st digit)
      y != x_2 (differs in the 2nd digit)
      y != x_n for any n!

 But y in [0,1], so y should be in the list. Contradiction.

 Conclusion: [0,1] cannot be enumerated. R is uncountable.

 Deeper meaning:
   The diagonal argument is a universal method. It shows:
   P(A) always has greater cardinality than A (Cantor's theorem).
   Infinities form a hierarchy: aleph_0 &lt; 2^aleph_0 &lt; 2^(2^aleph_0) &lt; ...

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 The continuum hypothesis — a question without an answer
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The cardinality of R is called the continuum and denoted c or 2^aleph_0.

 Cantor's question (1878):
   Is there a set of cardinality between aleph_0 and c?
   That is: can there be an infinity "midway" between N and R?

 Continuum hypothesis (CH): No, there is not. That is, c = aleph_1.

 The shocking result of the 20th century:

   * Goedel (1940): CH is consistent with ZFC (cannot be disproved)
   * Cohen (1963):  not-CH is also consistent with ZFC (cannot be proved)

   => CH is independent of the ZFC axioms.

 This is not a matter of "we don't know yet." It is fundamental: within ZFC
 the question has no answer. One can work in mathematics with or without CH.

 Philosophical lesson:
   There are meaningful mathematical questions that the axioms do not settle.
   Mathematics is not a single "true" system but a family of possible ones.
===============================================================================
Number extensions — what is missing?
===============================================================================

 Main idea:

 Each number system arises as an answer to the question:
 "Which equation has no solution?"

 Extend the numbers -> get the solution -> lose some property

 Terminology (strict definitions):
   * Field = set with +, x, where everything except 0 is invertible (Q, R, C)
   * Ring = field without mandatory division (Z)
   * Division algebra = "almost a field," possibly non-commutative (H, O)


 Hierarchy of number systems
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
| Number| Equation    | Solution| What we lose         | What we gain     |
|       | without sol.|         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| N     | x + 3 = 1   | none inN| --                   | Counting         |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| Z     | 2x = 3      | none inZ| "always >= 0"        | Subtraction      |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| Q     | x^2 = 2     | none inQ| Discreteness         | Division         |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| R     | x^2 = -1    | none inR| Countability         | Continuity       |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| C     | all solved  | --      | Order (&lt;, &gt;)   | Algebraic        |
|       |             |         |                      | closedness       |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| H     | (special)   | --      | Commutativity        | 4D rotations     |
|       |             |         | ab != ba             |                  |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
| O     | (special)   | --      | Associativity        | 8D, exceptional  |
|       |             |         | (ab)c != a(bc)       | structures       |
|       |             |         |                      |                  |
+-------+-------------+---------+----------------------+------------------+

 Visualization


   N sub Z sub Q sub R sub C
   |   |   |   |   |
   |   |   |   |   +-- Plane (2D): a + bi
   |   |   |   +------ Line (1D): continuous
   |   |   +---------- Line (1D): with holes (sqrt(2) absent)
   |   +-------------- Line (1D): integer points
   +------------------ Ray: 0, 1, 2, 3, ...

   H (quaternions): 4D, basis {1, i, j, k},  i^2 = j^2 = k^2 = ijk = -1
   O (octonions):   8D, the last division algebra over R

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Frobenius theorem (1877) and Hurwitz theorem (1898)
