-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Frobenius: Associative division algebras over R:  R, C, H — and nothing else.
 Hurwitz:   Normed division algebras over R:  R, C, H, O — and nothing else.

 Dimensions: 1, 2, 4, 8 — no more.

 With each extension we lose a fundamental property:
   R -> C:  lose order
   C -> H:  lose commutativity
   H -> O:  lose associativity
   O -> S:  lose the ability to divide (zero divisors appear)

 Sedenions S (16D) and beyond exist mathematically, but are useless
 for physics: in them ab = 0 is possible with a != 0 and b != 0.
 Therefore the chain R -> C -> H -> O is all there is.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Fundamental theorem of algebra
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Every polynomial with complex coefficients has a root in C.

 This means: C is closed under solving equations.
 There is no need to extend further "for the sake of equations."

 That is why C is called an "algebraically closed field."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Numbers as points in spaces — topological distinctions
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Hierarchy of embeddings:

   N sub Z sub Q sub R sub C

   Each successive set "fills the holes" of the previous one:
   N -> Z : added negatives (solution of a + x = 0)
   Z -> Q : added fractions (solution of ax = b)
   Q -> R : filled "holes" (limits of sequences)
   R -> C : added sqrt(-1) (roots of all polynomials)


 Geometric picture:

   Z — lattice on the line          C — the plane
   (isolated points)                (R is the horizontal axis)

   --*--*--*--*--*--*--               Im
     -2 -1  0  1  2  3                |
                                      |        * 2+i
                                  ----+----*---------> Re
                                      |     1
                                      * -i
                                      |

   Q — "dust" on the line           R — a solid line
   (dense everywhere, but full      (no holes, complete)
    of holes)

   -.-.-.-.-.-.-.-.-.-.-             ====================
       sqrt(2) is a hole              sqrt(2) is here

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Topological properties
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+
 | Space   | Topological properties                                  |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+
 | N, Z    | Discrete topology: each point is an open set            |
 |         | Not compact, not connected (each point is a component)  |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+
 | Q       | Dense everywhere in R, but "full of holes"              |
 |         | Not complete (sequence -> sqrt(2) has no limit in Q)    |
 |         | Not connected (Q = (-inf,sqrt2)capQ cup (sqrt2,+inf)capQ)|
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+
 | R       | Complete, connected, locally compact                    |
 |         | The unique complete ordered field.                      |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+
 | C       | Complete, connected, locally compact                    |
 |         | Algebraically closed (every polynomial has a root)      |
 |         | No ordering compatible with the operations.             |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------+

 Key takeaway:
   N sub Z sub Q sub R sub C  is not merely "adding numbers."
   At each step the topology and algebraic structure of the space change.
===============================================================================
Dictionary of mathematical jargon
===============================================================================

Mathematicians use words that are rarely explained in textbooks.
Here is a dictionary of the most common "invisible" terms.

 Canonical


 = "natural," "unique," "requiring no arbitrary choice"

 Example:
   * V** iso V — canonical isomorphism (there is a natural correspondence)
   * V* iso V — not canonical (requires choosing a basis; the result depends
                on the choice)

 When someone says "canonical," it means there is one right way,
 and no additional choices need to be made.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Well-defined
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 = "the result does not depend on the choice of representative"

 Problem: Sometimes the same object can be written in different ways.
          A function is well-defined if it gives the same answer
          for all representations.

 Example 1 (not well-defined):
   Let f: Z/3Z -> Z, f([x]) = remainder of x on division by 2.
   Check: f([0]) = 0, f([3]) = 1.
   But [0] = [3] in Z/3Z. So f([0]) should = f([3]).
   Contradiction: 0 != 1. The function f is not well-defined.

 Example 2 (well-defined):
   Let g: Z/6Z -> Z/2Z, g([x]) = [x mod 2].
   Check: if [x] = [y] in Z/6Z, then x = y (mod 6).
   Hence x = y (mod 2), and [x mod 2] = [y mod 2].
   The function g is well-defined. ok

 Conclusion: When working with equivalence classes, one must always verify
             that the definition is well-defined.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Without loss of generality (WLOG)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 = "it suffices to consider a special case; the rest reduce to it"

 Example 1:
   Theorem: |a + b| <= |a| + |b|
   Proof: "WLOG assume a >= 0."
   Why is this allowed? If a &lt; 0, replace a with -a (the absolute value
   does not change), returning to the case a >= 0.

 Example 2:
   Prove something for two points A and B.
   "WLOG assume A is to the left of B."
   Why? We can relabel: A <-> B.

 When it is allowed:
   * There is symmetry in the hypotheses (a and b play equal roles)
   * We can reduce to the needed case by substitution
   * The operation (relabeling, sign change) does not alter the problem

 When it is not allowed:
   * The conditions on a and b are different
   * The transformation changes the structure of the problem

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Trivial / non-trivial
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Trivial = "obvious," "empty," "the simplest possible"
 Non-trivial = "substantive," "not reducible to the obvious"

 Examples:
   * Trivial solution of Ax = 0: x = 0
   * Trivial subgroup: {e} or the whole G
   * Non-trivial root: x != 0

 Irony: What is "trivial" for a professor may be hard for a student.
        "Trivially follows." often means "I do not want to explain this."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why the symbol for composition
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The notation (f . g)(x) = f(g(x)) means: "apply g first, then f."

 Why right to left:
   From the expression f(g(x)) — we first compute g(x), then f of the result.
   The inner function is applied first.

 Origin of the symbol:
   . is a small circle, a "linking element" between functions.
   Introduced in the 19th century for brevity.

 Alternative notation (rare):
   f ; g = g . f — "first f, then g" (read left to right)
   Used in some programming languages and category theory.

 Mnemonic: f . g reads as "f after g" or "f circle g."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Unique up to
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 = "unique if we do not distinguish objects of a certain kind"

 Examples:
   * "The basis is unique up to ordering" — one may permute the vectors
   * "The solution is unique up to sign" — there are exactly two: x and -x
   * "The group is unique up to isomorphism" — all such groups are
     isomorphic to each other

 Meaning: There are several objects, but they are "the same" in a specific sense.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Common reasoning errors
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Error 1: Confusing implication A=>B with equivalence A<=>B


   A => B  does not mean  B => A

   True:  "If n is divisible by 4, then n is divisible by 2"
   False: "If n is divisible by 2, then n is divisible by 4"
          Counterexample: n = 6 is divisible by 2, but 6 is not divisible by 4.

   Correct relationships:
     A => B   (direct implication)
     B => A   (converse) — a different statement
     ~B => ~A (contrapositive) — equivalent to the direct


 Error 2: Circular proof


   Wrong: "Prove A. Assume A. Then ... Therefore A. QED"

   This is not a proof. You used A to prove A.

   A subtler version:
   "From A follows B. From B follows C. From C follows A."
   This proves A <=> B <=> C, but does not prove that A is true.


 Error 3: Confusing quantifier order forall-x exists-y and exists-y forall-x


   forall x exists y: P(x,y) — for each x there is its own y (y depends on x)
   exists y forall x: P(x,y) — there is a single y that works for all x

   Example: Continuity vs uniform continuity

   forall eps forall x exists delta: ...  — delta may depend on x and eps
                                            (ordinary continuity)
   forall eps exists delta forall x: ...  — one delta for all x
                                            (uniform continuity)

   These are different properties. f(x) = 1/x is continuous on (0,1),
   but not uniformly.


 Error 4: Dividing by an expression that may be zero


   Wrong: "From ax = ay, divide by a to get x = y"

   What if a = 0? Then 0 = 0 holds for any x, y.

   Correct: "If a != 0, then ax = ay implies x = y."
            "If a = 0, then the equality holds for any x, y."


 Error 5: One example is not a proof


   Wrong: "41 is prime, 41+2=43 is also prime.
           Therefore, if p is prime, then p+2 is also prime."

   Counterexample: 7 is prime, but 7+2 = 9 = 3^2 is not.

   Asymmetry:
     * To prove forall x P(x), one must check all x
     * To disprove forall x P(x), one counterexample suffices


 Error 6: Conflating necessary and sufficient


   A is a sufficient condition for B:  A => B  (if A, then certainly B)
   A is a necessary condition for B:   B => A  (without A, B cannot hold)

   Example: "Being a perfect square" for "being divisible by 4"

   Sufficient? n = k^2 => n divisible by 4?  No. (9 = 3^2, but 9 not | 4)
   Necessary?  n divisible by 4 => n is a square? No. (8 | 4, but 8 != k^2)


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Why symbols instead of words
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Question: Why do mathematicians write forall eps>0 exists delta>0
           instead of words?


 Reason 1: Compactness

   In words:
   "For every positive number epsilon there exists a positive number delta
    such that for all x, if the absolute value of x minus a is less than
    delta, then the absolute value of f(x) minus L is less than epsilon."

   In symbols:
   forall eps>0 exists delta>0 forall x: |x-a|<delta => |f(x)-L|<eps

   One line instead of four.


 Reason 2: Precision

   The order of quantifiers is immediately visible:

   forall eps exists delta — for each eps its own delta (ordinary continuity)
   exists delta forall eps — one delta for all eps (a different property)

   In words this is easy to mix up.


 Reason 3: Manipulation

   Logical rules apply mechanically:

   ~(forall x P(x)) = exists x ~P(x)  — just flip the quantifier and negate P
   ~(exists x P(x)) = forall x ~P(x)

   In words such transformations are easy to muddle.


 Advice for reading:
   On first reading, translate symbols into words aloud.
   "For all epsilon greater than zero there exists delta greater than zero."

 Advice for writing:
   Use symbols for structural precision.
   Add verbal commentary for intuition.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Why terms are named the way they are (etymology)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Understanding the origin of terms helps remember their meaning.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Algebraic terms
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Kernel (from German Kern = seed, core):
   Historical metaphor: the "core" of what is lost under a map.
   The kernel is what "collapses" to the neutral element.
   ker(phi) = {x : phi(x) = 0} — the central part of the "losses."

 Image (from Latin imago = reflection):
   Literally: "picture," "reflection" of the original set.
   Im(f) = f(A) — where the set A "maps to."

 Homomorphism (homos = same, morphe = form):
   "Preserving form" — carries operations without changing structure.
   phi(a * b) = phi(a) * phi(b) — the operation "looks the same" before and after.

 Isomorphism (isos = equal):
   "Equal-formed" — structures are completely identical algebraically.
   A bijective homomorphism: nothing is lost, nothing is glued.

 Group (Fr. groupe, Ger. Gruppe):
   Introduced by Galois (1830s) for the "group of substitutions of roots
   of an equation." Originally: a collection of symmetries associated
   with an algebraic equation.

 Ring (Ger. Ring):
   Originally: cyclic structures like Z/nZ that "loop around."
   Dedekind (1871) used it for "rings of integers" in fields.

 Field (Ger. Koerper = body, Eng. field):
   German "body" = a space for full-fledged arithmetic.
   English "field" = a domain of activity for all operations.
   A field is where one can divide by anything nonzero.

 Ideal (from Kummer, 1840s):
   Originally "ideal numbers" — fictitious elements to restore
   the uniqueness of prime factorization.
   Dedekind formalized them as subsets of rings.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Geometric and topological terms
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Topology (topos = place, logos = study):
   "Science of places/positions" — studies what is near what.
   Introduced by Listing (1847): "Topology is the geometry of position."

 Manifold (from Ger. Mannigfaltigkeit):
   "Manifold" — can look different in different places,
   but locally always like R^n. Riemann (1854).

 Homotopy (homos = same, topos = place):
   Two paths are "same-placed" = one can be continuously deformed
   into the other.

 Simplex (Lat. simplex = simple):
   The simplest polyhedron in a given dimension:
   point -> segment -> triangle -> tetrahedron -> ...

 Compact (Lat. compactus = tightly packed):
   A set "tightly packed" — from any cover one can extract
   a finite subcover. No "infinite holes" or "escape to infinity."

 Connected:
   "Whole" — cannot be split into two disjoint open sets.
   One can walk from any point to any other without leaving the set.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Analytic terms
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Continuous (from Lat. continuus = unbroken):
   "Without breaks" — small changes in the argument yield small changes
   in the value.

 Differentiable (from Lat. differentia = difference):
   One can compute the "difference quotient" and pass to the limit.
   The function is "discernible" — one can see how it changes.

 Integral (from Lat. integer = whole):
   "Restoring the whole" from parts (summing infinitesimals).
   Leibniz used the integral sign as a stylized letter S (summa).

 Convergence (from Lat. convergere = to incline toward):
   The terms of a sequence "incline toward" a single point.

 Limit (from Lat. limes = boundary):
   The "boundary" toward which a sequence or function tends.
===============================================================================
The categorical viewpoint — a language for everything that follows
===============================================================================

Before moving on to specific structures (groups, spaces, manifolds),
let us introduce the language in which all of them are described uniformly.

 Main idea:
 Mathematical objects matter not in themselves, but through the relations
 between them.

 Category = Objects + Arrows (morphisms) between them

      A --f--> B --g--> C

 Objects: what we study (sets, groups, spaces)
 Arrows: how objects are related (functions, homomorphisms, continuous maps)

 Key property: arrows can be composed (g.f: A -> C)


 Examples of categories (which we will encounter later)
 +-----------+--------------+-------------------------+
 | Category  | Objects      | Arrows                  |
 +-----------+--------------+-------------------------+
 | Set       | Sets         | Functions               |
 | Grp       | Groups       | Homomorphisms           |
 | Vect      | Vector spaces| Linear maps             |
 | Top       | Top. spaces  | Continuous maps         |
 | Man       | Manifolds    | Smooth maps             |
 +-----------+--------------+-------------------------+

 In all these cases the pattern is the same:
   * There are objects with some structure
   * There are maps preserving that structure
   * Maps can be composed


===============================================================================
How a mathematician thinks — heuristics and modes of thought
===============================================================================

We have defined the basic objects: sets, numbers, relations, categories.
Before building spaces on them (Part II) — a few words about how
mathematical thinking itself works. What is an invariant?
When to generalize? When to look for a counterexample? These heuristics
permeate the entire atlas.



 When to look for an invariant


 An invariant is a quantity that does not change under transformations.
 If we rotate, deform, recompute in different coordinates —
 and some number or property remains the same — that is an invariant.
 Invariants separate the essential from the inessential.

 If objects change but something is preserved — look for an invariant.

 Examples:
   * Rotations change coordinates but preserve length: ||v|| = inv
   * Deformations change shape but preserve the number of holes: pi_1 = inv
   * Time changes a system but preserves energy: H = inv (if dL/dt = 0)

 Heuristic: "What has not changed?" is the mathematician's first question.


 When to generalize, when to specialize


 Generalize if:
   * The proof works for a broader class
   * The specific details are not used
   * You want to understand the essence, discarding the "noise" of particulars

 Specialize if:
   * The general theorem does not give an explicit answer
   * A numerical result is needed
   * The special case has additional structure

 Example: Fixed-point theorem (general) -> Newton's method (specific).
 The general says "exists"; the specific says "how to find."


 What to do when a proof is stuck


 1. Check special cases
    Is the statement true for n=1,2,3? For the simplest examples?
    If not — look for a counterexample, not a proof.

 2. Weaken the statement
    Perhaps it holds under additional conditions?
    Perhaps a weaker estimate is true?

 3. Strengthen the statement
    Paradoxically, a stronger statement is sometimes easier to prove.
    Induction often requires strengthening the hypothesis.

 4. Reformulate
    The same problem in a different language (algebra <-> geometry <-> analysis).
    Sometimes a different viewpoint makes the solution obvious.

 5. Study analogous theorems
    How were similar results proved? What ideas were used?


 How to choose between formalizations


 One problem — many languages (see introduction). How to choose?

 Criteria:
   * Which operations are needed? (addition -> lin.alg, closeness -> topology)
   * What kind of answer is needed? (existence -> abstract, a number -> concrete)
   * What is known? (symmetry -> groups, smoothness -> analysis)

 Heuristic: Choose the language where the problem becomes standard.

 Example: The heat equation
   * Want qualitative behavior -> semigroups (e^{At})
   * Want a specific solution -> Fourier or numerics
   * Want to prove existence -> functional analysis


 The principle of structural economy


 Do not introduce more structure than needed for the solution.

 Bad:  "Let V be a Hilbert space." (if only a norm is needed)
 Good: "Let V be a normed space."

 Why this matters:
   * The proof works for a broader class
   * It is easier to see what is actually used
   * The result is easier to apply in other contexts

 Exception: If additional structure makes the proof simpler,
 sometimes it is worth using it and generalizing the result afterward.


 Intuition vs rigor


 A mathematician works in two stages:

   Stage 1 (intuition): "Why should this be true?"
   Drawings, analogies, physical reasoning, examples.
   Goal: to understand, not to prove.

   Stage 2 (rigor): "How to prove it?"
   Formal definitions, logical steps, checking all cases.
   Goal: to convince (oneself and others).

 Beginner's mistake: skipping stage 1.
   Without intuition, a proof is a blind search.

 Physicist's mistake: stopping at stage 1.
   Intuition sometimes deceives (example: paradoxes of 19th-century analysis).

                              PART II: SPACES
▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄▄

          Introduction: Different views on a single space

===============================================================================
Three tasks on spaces
===============================================================================

 What can one do with mathematical objects?


 Any work with a space (and with any mathematical object in general)
 reduces to one of three tasks:

   Classification — what kind of object is this? what is possible in principle?
   Computation    — find a specific value within a known object
   Construction   — create a new object from existing ones

 These three tasks exhaust everything one can do in mathematics.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Classification — normative boundaries
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Classification answers: What is this? What is possible in principle?

 Result: "this is X, not Y", "this is possible / impossible", "there are exactly N"

 Examples:
   * Closed surfaces are exactly these: spheres with handles + non-orientable
   * A rigid body has 6 degrees of freedom (not 5, not 7)
   * A 5th-degree equation is not solvable by radicals (S_5 is not solvable)
   * F = grad phi exists iff curl F = 0 and the domain is simply connected
   * The sphere and the torus are not homeomorphic (different pi_1, different chi)

 Classification establishes boundaries — what to search for and what is pointless.

 Tools: topological invariants, symmetry groups, existence/uniqueness theorems.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Computation — finding specifics
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Computation answers: Where exactly? How much? What value?

 Result: a number, coordinates, a formula, a concrete object

 Examples:
   * Find the roots of x^3 - 2x + 1 = 0
   * Compute the integral int_0^inf e^{-x^2} dx = sqrt(pi)/2
   * Find the eigenvalues of a matrix
   * Determine the shortest path on a surface (a geodesic)
   * Find the minimum of a function on a compact set

 Computation works within the boundaries established by classification.
 If classification says "does not exist" — there is nothing to compute.

 Tools: algorithms, optimization methods, numerical methods, analytical techniques.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Construction — creating something new
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Construction answers: How to obtain a new object from existing ones?

 Result: a new object that did not exist before

 Examples:
   * a x b — a new vector from two given ones
   * V tensor W — a new space from two given ones
   * G/H — a quotient group (a new group from a group and a subgroup)
   * Q -> R — completion (a new space from an old one)
   * Product M x N — a new manifold
   * Tangent bundle TM — a new space over M
   * Auxiliary constructions in geometry (draw a line, drop a perpendicular)

 Difference from computation: computation finds something existing (the root
 is already there, we search for it). Construction creates — before the
 operation a x b, that vector did not exist.

 Tools: operations (x, tensor, wedge, /, x), constructions (completion,
 covering, extension), universal properties.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connection of the three tasks
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

                      Classification
                      "what is possible?"
                           |
              +------------+------------+
              v                         v
        Construction              Computation
      "create new"              "find within"
              |                         |
              +----------+--------------+
                         v
                   New objects
                         |
                         v
              Classification of new ones

 Classification establishes boundaries ->
   Construction creates objects within boundaries ->
     Computation finds specific values ->
       Results may require new classification


 Classification (what is possible, invariants, types):
   Groups — classification of symmetries and motions
   Topology — classification of spaces by shape
   Number theory — classification of numbers

 Computation (find a value, solve an equation):
   Linear algebra — systems of equations, eigenvalues
   Analysis — derivatives, integrals, series
   Functional analysis — equations in infinite dimensions

 Construction (create a new object):
   Products of vectors — <,>, x, wedge, tensor
   Duality — V -> V*
   Tensors — multilinear constructions
   Manifolds — gluing from charts
   Differential forms — forms from vectors

 Most sections involve all three tasks in different proportions.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Table of spaces — the central table
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Space is the central object of mathematics

 All of mathematics studies spaces and structures on them:
   * Topology: the shape of a space (holes, connectedness)
   * Algebra: symmetries of a space (groups)
   * Analysis: functions on a space
   * Geometry: measurements on a space (metric, curvature)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hierarchy of spaces — what is added at each level
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set (just a collection of points)
       | + topology (notion of "closeness," open sets)
       v
   Topological space
       | + local Euclideanness (looks like R^n in the small)
       v
   Manifold
       | + metric (a way to measure distances)
       v
   Riemannian manifold
       | + physical equations
       v
   Spacetime (GR)

   Parallel branch:

   Set
       | + linear structure (addition, scalar multiplication)
       v
   Vector space
       | + inner product
       v
   Euclidean space R^n
       | + infinite dimension
       v
   Hilbert space (quantum mechanics)

===============================================================================
Main spaces — catalog
===============================================================================

 Notation in the table:

   dim = dimension (how many coordinates are needed to describe a point)
   pi_1 = fundamental group (which loops cannot be contracted to a point?)
          0 means "all loops contract," Z means "there is one essential loop"
   H_1  = first homology group (similar to pi_1, but the abelian version)
   chi  = Euler characteristic = V - E + F (vertices - edges + faces)
          Shape invariant: sphere chi=2, torus chi=0, projective plane chi=1

   Invariants — table

+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
| Space       |dim  | pi_1  | H_1   | chi | What it is / where it appears       |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Point {*}   |  0  |   0   |   0   |  1  | Trivial; zero-dimensional "world"   |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Line R      |  1  |   0   |   0   |  -- | Non-compact; time, temperature      |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Circle S^1  |  1  |   Z   |   Z   |  0  | S^1 = {|z|=1}; angles, phases,     |
|             |     |       |       |     | periods. Group U(1).               |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Plane R^2   |  2  |   0   |   0   |  -- | Non-compact; ordinary geometry      |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Sphere S^2  |  2  |   0   |   0   |  2  | Surface of a ball; Earth, sky       |
|             |     |       |       |     | All loops contract.                |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Torus T^2   |  2  |  Z^2  |  Z^2  |  0  | Doughnut = S^1 x S^1; two angles   |
|             |     |       |       |     | Periodic boundary conditions       |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Projective  |  2  |  Z/2  |  Z/2  |  1  | RP^2 = "directions of lines"       |
| plane       |     |       |       |     | Non-orientable.                    |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| Klein       |  2  | Z><Z  |Z+Z/2  |  0  | Non-orientable; needs 4D           |
| bottle K    |     |       |       |     |                                     |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| 3-sphere S^3|  3  |   0   |   0   |  0  | iso SU(2). Space of rotations      |
|             |     |       |       |     | (up to +/-)                        |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| SO(3)       |  3  |  Z/2  |  Z/2  |  -- | All rotations in R^3               |
|             |     |       |       |     | iso RP^3 (not S^3)                 |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
| R^4 (space- |  4  |   0   |   0   |  -- | Spacetime (SR)                     |
| time, SR)   |     |       |       |     | Flat, Minkowski metric             |
|             |     |       |       |     |                                     |
+-------------+-----+-------+-------+-----+-------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Classification of closed surfaces (dim = 2)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Theorem: Every closed surface is one of:

   Orientable:         Sphere with g handles (genus g)

     g = 0     g = 1     g = 2
       O         @        @@       chi = 2 - 2g
     sphere    torus    "pretzel"

   Non-orientable:     Sphere with k "crosscaps" (Moebius band glued in)

     k = 1     k = 2
      RP^2      K        chi = 2 - k
   projective  Klein

 This is the complete classification. No other closed surfaces exist.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connection of spaces with groups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Many important spaces are simultaneously groups:

   S^1 iso U(1) iso SO(2)    — circle = group of plane rotations
   S^3 iso SU(2)             — 3-sphere = group (double cover of SO(3))
   SO(3) iso RP^3            — 3D rotations = projective space
   GL(n), SL(n), O(n)        — matrix groups = manifolds

 These are Lie groups — groups that are simultaneously manifolds.

 The fundamental group pi_1 also connects:
   pi_1(S^1) = Z             — integers as a group
   pi_1(T^2) = Z^2           — lattice
   pi_1(wedge_n S^1) = F_n   — free group


===============================================================================
Three attitudes toward a space
===============================================================================

 Task: identify the subject of each branch of mathematics

+------------------+-------------+---------------------------+
| Branch           | Verb        | Question                  |
+------------------+-------------+---------------------------+
| Differential     | Measures    | How is it built inside?   |
| geometry         |             | (curvature, metric,angles)|
+------------------+-------------+---------------------------+
| Algebraic        | Distinguishes| How does it differ from  |
| topology         |             | others? (invariants:pi_1,H_n)|
+------------------+-------------+---------------------------+
| Group theory     | Transforms  | What can be done with it? |
|                  |             | (symmetries, actions)     |
+------------------+-------------+---------------------------+

The word "algebraic":

+--------------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
| Algebraic topology       | Space -> Group.  Algebra as a tool.             |
| Algebraic geometry       | Equations -> Space. Algebra as a source.        |
+--------------------------+-------------------------------------------------+


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Groups — symmetries of a space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If a set is dust and topology is fabric, then a group is mobility.
A group answers the question: how can one move through a space without
breaking it? Which transformations are admissible?

In "object-observer" terms, a group is the key concept.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A group is a catalog of observer motions that leave the object unchanged
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

If the observer rotates, the object looks different, but the object itself
has not changed. The group SO(3) is all possible rotations. Each group element
is a specific rotation. Composition means performing one rotation, then another.

Why this is fundamental: physical laws must not depend on how the observer
is oriented. "Covariance" in physics is the requirement that equations look
the same for all observers related by the group.

A group invariant is something that does not change under any motion from
the group. For example, distance is an invariant of the rotation group.
Two observers, rotated relative to each other, will measure the same distance.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A group as a viewpoint on a space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A group is a set of transformations of a space that preserve its structure.
 Different groups "see" different things in the same space:

   * SO(3) sees rotations in R^3 (preserves distances and orientation)
   * GL(n) sees linearity (preserves lines and the origin)
   * Crystal symmetries see a discrete lattice

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Where groups came from — history
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Equations of degrees 1, 2, 3, 4 are solvable by formulas (Cardano, Ferrari).
 Equation of degree 5: x^5 + ax^4 + bx^3 + cx^2 + dx + e = 0

   For years a formula was sought. Galois (at age 20) proved: none exists.

 How? He linked the equation to the permutation group of its roots:
   If this group is "solvable" -> a formula exists.
   The permutation group of 5 elements is not solvable -> no formula.

 Thus group theory was born — from the question of solving equations.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Physical viewpoint: degrees of freedom of a rigid body
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Take any object — a book, a stone, a molecule.
 What motions are possible if deformation is forbidden?

 Translations: can be moved to any point in space.
               Three directions: forward-back, left-right, up-down -> 3 numbers.

 Rotations: can be rotated about any axis.
            Axis (2 parameters) + angle (1 parameter) -> 3 numbers.

 Total: 6 parameters. The position of a rigid body is described by 6 numbers.
        Not 5, not 7 — exactly 6. This is a fact about the structure of R^3.

 The set of all such motions is called SE(3) — the special Euclidean group.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why this is a group — properties of motions
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Motions possess specific properties:

 1. Two motions in succession are also a motion
    First translate, then rotate — the result is some motion.
    We do not leave SE(3).

 2. The order of grouping does not matter
    (A then B) then C = A then (B then C)
    This is a property of composition of any maps.

 3. There is "do nothing"
    The identity motion — leave everything in place.

 4. Any motion can be undone
    Shifted 3 meters right -> shift 3 meters left.
    Rotated by 30 degrees -> rotate by -30 degrees.

 These four properties are the group axioms.
 They are not invented but follow from the nature of "motion."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Different constraints — different groups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Which motions are "allowed" depends on what needs to be preserved:

 +-------------------+------------+---------------------------------+
 | What we preserve  | Group      | Where it appears                |
 +-------------------+------------+---------------------------------+
 | Distances & angles| E(3)       | Rigid body (with reflections)   |
 | + orientation     | SE(3)      | Rigid body (no reflections)     |
 | Only angles       | Conformal  | Cartography, complex analysis   |
 | Parallelism       | Aff(3)     | Shadows in sunlight             |
 | Only straightness | PGL(3)     | Perspective in painting         |
 | Volume            | SL(3)      | Incompressible fluid            |
 | Linearity         | GL(3)      | Any linear deformation          |
 +-------------------+------------+---------------------------------+

 Fewer constraints -> larger group:
   Linear:  SO(3) sub O(3) sub SL(3) sub GL(3)
   Affine:  SE(3) sub E(3) sub Aff(3)
   Link: GL(3) sub Aff(3), but E(3) not-sub GL(3) (isometries include shifts)

 This is classification: a group describes which transformations are possible.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Rotations only — the group SO(3)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 If the object is fixed at one point (a spinning top, a gyroscope, a satellite),
 only rotations remain. This is the group SO(3).

   SO(3) = { rotations of R^3 about the origin }
         = { orthogonal 3x3 matrices with det = +1 }

 Dimension: 3 (three Euler angles, or axis + angle).

 Important fact: SO(3) is a non-abelian group.

   Rotate about X, then about Y  !=  rotate about Y, then about X

   You can verify with a book:
   1) lay the book flat, rotate 90 degrees about the vertical axis, then
      90 degrees about the horizontal axis "away from you"
   2) do it in reverse order
   The results differ.

 This is not an abstraction — satellite control must account for this.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Discrete symmetries — finite groups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Not all objects admit arbitrary rotations.
 A salt crystal (cube) looks the same only under specific rotations.

 Symmetries of a square — the group D_4:

    1---2     Original         2---1    Rotation by 90 degrees
    |   |     square           |   |
    |   |                      |   |
    4---3                      3---4

 Possible transformations:
   * 4 rotations: by 0, 90, 180, 270 degrees
   * 4 reflections: about horizontal, vertical, two diagonals

 Total: 8 elements. Not 7, not 9 — exactly 8.
 This is the complete answer to "what are the symmetries of a square."

 Symmetries of regular polyhedra
 +----------------------+--------+----------------------+
 | Polyhedron           | Group  | Number of symmetries |
 +----------------------+--------+----------------------+
 | Tetrahedron          | Td     | 24                   |
 | Cube / Octahedron    | Oh     | 48 (they are dual)   |
 | Dodecahedron / Icosahedron | Ih | 120                |
 +----------------------+--------+----------------------+

 The cube and octahedron share the same symmetry group — they are
 "geometrically equivalent" in the sense of symmetries (dual polyhedra).

===============================================================================
Groups — motivation and examples
===============================================================================

 Visualization: symmetries of a square


    1---2     Original         2---1    Rotation 90°     4---3   Rotation 180°
    |   |     square           |   |    counterclockwise |   |
    |   |                      |   |                     |   |
    4---3                      3---4                     1---2

    1---4     Reflection       2---3    Reflection       Total: 8 symmetries:
    |   |     (vert. axis)     |   |    (horiz. axis)    * 4 rotations
    |   |                      |   |                     * 4 reflections
    2---3                      1---4

   Any two symmetries can be combined -> the result is a symmetry.
   Every symmetry has an inverse (undo it).
   There is the identity symmetry (do nothing).

   This is the group D_4 — the dihedral group (symmetries of the square).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 A group as a set of transformations
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A group formalizes the concept of "a set of invertible transformations."

 +------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
 | Axiom            | Meaning in terms of transformations             |
 +------------------+-------------------------------------------------+
 | Closure          | Composition of two transformations is a transf. |
 | Associativity    | (f.g).h = f.(g.h)                              |
 | Identity element | The identity transformation id                  |
 | Inverse element  | Every transformation is invertible              |
 +------------------+-------------------------------------------------+

 Definition: The symmetry group of an object X is the set of all bijections
             X -> X that preserve the structure of X.


 Necessity of the axioms
+-----------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Axiom           | Why it is necessary                               |
+-----------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Composition     | Sequential application of symmetries is a symmetry|
| Identity        | The identity map preserves structure               |
| Inverse         | The inverse of a symmetry is a symmetry           |
| Associativity   | Follows from associativity of function composition|
+-----------------+---------------------------------------------------+

===============================================================================
Formal definition
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Definition of a group
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A group (G, *) is a set G with a binary operation * : G x G -> G
 satisfying the axioms:

 Note on notation: The expression (G, *) is a tuple (ordered pair),
 where the first element is the carrier set, the second is the operation
 (signature). This is not the same as the pair {{G}, {G, *}} from set theory.
 Here the parentheses mean "structure = carrier + operations."

   +-----------------+-------------------+-----------------------+
   | Axiom           | Formula           | Meaning               |
   +-----------------+-------------------+-----------------------+
   |                 |                   |                       |
   | G1. Closure     | forall a,b in G:  | The result is in G    |
   |                 |   a*b in G        |                       |
   |                 |                   |                       |
   | G2. Associa-    | forall a,b,c in G:| Parentheses do not    |
   | tivity          | (a*b)*c = a*(b*c) | matter                |
   |                 |                   |                       |
   | G3. Identity    | exists e in G:    | "Do nothing"          |
   | element         | forall a in G:    |                       |
   |                 | e*a = a*e = a     |                       |
   |                 |                   |                       |
   | G4. Inverse     | forall a in G     | Everything can be     |
   | element         | exists a^{-1}:    | undone                |
   |                 | a*a^{-1} =        |                       |
   |                 | a^{-1}*a = e      |                       |
   |                 |                   |                       |
   +-----------------+-------------------+-----------------------+

 Notes:
   * The identity element is unique
   * The inverse of each a is unique
   * (a^{-1})^{-1} = a
   * (a*b)^{-1} = b^{-1}*a^{-1}  (order reverses)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Abelian vs non-abelian groups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Abelian group: forall a,b: a*b = b*a (commutativity)

+----------------------------+-------------------------------------------+
| Abelian (order irrelevant) | Non-abelian (order matters)               |
+----------------------------+-------------------------------------------+
|                            |                                           |
| (Z, +): 2+3 = 3+2         | D_4: rotation.reflection != reflection.rot|
| (R, +): pi+e = e+pi       | S_n (n>=3): permutations                  |
| (R*, x): 2x3 = 3x2        | GL(n): matrices AB != BA                  |
| (Z/n, +): cyclic           | SO(3): rotations in 3D                    |
|                            |                                           |
+----------------------------+-------------------------------------------+

===============================================================================
Examples of groups — in detail
===============================================================================

 Example 1: Integers (Z, +)


 Set: Z = {..., -2, -1, 0, 1, 2, 3, ...}
 Operation: addition +

 Checking axioms:

   G1. Closure:       a + b in Z for any a, b in Z                ok
                      (sum of integers is an integer)

   G2. Associativity: (a+b)+c = a+(b+c)                           ok
                      (2+3)+4 = 5+4 = 9
                      2+(3+4) = 2+7 = 9

   G3. Identity:      e = 0, because a + 0 = 0 + a = a            ok

   G4. Inverse:       a^{-1} = -a, because a + (-a) = 0           ok

 Additionally: a + b = b + a  ->  Abelian group

 Visualization: Shifts along the number line

     <----------*---------->
     ... -2  -1  0  1  2  3 ...

     +3 = "shift right by 3"
     -2 = "shift left by 2"
      0 = "stay in place"


 Example 2: Cyclic group Z/n (or Z_n)


 Set: Z/n = {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1}
 Operation: addition modulo n

 Example: Z/4 = {0, 1, 2, 3} — "clock arithmetic" with 4 divisions

     Cayley table (multiplication table of the group):

       +  |  0    1    2    3
     -----+--------------------
       0  |  0    1    2    3       0 is the identity element
       1  |  1    2    3    0       (the row and column for 0
       2  |  2    3    0    1        match the headers)
       3  |  3    0    1    2

   Check: 2 + 3 = 5 mod 4 = 1  ok (see table)
          3 + 3 = 6 mod 4 = 2  ok

 Visualization:                  Identity: e = 0
                                 Inverses:
        0                          0^{-1} = 0  (0+0=0)
     3     1                       1^{-1} = 3  (1+3=4=0)
        2                          2^{-1} = 2  (2+2=4=0)
                                   3^{-1} = 1  (3+1=4=0)
   Shifts around a circle of 4 points

 Generator: An element g whose powers yield the entire group.
            In Z/4: g = 1, because 1, 1+1=2, 1+1+1=3, 1+1+1+1=0
            Also g = 3 works: 3, 3+3=2, 3+3+3=1, 3+3+3+3=0
            But g = 2 does not: 2, 2+2=0, 2+2+2=2, ... (not all elements)


 Example 3: Symmetries of an equilateral triangle D_3


 Symmetries of the triangle — all ways to place it "the same way":

         1
        /\              Rotations:  e  = no rotation
       /  \                         r  = by 120 deg counterclockwise
      /    \                        r^2 = by 240 deg (= r.r)
     /______\
    3        2          Reflections: s_1 = about the altitude from 1
                                     s_2 = about the altitude from 2
                                     s_3 = about the altitude from 3

 Total: 6 elements: D_3 = {e, r, r^2, s_1, s_2, s_3}

     Cayley table (composition: first column, then row):

       .   |  e    r    r^2  s_1  s_2  s_3
     ------+------------------------------
       e   |  e    r    r^2  s_1  s_2  s_3
       r   |  r    r^2  e    s_3  s_1  s_2
       r^2 |  r^2  e    r    s_2  s_3  s_1
       s_1 |  s_1  s_2  s_3  e    r    r^2
       s_2 |  s_2  s_3  s_1  r^2  e    r
       s_3 |  s_3  s_1  s_2  r    r^2  e

 Observations:
   * r . s_1 = s_3,  but  s_1 . r = s_2  ->  non-abelian
   * Rotation subgroup {e, r, r^2} iso Z/3 — abelian
   * Every reflection: s_i^2 = e (apply twice = nothing)

 Physical meaning: D_3 describes the symmetry of a molecule with triangular
                   structure (e.g. BF_3 — boron trifluoride)


 Example 4: Permutation groups S_n


 S_n = all permutations of n elements
 |S_n| = n! elements

 Example: S_3 — all permutations of {1, 2, 3}

   Notation: sigma = (sigma(1), sigma(2), sigma(3))

     e    = (1, 2, 3)  — identity permutation
     s_1  = (1, 3, 2)  — swaps 2 and 3
     s_2  = (3, 2, 1)  — swaps 1 and 3
     s_3  = (2, 1, 3)  — swaps 1 and 2
     s_4  = (2, 3, 1)  — cyclic shift 1->2->3->1
     s_5  = (3, 1, 2)  — cyclic shift 1->3->2->1

 Composition: (sigma . tau)(x) = sigma(tau(x))  — first tau, then sigma

 Cycle notation (more compact):
   s_3 = (1 2) — transposition (swaps 1<->2, rest fixed)
   s_4 = (1 2 3) — 3-cycle (1->2, 2->3, 3->1)

 Fact: S_3 iso D_3 (permutation group of 3 elements is isomorphic to
       symmetries of the triangle)

 Critically important:
   S_5 is a non-solvable group (its normal series does not reach {e}
   through abelian quotients). By the Abel-Ruffini theorem, this means
   the 5th-degree equation is not solvable by radicals.


 Example 5: Nonzero reals (R*, x)


 Set: R* = R \ {0} = all reals except zero
 Operation: multiplication x

 Checking axioms:

   G1. Closure:       a x b in R* for a, b != 0                   ok
                      (product of nonzero numbers is nonzero)

   G2. Associativity: (axb)xc = ax(bxc)                           ok

   G3. Identity:      e = 1, because a x 1 = 1 x a = a            ok

   G4. Inverse:       a^{-1} = 1/a, because a x (1/a) = 1         ok
                      (this is why 0 is excluded — it has no inverse)

 Why (R, x) is not a group:
   0 x (anything) = 0, but 0 x ? = 1 has no solution.
   Zero has no inverse element.


 What is not a group — counterexamples

+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+
| Structure       | Why not a group       | How to fix         |
+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+
|                 |                       |                    |
| (N, +)          | No inverses:          | -> (Z, +) add     |
| naturals        | 3 + ? = 0 unsolvable  | negatives          |
|                 |                       |                    |
+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+
|                 |                       |                    |
| (Z, x)          | Inverses not integers:| -> (Q*, x) switch  |
| integers        | 2^{-1} = 1/2 not in Z| to rationals       |
|                 |                       |                    |
+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+
|                 |                       |                    |
| (R, x)          | 0 has no inverse      | -> (R*, x) remove 0|
| reals           | 0 x ? = 1 unsolvable  |                    |
|                 |                       |                    |
+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+
|                 |                       |                    |
| n x n matrices  | det=0 -> no inverse   | -> GL(n) only      |
| with x          |                       | invertible (det!=0)|
|                 |                       |                    |
+-----------------+-----------------------+--------------------+

===============================================================================
Subgroups
===============================================================================

 Definition:
 H <= G is called a subgroup of G (notation <=, not sub), if H is a group
 with respect to the same operation (inherited from G).

 Notation H <= G is standard for subgroups; H sub G is for subsets.

 Subgroup criterion (convenient for verification):

   +-----------------------------------------+
   | H <= G  iff  H != empty  and            |
   |              forall a,b in H: a*b^{-1} in H |
   +-----------------------------------------+

 (One condition instead of four axioms)

 Why it works:
   * H != empty => exists a in H => a*a^{-1} = e in H  (identity exists)
   * e in H => e*b^{-1} = b^{-1} in H  (inverses exist)
   * a, b^{-1} in H => a*(b^{-1})^{-1} = a*b in H  (closure)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Examples of subgroups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Group               Subgroup                      Notation
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   (Z, +)              Even numbers 2Z               2Z < Z
                       Multiples of 3: 3Z            3Z < Z
                       Multiples of n: nZ             nZ < Z

   (R*, x)             Positive reals R+              R+ < R*
                       {1, -1}                       {+/-1} < R*

   D_4 (symmetries)    Rotations {e, r, r^2, r^3}    iso Z/4
                       {e, r^2}                      iso Z/2
                       {e, s} for any reflection      iso Z/2

   GL(n) (invertible   SL(n) = {A : det A = 1}       special linear group
   matrices)           O(n) = {A : A^T A = I}         orthogonal group
                       SO(n) = O(n) cap SL(n)         special orthogonal
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Trivial subgroups:
   {e} — trivial subgroup (exists in every group)
   G — the group itself (improper subgroup)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Lagrange's theorem
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   +---------------------------------------------------------------------+
   |  If G is a finite group and H is a subgroup of G, then:             |
   |                                                                     |
   |              |H| divides |G|                                        |
   |                                                                     |
   |  Moreover: |G| = |H| x [G : H], where [G : H] is the index        |
   +---------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Consequences:

   * The order of an element divides the order of the group
     (order of element a = smallest n such that a^n = e)

   * A group of prime order p is cyclic
     (no subgroups other than {e} and G)

   * In S_4 (24 elements), subgroups can have order
     1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24 (divisors of 24)
     Subgroups of order 5 or 7 are impossible


===============================================================================
Homomorphisms and isomorphisms
===============================================================================

 Homomorphism — a map preserving structure


 A map phi: G -> H is called a group homomorphism if:

   +---------------------------------------------+
   | forall a, b in G:  phi(a * b) = phi(a) . phi(b) |
   |                                             |
   | "Image of product = product of images"      |
   +---------------------------------------------+

 Properties (follow automatically):
   * phi(e_G) = e_H            (image of identity is identity)
   * phi(a^{-1}) = phi(a)^{-1} (image of inverse is inverse)

 Examples of homomorphisms:
 +-------------------------+-------------------------------------+
 | Homomorphism            | Check: phi(a*b) = phi(a)*phi(b)     |
 +-------------------------+-------------------------------------+
 | exp: (R,+) -> (R+,x)   | exp(a+b) = exp(a) x exp(b) ok      |
 | det: (GL(n),x) -> (R*,x)| det(AB) = det(A) x det(B) ok      |
 | sign: (S_n,.) -> ({+/-1},x)| sign(s.t) = sign(s) x sign(t) ok|
 | mod n: (Z,+) -> (Z/n,+)| (a+b) mod n = (a mod n)+(b mod n) ok|
 +-------------------------+-------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Kernel and image
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 For a homomorphism phi: G -> H:

 +---------+------------------------+------------------------+
 | Concept | Definition             | Properties             |
 +---------+------------------------+------------------------+
 | Ker(phi)| {a in G : phi(a) = e_H}| Normal subgroup of G   |
 | (kernel)| What maps to identity  | phi injective iff      |
 |         |                        |   Ker = {e}            |
 +---------+------------------------+------------------------+
 | Im(phi) | {phi(a) : a in G} sub H| Subgroup of H          |
 | (image) | Where G lands          | phi surjective iff     |
 |         |                        |   Im = H               |
 +---------+------------------------+------------------------+

 Example: phi: Z -> Z/6,  phi(k) = k mod 6
   Ker(phi) = 6Z = {..., -12, -6, 0, 6, 12, ...}
   Im(phi)  = Z/6 = {0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5}

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 * First isomorphism theorem — a fundamental result
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Theorem: For any homomorphism phi: G -> H:

                        G / Ker(phi)  iso  Im(phi)

 Geometric intuition:

        G                           H
   +-------------+             +-------------+
   |  o o o o    |     phi     |             |
   |  | | | |    | --------->  |   *   *     |   Im(phi) — where things land
   |  o o o o    |             |   |   |     |
   |  | | | |    |             |   *   *     |
   |  o o o o    |             |             |
   +-------------+             +-------------+
       |
   Glue elements with
   the same image
       |
   +---------+
   |  @   @  |  G/Ker(phi)  — cosets
   |  @   @  |  (elements mapping to same point are glued)
   +---------+

 Meaning: "Factoring out the kernel removes everything superfluous
           and leaves only the image"

 The quotient group G/H is the quotient set G/~, where the equivalence
 relation ~ is defined by g_1 ~ g_2 iff g_1 g_2^{-1} in H.
 Key condition: for the group operation on G/~ to be well-defined,
 the subgroup H must be normal (gHg^{-1} = H for all g in G).

 Example: phi: Z -> Z/6,  k |-> k mod 6

   Ker(phi) = 6Z = {..., -6, 0, 6, 12, ...}
   Im(phi)  = Z/6

   Theorem: Z / 6Z iso Z/6  ok
            (quotient by kernel is isomorphic to image)


 Isomorphism — when groups are "the same"


 An isomorphism phi: G -> H is a homomorphism + bijection. We write G iso H.
 "G and H are the same group, differing only in the names of elements"

 +-------------------+--------------------+--------------------------+
 | Isomorphism       | Map                | Why it works             |
 +-------------------+--------------------+--------------------------+
 | (Z, +) iso (2Z,+) | phi(n) = 2n       | Evens <-> all integers   |
 | (R, +) iso (R+, x)| phi=exp, inv=ln   | Addition <-> multiplic.  |
 | Z/6 iso Z/2 x Z/3| k->(k mod2,mod3)  | Chinese remainder thm    |
 | S^1 iso U(1)      | e^{ith} <-> rot.  | Circle iso rotations     |
 |    iso SO(2)      |   by theta         |                          |
 +-------------------+--------------------+--------------------------+

   Z/2 iso {+/-1}                       (two elements, one structure)
        phi(0) = 1,  phi(1) = -1

   S_3 iso D_3                          (6 elements, symmetries of triangle)

 Non-isomorphic:

   Z/4 not-iso Z/2 x Z/2               (different structure)
        In Z/4 there is an element of order 4 (a generator).
        In Z/2 x Z/2 all elements have order <= 2.

===============================================================================
Groups in physics and everyday life
===============================================================================

 Applications of groups
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
| Domain                | Group             | What it describes                |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Crystallography       | 230 space groups  | All crystal symmetries           |
|                       |                   | NaCl: cubic symm. -> optics      |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Standard model        | U(1)              | Electromagnetism (phase)         |
| of particle physics   | SU(2)             | Weak interaction                 |
|                       | SU(3)             | Strong interaction (quarks)      |
|                       | U(1)xSU(2)xSU(3) | The entire Standard Model        |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Theory of relativity  | SO(3,1)           | Lorentz: preserves speed of light|
|                       |                   | 3 spatial + 1 time               |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Quantum mechanics     | SU(2)             | Particle spin                    |
|                       |                   | e-: spin 1/2 -> 720 deg rotation |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Music                 | Z/12              | 12 semitones, transposition      |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Cryptography          | (Z/n)*            | RSA: multiplicative group        |
|                       | Elliptic curves   | Point groups on curves           |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Thermal engineering   | Similarity group  | Dimensional analysis = Lie groups |
| (example for the      | (scaling)         |                                  |
|  engineer)            |                   | Finding Nu = f(Re, Pr)           |
|                       |                   | = choosing a Lie group orbit     |
|                       |                   | by invariant values (Re, Pr)     |
|                       |                   |                                  |
|                       |                   | Physical quantities (alpha,      |
|                       |                   | lambda, v, L) are coordinates    |
|                       |                   | on a manifold symmetric under    |
|                       |                   | the scaling group action         |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+
|                       |                   |                                  |
| Combinatorics         | Any G             | Counting "up to symmetry"        |
|                       |                   | Burnside's lemma                 |
|                       |                   |                                  |
+-----------------------+-------------------+----------------------------------+

 Sylow theorems — structure of finite groups

 Let |G| = p^n * m, where p is prime and gcd(p, m) = 1.

 Sylow subgroup: a subgroup of order p^n (maximal p-power)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Theorem 1: A Sylow subgroup exists
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   For any prime p dividing |G|, a subgroup of order p^n exists.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Theorem 2: All Sylow subgroups are conjugate
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   Any two p-Sylow subgroups P and Q satisfy: Q = gPg^{-1} for some g.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Theorem 3: The number of Sylow subgroups n_p satisfies
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
   * n_p = 1 (mod p)
   * n_p divides m = |G|/p^n

 Application — classification of small groups:

   |G| = 15 = 3*5: n_3 | 5 and n_3 = 1 (mod 3) => n_3 = 1
                    n_5 | 3 and n_5 = 1 (mod 5) => n_5 = 1
                    Unique Sylow subgroups => normal => G iso Z_15

   The Sylow theorems are a powerful tool: from the size of a group one can
   deduce its structure.


 Where this leads — connections to other topics
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+
| Direction            | Link          | Idea                         |
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+
|                      |               |                              |
| Lie groups           | -> (manifolds)| Group + smooth structure     |
|                      |               | SO(3), SU(2), GL(n)         |
|                      |               |                              |
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+
|                      |               |                              |
| Representation theory| -> (lin.alg.) | g |-> matrix rho(g)          |
|                      |               | Groups via linear algebra    |
|                      |               |                              |
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+
|                      |               |                              |
| Noether's theorem    | -> (DEs)      | Symmetry -> conservation law |
|                      |               | Time shift -> energy         |
|                      |               | Rotation -> angular momentum |
|                      |               |                              |
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+
|                      |               |                              |
| Fundamental group    | -> (topology) | Loops form a group           |
| pi_1                 |               | Classification of spaces     |
|                      |               |                              |
+----------------------+---------------+------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary: hierarchy of algebraic structures
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 From simple to complex


   Set
       |
       | + one operation + group axioms
       v
   Group (G, *)
       |
       | + commutativity
       v
   Abelian group
       |
       | + second operation with distributivity
       v
   Ring (R, +, x)
       |
       | + inverses for x
       v
   Field (F, +, x) <-- multiplication is commutative

       Important: Field = commutative multiplication (ab = ba).
       Removing commutativity => Division Ring.
       Example of a division ring: Quaternions H (ij != ji).

   Next — a different type of object (not a "special case of field"
   but a new set V with an action of field F on it):

   Set V + field F + addition in V + scalar multiplication from F
       v
   Vector space (V over F)
       |
       | + norm ||.||
       v
   Normed space
       |
       | + completeness (all limits exist)
       v
   Banach space
       |
       | + inner product (||x||^2 = <x,x>)
       v
   Hilbert space

 Each level inherits structure from the previous + adds something new.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Main idea
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Group = the minimal structure for describing symmetries.

   If you can:
     * Combine transformations (composition)
     * Do nothing (identity)
     * Undo actions (inverse)

   — you have a group.

   The symmetries of an object determine its properties.
   The symmetry group is the "DNA" of an object.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Applied example: balancing a turbine rotor
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: A turbine rotor with 6 blades. Vibration occurs during rotation.
 Need to understand which blade defects cause which vibration frequencies.

                     *
                    /|\        Symmetry group of the rotor:
                  /  |  \      C_6 = {e, r, r^2, r^3, r^4, r^5}
                *    |    *    where r = rotation by 60 deg
                |    o    |
                *    |    *    This is a cyclic group of order 6
                  \  |  /
                    \|/
                     *

 Key fact: Rotor vibration decomposes into representations of C_6

 +-----------------+---------------------+---------------------------------+
 | Representation  | Physical meaning    | Vibration frequency             |
 +-----------------+---------------------+---------------------------------+
 | Trivial         | All blades deflected| No vibration (perfect balance)  |
 | (symmetric)     | identically         |                                 |
 +-----------------+---------------------+---------------------------------+
 | Alternating     | Alternation +/-     | f = 3 x rev/s (3rd harmonic)    |
 |                 | "every other one"   |                                 |
 +-----------------+---------------------+---------------------------------+
 | 2-dimensional   | Imbalance "wave"    | f = n x rev/s (1st, 2nd)       |
 | representations | around circumference|                                 |
 +-----------------+---------------------+---------------------------------+

 Practical application:
   * Vibration at 1x rev/s -> static imbalance (one blade)
   * Vibration at 2x rev/s -> a pair of opposite blades
   * Vibration at 3x rev/s -> every other blade

 Group theory allows classifying imbalance types before measurement

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Another example: three-phase electrical network
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Three phases: A, B, C shifted by 120 deg

 Symmetry group: C_3 = {e, r, r^2} where r = phase shift by 120 deg

   * Symmetric load (all phases equal) -> neutral current = 0
   * Broken symmetry -> neutral current != 0

 The method of symmetrical components (Fortescue): decomposing an asymmetric
 system into symmetric components is a decomposition by representations of C_3.


===============================================================================
Hierarchy of algebraic structures
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why different structures are needed
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Each structure answers: "What do we want to be able to do?"

   Want to add                         -> Semigroup
   + there is a "zero"                 -> Monoid
   + can subtract                      -> Group
   + order does not matter             -> Abelian group
   + can multiply                      -> Ring
   + can divide (except by 0)          -> Field

 More operations -> more capability, but fewer examples.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hierarchy: what is added at each step
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  Semigroup -------> monoid -------> group -------> abelian group
       |                |              |                  |
   associativity    + identity     + inverses      + commutativity
   a(bc)=(ab)c        a*e=a        a*a^{-1}=e         ab=ba
       |                |              |                  |
   Example:          Example:       Example:           Example:
   (N+, *)           (N, +, 0)     (Z, +, 0)          (Z, +)


  Abelian group -------> ring -------> field
         |                   |             |
     (one operation)    + multiplication  + division
                        distributivity     (except 0)
                        a(b+c)=ab+ac       a!=0 => exists a^{-1}
         |                   |             |
     Example:            Example:       Example:
     (Z, +)              (Z, +, x)     (Q, +, x)


 Where they appear in practice
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Structure  | Examples                                          |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|            |                                                   |
| Semigroup  | String concatenation "abc"+"def"="abcdef"         |
|            | (actually a monoid — the empty string "" exists)  |
|            |                                                   |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|            |                                                   |
| Monoid     | (N, +, 0) — naturals with zero. Cannot subtract.  |
|            | Functions with composition . and identity id       |
|            |                                                   |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|            |                                                   |
| Group      | Symmetries (everything is reversible)             |
|            | Cryptography: elliptic curves, RSA                |
|            |                                                   |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|            |                                                   |
| Ring       | Polynomials Z[x] — can +,-,x but not /            |
|            | Matrices n x n — not every one is invertible       |
|            | Integers Z — 5/2 is not an integer                |
|            |                                                   |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|            |                                                   |
