| Field      | Q, R, C — everything works: +,-,x,/               |
|            | Finite fields F_p — cryptography, codes            |
|            |                                                   |
+------------+---------------------------------------------------+

Summary table:
+----------------+--------+---------+----------+-----------+---+---------+
| Structure      | Assoc. | Identity| Inverses | Commutat. | x | Division|
+----------------+--------+---------+----------+-----------+---+---------+
| Semigroup      | ok     |         |          |           |   |         |
| Monoid         | ok     | ok      |          |           |   |         |
| Group          | ok     | ok      | ok       |           |   |         |
| Abelian group  | ok     | ok      | ok       | ok        |   |         |
| Ring           | ok     | ok      | ok       | (+)       | ok|         |
| Field          | ok     | ok      | ok       | ok ok     | ok| ok      |
+----------------+--------+---------+----------+-----------+---+---------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why this matters
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 When you see a new object, ask: "What structure is this?"

   * Matrices — a ring (can multiply, cannot always divide)
   * Functions [0,1]->R — a vector space (over the field R)
   * Permutations — a group (everything is invertible)
   * Polynomials — a ring (or even an algebra over a field)

 Knowing the structure tells you which theorems apply.


===============================================================================
Rings and fields — arithmetic + algebra
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why does the engineer need this?
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Rings and fields are not abstraction for abstraction's sake. They underlie:

   * Cryptography: RSA works in the ring Z_n (residues)
   * Error-correcting codes: QR codes, CDs, internet — Galois fields GF(2^8)
   * Discrete mathematics: hash functions, checksums
   * Signals: the Z-transform is a ring of formal power series

 Main idea: sometimes we need arithmetic where numbers "wrap around"
 (like a clock: after 12 comes 1), or where division works differently.

A group has one operation. But arithmetic has two: addition and multiplication.
How do we combine them?

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Ring = two linked operations
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: A ring is "arithmetic" where one can add, subtract,
 multiply, but not necessarily divide.

 Formally: A ring (R, +, *) is a set R with two operations:

   * (R, +) is an abelian group (addition works as usual)
   * (R, *) is a monoid (multiplication exists, but inverses may not)
   * a*(b + c) = a*b + a*c  (distributivity — parentheses expand)

 Examples:

   +-----------------+---------------------------------------+
   | Ring            | Why a ring, not a field                |
   +-----------------+---------------------------------------+
   | Z (integers)    | 2 is not invertible: 1/2 not in Z     |
   | Z_n (residues)  | If n is not prime, zero divisors exist |
   | Z[x] (polynom.) | x is not invertible: 1/x is not a poly|
   | M_n(R) (matrices)| Singular matrices are not invertible  |
   +-----------------+---------------------------------------+

 Zero divisors — a ring peculiarity:
   In Z_6:  2 * 3 = 6 = 0 (mod 6)
   Both factors are nonzero, yet the product = 0.
   This never happens with ordinary numbers — a sign of structural "defect."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Field = Ring where division is possible
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: A field is "full-fledged arithmetic" with division.
 Everything we learned in school about numbers — those are field properties.

 Formally: A field is a ring where every a != 0 has an inverse a^{-1}.

 Examples of fields:
   * Q (rationals) — the minimal field containing Z
   * R (reals) — the completion of Q
   * C (complex) — algebraically closed
   * Z_p for prime p — a finite field (important for cryptography)

 Why Z_p is a field for prime p?

   In Z_5: elements {0, 1, 2, 3, 4}

   Inverses: 1^{-1}=1, 2^{-1}=3 (because 2*3 = 6 = 1 mod 5)
             3^{-1}=2, 4^{-1}=4 (because 4*4 = 16 = 1 mod 5)

   Every nonzero element is invertible. This is a field.

 Why Z_6 is not a field?

   In Z_6: 2 * 3 = 0, so 2 and 3 are zero divisors.
   A zero divisor cannot be invertible (otherwise 0 = 2^{-1}*0 =
   2^{-1}*2*3 = 3 != 0).

 Theorem: Z_n is a field iff n is prime.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Ideals — "divisibility" in an abstract ring
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: An ideal is a generalization of "all numbers divisible by n."

 In Z: the set of all multiples of 3 is {..., -6, -3, 0, 3, 6, ...}
       Notation: 3Z or (3)

 Key property: if a is divisible by 3, then a*k is also divisible by 3.
 "Divisibility absorbs multiplication" — this is the definition of an ideal.

 Formally: An ideal I sub R is a subset such that:
   * I is a subgroup under addition
   * a in I, r in R  =>  r*a in I  (multiplication by any element stays in I)

 Examples:

   +----------------+----------------------------------------+
   | Ring           | Examples of ideals                     |
   +----------------+----------------------------------------+
   | Z              | nZ = {nk : k in Z} — all ideals are   |
   |                | of this form                           |
   | R[x]           | (x^2 + 1) = all multiples of (x^2 + 1)|
   | C(X) (functions)| {f : f(x_0) = 0} — functions vanishing|
   |                | at x_0                                 |
   +----------------+----------------------------------------+

 Why ideals?
   Ideals allow "gluing" ring elements — just as normal subgroups allow
   gluing group elements. The result is a quotient ring.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Quotient ring — "arithmetic of residues"
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: The quotient ring R/I is "gluing" elements that differ by an
 element of I. As though everything in I became zero.

 Main example:

   Z/3Z = {0-bar, 1-bar, 2-bar} — residues modulo 3

   Here 0-bar = {..., -6, -3, 0, 3, 6, ...} (all multiples of 3 "glued" to 0)
        1-bar = {..., -5, -2, 1, 4, 7, ...}
        2-bar = {..., -4, -1, 2, 5, 8, ...}

   Arithmetic: 2-bar + 2-bar = 4-bar = 1-bar,  2-bar * 2-bar = 4-bar = 1-bar

 A deeper example — how to construct C:

   Problem: in R there is no square root of -1.
   Solution: C = R[x]/(x^2 + 1)

   Take polynomials in x with real coefficients.
   "Glue" all multiples of (x^2 + 1), i.e. set x^2 + 1 = 0.
   Then x^2 = -1, and x plays the role of i.

   Elements: a + bx (higher powers reduce: x^2 -> -1)
   These are precisely the complex numbers a + bi.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Finite fields — cryptography and codes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Theorem: A finite field exists if and only if the number of elements
 = p^n (a prime power). Notation: GF(p^n) or F_{p^n}.

   * GF(2) = {0, 1} — binary arithmetic (XOR = addition)
   * GF(2^8) = 256 elements — used in AES, QR codes
   * GF(p) = Z_p — the simplest finite fields

 Example: GF(4) — a field with 4 elements

   One cannot simply take Z_4 — there 2*2 = 0, zero divisors.

   Correct construction: GF(4) = GF(2)[x]/(x^2 + x + 1)
   Elements: {0, 1, x, x+1} with arithmetic mod 2 and mod (x^2 + x + 1)

   Multiplication table:
   +---+---+---+---+-------+------+
   | * | 0 |   1   |   x   |  x+1 |
   +---+---+---+---+-------+------+
   | 0 | 0 |   0   |   0   |   0  |
   | 1 | 0 |   1   |   x   |  x+1 |
   | x | 0 |   x   |  x+1  |   1  |   <- x^2 = x+1 (from x^2+x+1=0)
   |x+1| 0 |  x+1  |   1   |   x  |
   +---+---+-------+-------+------+

 Application: Reed-Solomon codes (CD, DVD, QR) work over GF(2^8).
 This allows correcting errors in a mathematically exact way.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hierarchy: from set to field
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

         Set (just elements)
              |
              v + one operation
         Semigroup (associativity)
              |
              v + identity element
          Monoid
              |
              v + inverse elements
          Group
              |
              v + commutativity
       Abelian group
              |
              v + second operation (multiplication) + distributivity
          Ring (Z, Z[x], matrices)
              |
              v + no zero divisors
     Integral domain (Z)
              |
              v + every nonzero element invertible
           Field (Q, R, C, Z_p)

 At each step a property is added -> the structure becomes "better."
 A field is the "best" arithmetical structure.


===============================================================================
Number theory — arithmetic as structure
===============================================================================

 Number theory as a view of space


 Numbers are not merely objects for computation. Numbers form spaces
 with rich structure.

 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+
 | Space             | Structure              | What we study              |
 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+
 | Z                 | Ring (+ and x)         | Divisibility, primes       |
 |                   | + ordering             |                            |
 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+
 | Z_n = Z/nZ        | Finite ring            | Modular arithmetic         |
 | (residues mod n)  | When n=p — a field     | Cryptography               |
 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+
 | Q_p (p-adic)      | Field with ultrametric | Local analysis             |
 |                   | |x+y|_p<=max(|x|,|y|) | Diophantine equations      |
 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+
 | Z[i] (Gaussian)   | Ring in C              | Sums of two squares        |
 |                   | Euclidean              |                            |
 +-------------------+------------------------+----------------------------+

 Key idea: The same number can be viewed in different spaces

   Number 7:
     * In Z: prime, irreducible
     * In Z_7: zero (7 = 0 mod 7)
     * In Z[i]: still prime (7 = 3 mod 4, not a sum of two squares)
     * In Z[sqrt(-5)]: remains prime

   Number 6 in Z[sqrt(-5)]: two different factorizations
     = 2 * 3 = (1+sqrt(-5))(1-sqrt(-5))
     This shows that Z[sqrt(-5)] lacks unique factorization.

 Number theory studies how arithmetic properties depend on
 the algebraic structure of the space.


Number theory studies properties of integers. It is one of the oldest areas
of mathematics, yet connected to the most modern: cryptography, algebraic
geometry, representation theory.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Divisibility — basic concepts
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 a | b  means  "a divides b"  iff  exists k in Z: b = a*k

 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
 | Concept           | Definition                                        |
 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
 | gcd(a,b)          | Greatest common divisor                           |
 |                   | max{d : d|a and d|b}                              |
 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
 | lcm(a,b)          | Least common multiple                             |
 |                   | min{m &gt; 0 : a|m and b|m}                        |
 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
 | Coprime           | gcd(a,b) = 1                                      |
 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
 | Prime number p    | p &gt; 1, divisors only 1 and p                    |
 +-------------------+---------------------------------------------------+

 Key relation:  gcd(a,b) * lcm(a,b) = a * b

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Fundamental theorem of arithmetic
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Every natural number n &gt; 1 factors uniquely (up to order)
 into a product of primes:

   n = p_1^{a_1} * p_2^{a_2} * ... * p_k^{a_k}

 +---------------------+
 | Examples:            |
 | 60 = 2^2 * 3 * 5    |
 | 100 = 2^2 * 5^2     |
 | 2024 = 2^3 * 11 * 23|
 +---------------------+

 Consequences:

 +--------------------+--------------------------------------------------+
 | Operation          | Via factorization                                |
 +--------------------+--------------------------------------------------+
 | gcd(a,b)           | Product of p^{min(a_p, b_p)} over all p          |
 | lcm(a,b)           | Product of p^{max(a_p, b_p)} over all p          |
 | a | b              | a_p <= b_p for all p                             |
 | Number of divisors | (a_1+1)(a_2+1)...(a_k+1)                        |
 +--------------------+--------------------------------------------------+

 Analogy with vectors:
   Number n <-> vector (a_1, a_2, a_3, ...) — prime exponents
   Multiplication <-> vector addition
   gcd <-> component-wise min
   lcm <-> component-wise max

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Congruences — arithmetic of residues
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 a = b (mod n)  means  n | (a - b)  iff  a and b give the same remainder

 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Properties (congruences can be added, multiplied, exponentiated)     |
 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | a = b, c = d  =>  a + c = b + d (mod n)                              |
 | a = b, c = d  =>  a * c = b * d (mod n)                              |
 | a = b         =>  a^k = b^k (mod n)                                  |
 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Ring Z_n = {0, 1, 2, ..., n-1} with operations mod n

 +-------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | n           | Structure of Z_n                                   |
 +-------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | n = p       | Field. Every nonzero element is invertible.         |
 | (prime)     | Example: Z_5, where 2*3 = 6 = 1, so 2^{-1} = 3    |
 +-------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | n = p^k     | Local ring (unique maximal ideal)                  |
 +-------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | n = p*q     | Has zero divisors. In Z_6: 2*3 = 0                 |
 | (composite) | But by CRT: Z_n iso Z_p x Z_q if gcd(p,q)=1        |
 +-------------+----------------------------------------------------+

 CRT (Chinese Remainder Theorem):
   If gcd(m,n) = 1, then Z_{mn} iso Z_m x Z_n
   Practically: the system x = a (mod m), x = b (mod n) has a unique solution

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Fermat's little theorem and Euler's function
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 phi(n) = Euler's function = count of numbers from 1 to n coprime to n

 +-------------+----------------+----------------------------------------+
 | n           | phi(n)         | Formula                                |
 +-------------+----------------+----------------------------------------+
 | p (prime)   | p - 1          | All except 0 are coprime to p          |
 | p^k         | p^k - p^{k-1}  | = p^k(1 - 1/p)                        |
 | m*n         | phi(m)*phi(n)  | if gcd(m,n) = 1 (multiplicative)       |
 | general     | n * prod(1-1/p)| product over all primes p | n          |
 +-------------+----------------+----------------------------------------+

 Theorems:
 +---------------------+--------------------------------------+
 | Fermat's little thm | a^p = a (mod p) for any a            |
 | (p prime)           | If gcd(a,p)=1: a^{p-1} = 1 (mod p)  |
 +---------------------+--------------------------------------+
 | Euler's theorem     | a^{phi(n)} = 1 (mod n) if gcd(a,n)=1 |
 | (generalization)    | Generalizes Fermat to composite n     |
 +---------------------+--------------------------------------+

 Application — RSA cryptography:
   Choose large primes p, q. Let n = p*q, phi(n) = (p-1)(q-1).
   Choose e coprime to phi(n), find d: e*d = 1 (mod phi(n)).
   Encrypt: c = m^e mod n. Decrypt: m = c^d mod n.
   Works by Euler's theorem: m^{ed} = m^{1 + k*phi(n)} = m (mod n).

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 P-adic numbers — an alternative completion of Q
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 R is the completion of Q under the usual metric |x - y|.
 But there are other metrics on Q.

 p-adic norm:
   |x|_p = p^{-v_p(x)}, where v_p(x) = power of p in the factorization of x

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Examples (p = 5):                                                       |
 |   |25|_5 = 5^{-2} = 1/25  (25 = 5^2, many fives -> small norm)         |
 |   |1/5|_5 = 5^1 = 5       (few fives in numerator)                     |
 |   |7|_5 = 5^0 = 1         (no fives at all)                            |
 |   |0|_5 = 0                                                            |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+

 +--------------------+--------------------+-----------------------------+
 | Property           | Usual norm |.|     | p-adic |.|_p                |
 +--------------------+--------------------+-----------------------------+
 | Large numbers      | Far from 0         | Can be close to 0!          |
 | Triangle ineq.     | |x+y| <= |x|+|y|  | |x+y|_p<=max(|x|_p,|y|_p)  |
 |                    |                    | (Ultrametric — stronger)    |
 | Completion         | R                  | Q_p (p-adic numbers)        |
 | Algebraic closure  | C (dim 2 over R)   | C_p (infinite-dimensional)  |
 +--------------------+--------------------+-----------------------------+

 Why this matters:
   * Local-global principle: an equation has a solution in Q iff
     it has a solution in R and in all Q_p (with caveats)
   * Modern algebraic geometry works over all these fields
   * Number theory: many problems are easier to solve "locally" in Q_p


 Connection to other topics
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Topic              | Link to number theory                        |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Groups             | (Z/nZ)* — group of invertible elements       |
 |                    | Order = phi(n), Lagrange's thm -> Euler      |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Rings              | Z is a principal ideal domain                |
 |                    | Ideal (n) = nZ, quotient ring = Z_n          |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Fields             | Z_p — finite field, extensions -> coding th. |
 |                    | Q_p — local field for arithmetic geometry     |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Topology           | Z_p = lim Z/p^n Z — projective limit         |
 |                    | Topology on Z_p: base = classes mod p^n       |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Complex analysis   | zeta(s) = sum n^{-s} — Riemann zeta function |
 |                    | Connects primes and complex analysis          |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------------+


 Galois theory — why there is no formula for 5th-degree roots


 The quadratic equation is solved by formula (known ~2000 years).
 Cubic and quartic — also (Cardano, Ferrari, 16th century).
 For the quintic, Abel (1824) proved: no general formula exists.
 Galois (1832) explained why — and created group theory.

 Key idea:
   To each polynomial p(x) there corresponds a group Gal(p) — the group
   of permutations of roots preserving all algebraic relations.

 Galois's theorem:
   A polynomial is solvable by radicals (roots expressed via +,-,x,/,root)
   if and only if its Galois group is solvable.

 Why it works:
   Extracting a root adds a "layer" of symmetry — a cyclic group Z/nZ.
   A solvable group = can be decomposed into a "tower" of cyclic subgroups.
   The symmetric group S_5 is not solvable (it contains the simple group A_5).
   Therefore the general 5th-degree polynomial is not solvable by radicals.

 Galois correspondence:

 +---------------------------+---------------------------+
 | Subgroups of Gal(p)       | Intermediate fields       |
 +---------------------------+---------------------------+
 | Gal(p)                    | Q (base field)            |
 | {e} (trivial)             | Splitting field           |
 | Normal subgroup H <| G    | Normal extension          |
 +---------------------------+---------------------------+

 This bijective "dictionary" between groups and fields is one of the
 deepest ideas in mathematics: a problem about equations is solved
 through symmetries.

===============================================================================
Table of groups — a systematics of symmetries
===============================================================================

 Group = symmetries of an object
 Task: classify groups and their connections

 Hierarchy of groups


                     All groups
                    /          \
           Finite             Infinite
          /        \            /           \
     Abelian    Non-abelian  Discrete    Continuous
        |           |           |         (Lie groups)
     Z/n, ...   S_n, D_n,...  Z, F_n,...     |
                                         Compact / Non-compact
                                             |              |
                                        SO(n), SU(n)    R^n, GL(n)


Finite groups — a complete classification exists

+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
| Group     | Order   | Geometric meaning / connections               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| {e}       | 1       | Trivial: "do nothing"                         |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| Z/n       | n       | Cyclic: rotations of a regular n-gon          |
|           |         | iso roots of unity: {1,w,w^2,...},w=e^(2pi*i/n)|
|           |         | Abelian. Z/p (p prime) — simplest "atoms"     |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| Z/2 x Z/2| 4       | Klein four-group: symmetries of a rectangle   |
| (Klein    |         | Not cyclic (no element of order 4)            |
|  four-grp)|         |                                               |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| D_n       | 2n      | Dihedral: rotations + reflections of n-gon    |
|           |         | D_3 iso S_3 (the only such case)              |
|           |         | Non-abelian for n >= 3                        |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| S_n       | n!      | Symmetric: all permutations of n elements     |
|           |         | Every finite group sub S_n (Cayley's theorem) |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| A_n       | n!/2    | Alternating: even permutations                |
|           |         | A_5 — simplest non-abelian simple group       |
|           |         | A_5 iso symmetries of the icosahedron         |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+
|           |         |                                               |
| Q_8       | 8       | Quaternion: {+/-1, +/-i, +/-j, +/-k}          |
|           |         | Non-abelian, but all subgroups are normal     |
|           |         | Connected to 3D rotations (see quaternions)   |
|           |         |                                               |
+-----------+---------+-----------------------------------------------+

Infinite discrete groups:

+--------+-------------------------------------------------+
| Group  | What it is / connections                         |
+--------+-------------------------------------------------+
|        |                                                 |
| (Z, +) | Integers: integer shifts along the line         |
|        | pi_1(S^1) = Z — fundamental group of the circle |
|        | The unique infinite cyclic group                |
|        |                                                 |
+--------+-------------------------------------------------+
|        |                                                 |
| Z^n    | Lattice: vertices of an integer lattice in R^n  |
|        | pi_1(T^n) = Z^n — fundamental group of n-torus  |
|        |                                                 |
+--------+-------------------------------------------------+
|        |                                                 |
| F_n    | Free on n generators: all words in n letters    |
|        | pi_1(wedge^n S^1) = F_n — bouquet of n circles  |
|        | "Universal": every group is its quotient         |
|        |                                                 |
+--------+-------------------------------------------------+

Lie groups (continuous) — symmetries of physics:

+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
| Group          |dim | What it is / where it appears                         |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| U(1) iso S^1   |  1 | Complex numbers |z|=1, plane rotations                |
|                |    | Phase in quantum mechanics, electromagnetism          |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SO(2)          |  1 | Plane rotations (iso U(1) as Lie groups)              |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SO(3)          |  3 | Rotations in R^3: rigid body orientation              |
|                |    | Not simply connected: pi_1(SO(3)) = Z/2              |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SU(2)          |  3 | Unitary 2x2 with det=1, double cover of SO(3)        |
|                |    | Spin in quantum mechanics.                           |
|                |    | SU(2) iso S^3 (3-sphere) — simply connected          |
|                |    |                                                       |
|                |    | Important for robotics:                              |
|                |    | Quaternion q and -q define the same rotation.        |
|                |    | 360 deg rotation gives -1, need 720 deg to return.   |
|                |    | Not a bug, it's topology: pi_1(SO(3)) = Z/2.        |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SU(3)          |  8 | Symmetry of strong interaction (quarks)               |
|                |    | Standard model: SU(3)xSU(2)xU(1)                    |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SO(3,1)        |  6 | Lorentz group: spacetime symmetries                  |
|                |    | Special relativity                                   |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| GL(n,R)        | n^2| All invertible n x n matrices (det != 0)             |
|                |    | "General linear group"                               |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+
|                |    |                                                       |
| SL(n,R)        |n^2-1| Matrices with det = 1 (volume-preserving)           |
|                |    |                                                       |
+----------------+----+-------------------------------------------------------+

 Key connections


 1. Fundamental groups (topology -> algebra):
    pi_1(S^1) = Z,  pi_1(T^2) = Z^2,  pi_1(wedge_n S^1) = F_n

 2. Coverings (links between groups):
    SU(2) ->2:1 SO(3),   R -> S^1 (exp),   SL(2,C) ->2:1 SO(3,1)

 3. Classification of finite simple groups (completed ~1980):
    Cyclic Z/p + Alternating A_n (n>=5) +
    Groups of Lie type + 26 sporadic (including the "Monster")

 4. Classification theorem for finite abelian groups:
    Every such group iso Z/n_1 x Z/n_2 x ... x Z/n_k (unique decomposition)


Groups describe symmetries — what can be done with a space. But to
talk about continuity of transformations, we need a notion of closeness.
What does "points are nearby" mean? What does "a transformation does not
tear the space" mean?

This is the domain of topology — the next fundamental view of a space.


===============================================================================
Topology — the study of closeness without distances
===============================================================================

If a set is dust, then topology is fabric. We add to points the notion
of "nearby": which points can be considered close, which cannot. But we
still do not know how close — there are no distance-numbers.

This is the minimal structure for continuity. To say "a function is continuous,"
it suffices to know which points are nearby. Specific distances are unnecessary.

In "object-observer" terms: topology is a property of the space itself,
not of the observer. Two observers with different coordinate systems will see
the same topology: the same holes, the same connectedness, the same boundaries.
Topological properties are invariants independent of the mode of description.

This is precisely why topology is so fundamental: it speaks about the shape
of an object, not about how the observer records it.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Topology as a viewpoint on space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Recall from the introduction: each branch of mathematics is a way of
 looking at a space. Topology sees in a space only the closeness of points,
 but not distances between them.

   Set -> topological space -> metric space
   (points)     (closeness)         (distances)

 Topology is the "middle ground": more structure than a bare set,
 but less than a metric space.


 Main example: doughnut = mug


       Doughnut (torus)                    Mug

          +--------+                    +-+
        +-   +--+  -+               +--  +-+
        |    |  |    |               | +-+   |
        |    |  |    |               | | |   |
        +-   +--+  -+               | +-+   |
          +--------+                 |       |
                                     +-------+

   One hole                        One hole (the handle)

   Topologically identical. One can be continuously deformed into the other.
   But the doughnut cannot be turned into a mug without a handle — the hole
   would disappear.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why topology is needed
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: We want to talk about "closeness" and "continuity," but:

   * Not always is there a distance (how to measure distance between functions?)
   * Sometimes distance is redundant (we care about shape, not size)
   * Different distances can yield the same "closeness"

 Solution: Define "closeness" directly, without distance.
           Instead of d(x,y) &lt; eps we say: "y is in a neighborhood of x"

 Analogy: Metro map vs city map
          * City map: exact distances, scale
          * Metro map: only connections between stations
          For navigating the metro, distances are unnecessary

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 What topology studies
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Geometry: angles, lengths, distances — metric invariants
 Topology: connectedness, holes, number of components — topological invariants

 +--------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Topology studies properties invariant under continuous deformations |
 | (homeomorphisms). Allowed: stretching, squeezing, bending.         |
 | Forbidden: tearing, gluing.                                        |
 +--------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Homeomorphic objects:
 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+
 | Objects                  | Topological invariant                      |
 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+
 | circle iso square iso    | No holes, pi_1 = 0                        |
 |   triangle               |                                            |
 | Mug iso Torus            | One hole, pi_1 = Z                        |
 | O iso D, B iso 8, A iso R| Classification by number of holes          |
 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Physical viewpoint: a frozen object
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Topology looks at a space as a frozen object.
 A group looks at it as a dynamical system.

 Four questions of topology:

   1. How many holes does the object have?
      A doughnut — one. A mug with a handle — also one. They are "the same."
      A ball — none. It cannot be deformed into a doughnut.

   2. Does the object have a boundary?
      A disk does (the circle). A sphere does not. A Moebius strip has one edge.

   3. Can it be cut without splitting into parts?
      Cut a torus across — a tube remains (one piece).
      Cut a sphere — it falls into two caps (two pieces).

   4. Can one distinguish "left" and "right" on it?
      On a Moebius strip — one cannot. It is non-orientable.

 These are all invariants — properties that do not change under deformation.

 Contrast with groups:

   A group asks:     What motions are possible with this object?
   Topology asks:    What is the shape of this object?

   Group: dynamics of motion -> SE(3), SO(3), D_4
   Topology: statics of shape -> holes (pi_1), connectedness (pi_0), Euler (chi)

   Both give classification — they establish what is possible and what is not.
   Both answer "what type is this object."

 Example of the connection: The fundamental group pi_1(X) is a group
 that classifies loops in a topological space X.
 Topology and algebra meet.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Alphabet letters: classification by holes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   No holes (all the same):    C, G, I, J, L, M, N, S, U, V, W, Z

   One hole (all the same):    A, D, O, P, Q, R

   Two holes (all the same):   B, 8

 This is the complete classification of letters from the topological viewpoint.
 The letter "O" and the letter "D" are the same object (deformable).
 The letter "O" and the letter "C" are different (C has no hole).


===============================================================================
Open sets — the basic concept
===============================================================================

 Definition of an open set:
 A set U sub X is called open if for every point x in U there exists
 a neighborhood of x entirely contained in U.

 Equivalent formulation (for a metric space):
 forall x in U  exists eps &gt; 0:  B(x, eps) sub U

 +----------------------------------------------------------+
 | x in U  iff  x is an interior point  iff                 |
 |              exists eps &gt; 0: B(x,eps) sub U              |
 +----------------------------------------------------------+

 Boundary points: forall eps &gt; 0, the ball B(x,eps) contains points
 from both U and X\U.
 An open set does not contain its boundary points.


 Examples on the real line R
 +---------------+--------------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Interval type | Visualization            | Property                       |
 +---------------+--------------------------+--------------------------------+
 |               |                          |                                |
 | open          | --o================o--   | Open: every point has          |
 | (0, 1)        |   0 excluded  1 excluded | "room" inside                  |
 |               |                          |                                |
 +---------------+--------------------------+--------------------------------+
 |               |                          |                                |
 | closed        | --*================*--   | Closed: contains boundary      |
 | [0, 1]        |   0 included  1 included | No "room" at the edges         |
 |               |                          |                                |
 +---------------+--------------------------+--------------------------------+
 |               |                          |                                |
 | half-open     | --*================o--   | Neither open nor closed        |
 | [0, 1)        |   0 included  1 excluded | This happens                   |
 |               |                          |                                |
 +---------------+--------------------------+--------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Openness depends on the ambient space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The same set can be open in one space and not open in another.

 Example 1: The set [0, 1)
   * In R: not open (the point 0 is a boundary point)
   * In [0, inf): open (no points to the left of 0, so 0 is interior)

 Example 2: The set [0, 1/2)
   In the space X = [0, 1] with the topology induced from R:
   * [0, 1/2) is open in X (because [0, 1/2) = X cap (-1, 1/2))
   * But the point 0 intuitively seems like an "edge" — this is a trap

 Conclusion: When saying "open," always specify — in which space

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Definition of a metric
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A metric on a set X is a function d: X x X -> [0, +inf) such that:

   +-------------------------------------------------------------+
   | (M1)  d(x,y) = 0  iff  x = y       (identity of indiscern.)|
   | (M2)  d(x,y) = d(y,x)              (symmetry)              |
   | (M3)  d(x,z) <= d(x,y) + d(y,z)   (triangle inequality)   |
   +-------------------------------------------------------------+

 The pair (X, d) is called a metric space.

 Examples of metrics:
   +---------------------+-----------------------------------------------+
   | Space               | Metric                                        |
   +---------------------+-----------------------------------------------+
   | R                   | d(x,y) = |x - y|                              |
   | R^n (Euclidean)     | d(x,y) = sqrt(sum_i (x_i - y_i)^2)           |
   | R^n (Manhattan)     | d(x,y) = sum_i |x_i - y_i|                   |
   | R^n (sup-metric)    | d(x,y) = max_i |x_i - y_i|                   |
   | C[a,b] (functions)  | d(f,g) = max_{x in [a,b]} |f(x) - g(x)|     |
   | Discrete            | d(x,y) = 0 if x=y, else 1                    |
   +---------------------+-----------------------------------------------+

 Why metrics matter:
   * They define "closeness" quantitatively
   * They generate a topology (open sets via balls)
   * They allow speaking of convergence: x_n -> x iff d(x_n, x) -> 0

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Formal definition of an open set (via metric)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Let (X, d) be a metric space.

 Open ball: B(x, eps) = {y : d(x,y) &lt; eps}
            (all points closer than eps to center x)

 A set U is called open if:

   +--------------------------------------------------------+
   | forall x in U  exists eps &gt; 0:  B(x, eps) sub U      |
   |                                                        |
   | "For every point x in U there is a ball centered at x  |
   | lying entirely in U"                                   |
   +--------------------------------------------------------+

 Visually:

        U — open                          V — not open
     +-----------------+               +-----------------+
     |    +---+        |               |            *----+ <- point on
     |    | * | ball   |               |           no    |   boundary,
     |    +---+ inside |               |           ball  |   ball does
     |                 |               |                 |   not fit
     +-----------------+               +-----------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Closed sets
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Closed set = complement of an open set

   F is closed  iff  X \ F is open

 Equivalently: F contains all its limit points.
               (If a sequence from F converges, the limit is also in F)

 Examples on R:

   [0, 1]     closed    (R \ [0,1] = (-inf,0) cup (1,+inf) — open)
   {0}        closed    (a single point — degenerate case)
   [0, +inf)  closed    (half-line with included endpoint)
   Q          not closed (sqrt(2) is a limit point, but sqrt(2) not in Q)

 Warning: "Not open" != "Closed."

   [0, 1)  — neither open nor closed
   empty   — both open and closed (the only such sets: empty and all of X)
   R       — both open and closed

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Boundary, interior, closure
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 For any set A sub X:

   Int(A) = interior = largest open subset of A
          = {x in A : exists eps &gt; 0, B(x,eps) sub A}

   Cl(A)  = closure = smallest closed superset of A
          = A cup {all limit points of A}

   dA     = boundary = Cl(A) \ Int(A)
          = points in every neighborhood of which there are both A and non-A

 Example: A = (0, 1]

     -------o========================*-------
            0                         1

   Int(A) = (0, 1)     — removed point 1 (it is on the boundary)
   Cl(A)  = [0, 1]     — added point 0 (a limit point)
   dA     = {0, 1}     — two boundary points

 Connections:
   A is open   iff  A = Int(A)  iff  A does not contain its boundary
   A is closed iff  A = Cl(A)   iff  A contains its boundary


===============================================================================
Topological space — the abstraction
===============================================================================

 Idea: forget the metric, keep only the "open sets"


 Observation: All properties of continuity can be expressed through open
              sets without mentioning distance.

 Idea: What if we directly declare which sets are "open"?
       Not derive from a metric, but simply declare.

 Rules are needed so that "open" sets behave sensibly.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Definition of a topology
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A topology on a set X is a family tau sub P(X) of subsets of X
 satisfying three axioms:

   +--------------------------------------------------------+
   | (T1)  empty in tau  and  X in tau                      |
   | The empty set and the whole space are open              |
   |                                                        |
   | (T2)  U_1, U_2 in tau  =>  U_1 cap U_2 in tau         |
   | Intersection of two open sets is open                  |
   | (by induction: finite intersection of open sets is open)|
   |                                                        |
   | (T3)  {U_i}_{i in I} sub tau  =>  union_i U_i in tau  |
   | Union of any family of open sets is open               |
   | (even infinite, even uncountable)                      |
   +--------------------------------------------------------+

 The pair (X, tau) is called a topological space.
 Elements of tau are called open sets.


 Why these axioms

 These axioms are derived from properties of open sets in metric spaces:

 (T1) Obvious: everywhere there is "room," nowhere there is "room"

 (T2) If in U_1 there is a ball of radius eps_1, and in U_2 one of eps_2,
      then in the intersection there is a ball of radius min(eps_1, eps_2)

      Why only finite? With infinite intersection, min can be 0.

      Example: cap_n (-1/n, 1/n) = {0} — a single point, not open.

 (T3) If a point is in some U_i, it has a ball in that U_i,
      hence also in the union. Works for any family.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Examples of topologies
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Let X = {a, b, c} — three points. Compare topologies:

 +----------------+--------------------------+--------------------+
 | Topology       | Open sets                | Meaning            |
 +----------------+--------------------------+--------------------+
 |                |                          |                    |
 | Discrete       | All subsets:             | Points are "far"   |
 | (maximal)      | empty,{a},{b},{c},{a,b}, | from each other,   |
 |                | {a,c},{b,c},{a,b,c}      | each is isolated   |
 |                |                          |                    |
 +----------------+--------------------------+--------------------+
 |                |                          |                    |
 | Indiscrete     | Only empty and X:        | Points "stuck      |
 | (minimal)      | {empty, {a,b,c}}         | together,"         |
 |                |                          | indistinguishable  |
 |                |                          |                    |
 +----------------+--------------------------+--------------------+
 |                |                          |                    |
 | Intermediate   | {empty, {a}, {a,b},      | a is "open,"       |
 | (example)      |  {a,b,c}}               | c is "closed"      |
 |                |                          |                    |
 +----------------+--------------------------+--------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Standard topology on R
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 tau = {U sub R : forall x in U exists eps &gt; 0: (x-eps, x+eps) sub U}

 Open sets = those where every point is surrounded by an interval.

 Basis of a topology:

   One need not describe all open sets.
   It suffices to specify a basis — a set of "building blocks" from
   which all others are obtained by unions.

   For R: basis = all open intervals (a, b)
   For R^n: basis = all open balls B(x, eps)

 Important: The standard topology on R is generated by the metric d(x,y)=|x-y|
            But other topologies can be defined on the same R.


 Metric -> Topology (but not conversely)


 Any metric d on X generates a topology:
   tau_d = {U : forall x in U exists eps &gt; 0: B_d(x,eps) sub U}

 But not every topology is generated by some metric.
 (Such topologies are called "metrizable")

 Example of a non-metrizable topology:
   Indiscrete on X with |X| &gt; 1: points cannot be separated by balls.

 Different metrics can yield the same topology:

   On R^2:  d_1(x,y) = |x_1-y_1| + |x_2-y_2|        (Manhattan)
            d_2(x,y) = sqrt((x_1-y_1)^2+(x_2-y_2)^2) (Euclidean)
            d_inf(x,y) = max(|x_1-y_1|, |x_2-y_2|)   (Chebyshev)

   Balls of different shapes:  <> (d_1)    O (d_2)    [] (d_inf)
   But the topology is the same. The same sets are open.

      <>          O           []
     / \         / \         +-+
    /   \       |   |        | |
    \   /       |   |        | |
     \ /         \ /         +-+

   Topologically equivalent (homeomorphic as balls)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Separation axioms — how "nice" is the space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: Not all topological spaces are "nice."
 In the indiscrete topology two distinct points are indistinguishable.

 Separation axioms define a hierarchy of "nice" spaces:

 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Axiom   | Condition                                                     |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |         |                                                               |
 | T_0     | For any x != y there is a neighborhood of one of the points  |
 |(Kolmog.)| not containing the other.                                     |
 |         | (At least some distinction is possible)                       |
 |         |                                                               |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |         |                                                               |
 | T_1     | For any x != y there is a neighborhood of x not containing y, |
 |(Frechet)| and a neighborhood of y not containing x.                     |
 |         | Equivalently: all singletons {x} are closed.                  |
 |         |                                                               |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |         |                                                               |
 | T_2     | For any x != y there exist disjoint neighborhoods:            |
 |(Hausd.) | U cont. x, V cont. y, U cap V = empty                        |
 |         |                                                               |
 |         |     x           y                                             |
 |         |    (U)         (V)        <- U and V do not intersect         |
 |         |   +---+       +---+                                           |
 |         |   | * |       | * |                                           |
 |         |   +---+       +---+                                           |
 |         |                                                               |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |         |                                                               |
 | T_3     | T_1 + for a point x and closed F not cont. x there exist     |
 |(regular)| disjoint neighborhoods of x and F.                            |
 |         |                                                               |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |         |                                                               |
 | T_4     | T_1 + for any disjoint closed F, G there exist               |
 |(normal) | disjoint neighborhoods.                                       |
 |         |                                                               |
 +---------+---------------------------------------------------------------+

 Hierarchy:

   T_4 (normal) sub T_3 (regular) sub T_2 (Hausdorff) sub T_1 sub T_0

 Why T_2 (Hausdorff) matters:

   1. Uniqueness of limits:
      In a Hausdorff space a sequence has at most one limit.
      (Without T_2 the limit may not be unique)

   2. Compact sets are closed:
      In a Hausdorff space a compact subset is closed.

   3. Practice:
      Almost all spaces in analysis and geometry are Hausdorff.
      R^n, manifolds, metric spaces are all T_2.

 Examples:

   ok  R^n with the usual topology — T_4 (normal)
   ok  Any metric space — T_4
   no  Indiscrete topology (|X| &gt; 1) — not even T_0
   no  Line with a doubled point — T_1 but not T_2

 Urysohn's lemma (consequence of T_4):
   In a normal space, for any disjoint closed F and G there exists
   a continuous f: X -> [0,1] with f|_F = 0 and f|_G = 1.
   (One can "smoothly" separate sets with a function)

===============================================================================
Continuous maps — preserving closeness
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Definition of continuity
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Let (X, tau_X) and (Y, tau_Y) be topological spaces.

 +-----------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: f: X -> Y is continuous iff                   |
 |   forall V in tau_Y: f^{-1}(V) in tau_X                  |
 | (the preimage of an open set is open)                     |
 +-----------------------------------------------------------+

 Here f^{-1}(V) = {x in X : f(x) in V} — the preimage (not the inverse).

           The preimage is always defined, even if f is not invertible.

 Visualization:

      X                           Y
   +-----------+    f        +-----------+
   | +------+  |   --->     |   +---+   |
   | |f^-1(V)| |             |   | V |   |   V open in Y
   | +------+  |             |   +---+   |
   +-----------+             +-----------+
     open?                     open ok

   f is continuous iff for any open V, its preimage f^{-1}(V) is open

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connection to the eps-delta definition
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Two definitions — one idea (equivalent for metric spaces):
 +----------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition     | Formulation                                        |
 +----------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | eps-delta      | forall eps>0 exists delta>0:                       |
 | (analysis)     |   d(x,x_0)<delta => d(f(x),f(x_0))<eps            |
 |                | "the image is arbitrarily close for close argument"|
 +----------------+----------------------------------------------------+
 | Topological    | Preimage of an open set is open                    |
 |                | "neighborhood of image <- neighborhood of argument"|
 +----------------+----------------------------------------------------+

 Why the topological version? It works without a metric.


 Examples and counterexamples
 +---------------------+-----------------------+-------------------------+
 | Function            | Continuous?           | Why                     |
 +---------------------+-----------------------+-------------------------+
 | f(x) = x^2          | YES                   | Preimage of (a,b) open  |
 | f(x) = sin(x)       | YES                   | Smooth => continuous    |
 | f(x) = |x|          | YES                   | Continuous at each pt   |
 | f(x) = floor(x)     | NO                    | Preimage not open       |
 | f(x) = theta(x)     | NO                    | Discontinuity at x=0   |
 | (Heaviside)         |                       |                         |
 +---------------------+-----------------------+-------------------------+

   The problem at 0: a jump.


===============================================================================
Homeomorphism — topological equivalence
===============================================================================

 Definition:
 A homeomorphism f: X -> Y is a bijection, continuous in both directions:

   1. f is a bijection (one-to-one correspondence)
   2. f is continuous
   3. f^{-1} is also continuous

 If a homeomorphism X -> Y exists, we write X iso Y ("X is homeomorphic to Y")

 Meaning: X and Y have the same topological structure.
          They differ only in the "names" of points.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Physical interpretation: a very viscous flow
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Imagine an object made of very viscous fluid (dough, clay, tar).
 A homeomorphism is a slow deformation in which:

   * The material flows, changes shape
   * But does not tear (one cannot create a hole)
   * And does not glue (one cannot seal a hole)

 Doughnut -> mug: the dough "flows over," the hole is preserved
 Doughnut -> ball: impossible without tearing (the hole must vanish)

 This intuition connects topology with hydrodynamics:
   * Homeomorphism = the result of an infinitely slow incompressible flow
   * Topological invariants = what is preserved under any such flow
   * Diffeomorphism = a smooth flow (without "folds")


 Why is continuity of f^{-1} needed?

 A continuous bijection need not be a homeomorphism.

 Counterexample:

   X = [0, 2pi)  with the usual topology (half-interval)
   Y = S^1       the circle
   f(t) = (cos t, sin t)

      [0------------2pi)         f          +------+
       *                       --->        /   *    \
       |                                 |    ^     |
       +-> wraps onto the circle         | start    |
                                          \        /
                                           +------+

   f is a bijection ok
   f is continuous ok
   f^{-1} is not continuous  no

   Why? Traversing the circle and approaching the starting point,
   f^{-1} makes a jump: ... -> 2pi-eps -> 0 (discontinuity)

   [0, 2pi) and S^1 are not homeomorphic, despite a continuous bijection.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Examples of homeomorphisms
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 (0, 1) iso R        via f(x) = tan(pi(x - 1/2))
                     Open interval <-> the entire line

 (0, 1) iso (0, inf) via f(x) = x/(1-x)

 Disk iso Square     "Inflate" the square into a disk

 R^2 \ {0} iso S^1 x R  Plane minus origin <-> Cylinder
                     (polar coordinates: (r,theta) |-> (theta, ln r))

 Not homeomorphic:

 [0,1] not-iso (0,1)  Closed interval != open (different number of endpoints)
 S^1 not-iso [0,1]    Circle != segment (the segment has endpoints)
 S^2 not-iso T^2      Sphere != torus (different number of holes)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hierarchy of equivalences
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Isometry sub Diffeomorphism sub Homeomorphism sub Homotopy equivalence

+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+
| Equivalence     | What it preserves | The square                   |
+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+
|                 |                   |                              |
| Isometry        | Distances         | iso only itself/rotation     |
| (strictest)     |                   |                              |
|                 |                   |                              |
+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+
|                 |                   |                              |
| Diffeomorphism  | Smooth structure  | iso disk (smoothly deform.)  |
|                 |                   |                              |
+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+
|                 |                   |                              |
| Homeomorphism   | Topology          | iso disk (continuously def.) |
|                 |                   |                              |
+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+
|                 |                   |                              |
| Homotopy equiv. | "Shape of holes"  | ~ point (contractible)       |
| (weakest)       |                   |                              |
|                 |                   |                              |
+-----------------+-------------------+------------------------------+

===============================================================================
Connectedness — "in one piece"
===============================================================================

 Connectedness — informal description


 A connected space: cannot be represented as the union of two disjoint
                    nonempty open sets

   Connected:                    Not connected:
   +-----------+               +-------+   +-------+
   |           |               |       |   |       |
   |     *     |               |   *   |   |   *   |
   |           |               |       |   |       |
   +-----------+               +-------+   +-------+
   one piece                   two separate pieces


 Formal definition

 A topological space X is called connected if there is no partition
 X = U cup V where:

   * U, V != empty       (both nonempty)
   * U cap V = empty     (disjoint)
   * U, V in tau         (both open)

 Equivalently: the only sets that are simultaneously open and closed
               are empty and X.

 Meaning: One cannot "cut" X into two open pieces.
          Between any two points there is a "topological path."


 Examples

 Connected:

   [0, 1]      Any partition into open sets is impossible
   (0, 1)      Also connected
   R           Connected
   R^n         Connected for any n
   S^1, S^2, S^n  All spheres are connected
   Disk, ball  Connected

 Not connected:

   (0,1) cup (2,3)   Two intervals = two pieces
                     U = (0,1), V = (2,3) — a partition

   Q               Rational numbers are not connected
                   Partition: {q &lt; sqrt(2)} and {q &gt; sqrt(2)}
                   (both open in the induced topology)

   R \ {0}         Line without zero = two rays
                   U = (-inf, 0), V = (0, +inf)

   {0, 1}          Two isolated points (discrete topology)
   (discrete)      U = {0}, V = {1} — both open

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Path-connectedness (stronger)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 X is path-connected if any two points can be joined by a path:

   forall x, y in X  exists gamma: [0,1] -> X continuous,
   gamma(0) = x,  gamma(1) = y

 Connection:
   Path-connected => Connected (always)
   Connected => Path-connected (for "nice" spaces, but not always)

 Counterexample (topologist's sine curve):

   A = {(x, sin(1/x)) : x &gt; 0} cup {(0, y) : -1 <= y <= 1}

       |++   ++   ++
   ----+  ---  ---  ---  <- oscillates infinitely
       |                   as x -> 0
       |
     vertical
     segment

   This is connected (in the topological sense)
   But not path-connected: no continuous path from the sine curve
   to the segment

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connected components
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The connected component of a point x is the maximal connected subset
 containing x.

 Properties:
   * X decomposes into disjoint connected components
   * There can be finitely many, countably many, or uncountably many

 Examples:
   R \ {0}       — two components: (-inf, 0) and (0, +inf)
   Z (discrete)  — countably many components: each point alone
   Cantor set    — uncountably many components (each point is a component)

 H_0 in homology counts connected components:
   H_0(X) = Z^k, where k = number of components


===============================================================================
Compactness — finiteness within infinity
===============================================================================

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why compactness is needed
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: On infinite or non-closed sets many theorems break down.

   * f(x) = x on (0, +inf): no maximum or minimum
   * f(x) = 1/x on (0, 1): continuous but unbounded
   * A sequence in R can "escape" to infinity

 Solution: Compactness is a property guaranteeing "finite-like" behavior
           on infinite sets.

 Analogy with thermal engineering:
   A compact space is like a bounded system with no "leaks."
   Energy cannot "escape to infinity."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Definition of compactness (via covers)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A cover of X is a family {U_i}_{i in I} such that X sub cup_i U_i
 An open cover is a cover by open sets.

 Definition:

   +--------------------------------------------------------------+
   | X is compact if from any open cover one can select a finite  |
   | subcover.                                                    |
   |                                                              |
   | forall {U_i} open cover of X                                 |
   | exists finite J sub I:  X sub cup_{j in J} U_j               |
   +--------------------------------------------------------------+

 Compactness: any open cover has a finite subcover.
           However finely we cover it, finitely many pieces suffice.


 Example: Why [0,1] is compact but (0,1) is not


 (0, 1) is not compact:

   Cover: U_n = (1/n, 1) for n = 2, 3, 4, ...

     U_2 = (1/2, 1)     +==============+
     U_3 = (1/3, 1)   +=================+
     U_4 = (1/4, 1)  +====================+
     ...            ...

   cup_n U_n = (0, 1) — a cover ok

   But any finite subcover U_{n_1}, ..., U_{n_k}
   covers only (1/N, 1), where N = max(n_1,...,n_k).
   Points in (0, 1/N) are not covered. no

 [0, 1] is compact:

   The same cover {(1/n, 1)} does not cover [0,1] — it misses 0.
   Any cover of [0,1] must contain a neighborhood of 0 and of 1.
   This "closes" the construction, allowing a finite subcover.

   (Formal proof: Heine-Borel theorem)

 Intuition: (0,1) is non-compact not because it is "large" — it is bounded.
   The problem is the absence of edges: covers can "leak" toward the boundary,
   requiring infinitely many sets to "catch up" with points near 0.
   Closedness adds edges to which covers "stick."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Heine-Borel theorem (for R^n)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   +-----------------------------------------------+
   | In R^n: X is compact  iff  X is closed and    |
   |                            bounded             |
   +-----------------------------------------------+

 Warning: This holds only for R^n. In a general topological space
          closed + bounded does not guarantee compactness.

 Counterexample (critical for functional analysis):
   The unit ball B = {f in L^2 : ||f|| <= 1} in infinite-dimensional L^2:
     * Closed? Yes
     * Bounded? Yes
     * Compact? No
   The sequence e_n(x) = sin(n*pi*x) is bounded but has no
   convergent subsequence in L^2.

   This breaks intuition from R^n. In infinite-dimensional spaces
   weak compactness or additional conditions are needed.

   Important: Although B is non-compact in the strong (norm) topology,
   it is weakly compact (Banach-Alaoglu theorem). This is critically
   important for the calculus of variations: the energy minimum exists
   precisely thanks to weak compactness.

 Why this is a disaster for the engineer:

   Weierstrass's theorem: "a continuous function on a compact set attains min."
   But if the space is non-compact, the minimum may not exist.

   In infinite-dimensional optimization (calculus of variations,
   neural network training) this means:
     * We descend a "hill," but there is no bottom
     * The minimizing sequence f_n "escapes to infinity"
     * The function becomes ever thinner and taller, ceasing to be a function

   Solution: add regularization (int |f'|^2 <= C), which makes
   the admissible set compact in the weak topology.

 Examples in R^n:

   +--------------------+-----------+-------------+------------------+
   | Set                | Closed?   | Bounded?    | Compact?         |
   +--------------------+-----------+-------------+------------------+
   | [0, 1]             | Yes       | Yes         | Yes              |
   | (0, 1)             | No        | Yes         | No               |
   | [0, +inf)          | Yes       | No          | No               |
   | N = {1, 2, 3, ...} | Yes       | No          | No               |
   | Unit disk D^2       | Yes       | Yes         | Yes              |
   | Sphere S^2         | Yes       | Yes         | Yes              |
   | R^n                | Yes       | No          | No               |
   | {1/n : n in N}     | No        | Yes         | No               |
   | {0}cup{1/n: n in N}| Yes       | Yes         | Yes (added 0!)   |
   +--------------------+-----------+-------------+------------------+


 Properties of compact spaces
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
| Property               | Statement                          |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
|                        |                                    |
| Image of compact       | f: X -> Y continuous, X compact    |
|                        | => f(X) is compact                 |
|                        |                                    |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
|                        |                                    |
| Weierstrass theorem    | f: X -> R continuous, X compact    |
| (Key to optimization)  | => f attains max and min           |
|                        |                                    |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
|                        |                                    |
| Closed sub compact     | X compact, F sub X closed          |
|                        | => F is compact                    |
|                        |                                    |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
|                        |                                    |
| Compact in Hausdorff   | Y Hausdorff, X sub Y compact      |
|                        | => X is closed in Y                |
|                        |                                    |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+
|                        |                                    |
| Tychonoff's theorem    | X, Y compact => X x Y compact     |
|                        | (works for any product)            |
|                        |                                    |
+------------------------+------------------------------------+

 Applications of compactness
+------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Domain           | How it is used                                 |
+------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Optimization     | Extremum exists on a compact set (Weierstrass) |
+------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Numerical methods| Convergence via subsequences                   |
+------------------+------------------------------------------------+
| Physics          | Compact phase space = closed system             |
+------------------+------------------------------------------------+

 Thermodynamics:
   Finite reservoir (compact domain) vs infinite medium.
   Properties of solutions of the heat equation depend essentially
   on the compactness of the domain.

===============================================================================
Topological invariants — how to distinguish spaces
===============================================================================

 Problem: how to prove that spaces are different?


 A homeomorphism shows that spaces are the same.
 But how to prove that no homeomorphism exists?

 Idea: Associate to a space a number or a group (an invariant).
       If the invariants differ — the spaces are certainly different.

 Invariant = a property preserved under homeomorphisms.


 Simple invariants
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
| Invariant               | Application                                       |
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|                         |                                                   |
| Number of points |X|    | |X| != |Y| => X not-iso Y (for finite)           |
|                         |                                                   |
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|                         |                                                   |
| Connectedness           | X connected, Y not => X not-iso Y                |
|                         | Example: (0,1) not-iso (0,1) cup (2,3)            |
|                         |                                                   |
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|                         |                                                   |
| Compactness             | X compact, Y not => X not-iso Y                  |
|                         | Example: [0,1] not-iso (0,1)                      |
|                         |                                                   |
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+
|                         |                                                   |
| Number of components    | pi_0(X) != pi_0(Y) => X not-iso Y                |
| pi_0                    |                                                   |
|                         |                                                   |
+-------------------------+---------------------------------------------------+

 Problem: These invariants are too coarse.
 S^1 and S^2 are both connected and compact, yet different.
 Finer invariants are needed.


 Main (fine) invariants
+---------------------+--------------------------------------+
| Invariant           | What it measures                     |
+---------------------+--------------------------------------+
| pi_1 (fund. group)  | "Group of loops" — distinguishes     |
|                     | S^1 from S^2                         |
| chi (Euler char.)   | V - E + F — the "mesh" number        |
| H_n (homology)      | "Holes" of various dimensions        |
+---------------------+--------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Fundamental group pi_1 — detailed explanation
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: Imagine tying a rope to a point and walking around the space.
 You return to the start. Can the rope be contracted to a point without
 tearing it or leaving the space?

   On the plane:             On the plane with a hole:

      +-----+                      +-----+
     /       \                    /   *   \   <- hole
    |    *    |                  |  / + \  |
    |   start |                  | |     | |
     \       /                    \ \   / /
      +-----+                      +-----+

   Can contract -> the loop       Cannot contract -> the loop
   is "trivial"                   is "nontrivial"


 Formal definition:

 A loop in X with base point x_0 is a continuous map
   gamma: [0,1] -> X,  where gamma(0) = gamma(1) = x_0

 Two loops gamma and delta are called homotopic (gamma ~ delta) if one
 can be continuously deformed into the other without tearing or releasing x_0.

 Homotopy = a family of loops H(s,t), where:
   * H(0,t) = gamma(t)  — start with gamma
   * H(1,t) = delta(t)  — end with delta
   * H(s,0) = H(s,1) = x_0  — base point is fixed

 Fundamental group:

   pi_1(X, x_0) = {loops in X from x_0} / {homotopy}
                = set of homotopy classes of loops

 Group operation:

   [gamma] . [delta] = [gamma * delta]

   where gamma * delta = "first traverse gamma, then delta":

              { gamma(2t)    if 0 <= t <= 1/2
   (gamma*delta)(t) = {
              { delta(2t-1)  if 1/2 <= t <= 1

   * Identity element: the constant loop [x_0]
   * Inverse element: [gamma]^{-1} = [gamma^{-1}], where gamma^{-1}(t)=gamma(1-t)


 Examples of fundamental groups
