+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
| Space               | pi_1              | Explanation                       |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| R^n (any n)         | {e} (trivial)     | Any loop can be contracted        |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| S^n for n >= 2      | {e} (trivial)     | On a sphere (n>=2) every loop     |
| (sphere)            |                   | contracts (no "holes")            |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| S^1 (circle)        | Z                 | Loops classified by winding       |
|                     |                   | number: ...,-2,-1,0,1,2,...       |
|                     |                   | n>0: counterclockwise             |
|                     |                   | n<0: clockwise                    |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| T^2 (torus)         | Z x Z             | Two independent loops:            |
|                     |                   | "around the hole" and             |
|                     |                   | "through the hole"                |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| R^2 \ {0}           | Z                 | Plane minus a point ~ circle      |
| (plane minus 0)     |                   | Loops = windings around the hole  |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| "Figure eight"      | F_2               | Free group on 2 generators        |
| S^1 v S^1           | (free)            | Non-commutative: ab != ba         |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+
|                     |                   |                                   |
| RP^2 (projective    | Z/2               | There is a non-contractible loop, |
| plane)              |                   | but traversed twice it contracts  |
|                     |                   |                                   |
+---------------------+-------------------+-----------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why pi_1(S^1) = Z — detailed explanation
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Circle S^1 = {e^{itheta} : theta in [0, 2pi)} sub C
 Base point: x_0 = 1 (at theta = 0)

 Loop with n windings: gamma_n(t) = e^{2pi*i*n*t}, t in [0,1]

   n = 0:  gamma_0(t) = 1 (standing still)
   n = 1:  gamma_1(t) = e^{2pi*i*t} (one counterclockwise turn)
   n = -1: gamma_{-1}(t) = e^{-2pi*i*t} (one clockwise turn)
   n = 2:  gamma_2(t) = e^{4pi*i*t} (two counterclockwise turns)

 Theorem: gamma_n ~ gamma_m  iff  n = m

 Intuitively: one cannot "unwind" a turn without breaking the loop.

 Group operation: [gamma_n] . [gamma_m] = [gamma_{n+m}]

 This is precisely the group (Z, +)!

 Consequence: S^1 not-iso S^2 (sphere)
   pi_1(S^1) = Z != {e} = pi_1(S^2)
   Different groups => spaces are not homeomorphic.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Applications of the fundamental group
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 1. Topology: Distinguishing spaces
    pi_1(X) != pi_1(Y) => X not-iso Y

 2. Borsuk-Ulam theorem:
    On the Earth's surface there are two antipodal points with the same
    temperature and pressure. (Follows from pi_1(RP^2) = Z/2)

 3. Knot theory:
    pi_1(R^3 \ knot) distinguishes knots (which knots can be untied?)

 4. Physics:
    * Defects in crystals are classified by pi_1
    * Vortices in superfluid helium: pi_1(parameter space) = Z
    * Magnetic monopoles: pi_2(parameter space)

 5. Coverings:
    Covering spaces <-> subgroups of pi_1
    Universal cover <-> pi_1 = {e}

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Higher homotopy groups pi_n
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 pi_1 measures "holes for loops." What if we use spheres?

   pi_1(X) — classes of maps S^1 -> X   (loops)
   pi_2(X) — classes of maps S^2 -> X   (spheres)
   pi_n(X) — classes of maps S^n -> X   (n-spheres)

 Examples:
 +------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Group      | Meaning                                |
 +------------+----------------------------------------+
 | pi_1(S^1) = Z | A loop can wind around the circle n times |
 | pi_1(S^2) = 0 | On a sphere any loop contracts      |
 | pi_2(S^2) = Z | S^2 can "wrap" S^2 an integer number of times |
 | pi_3(S^2) = Z | Hopf fibration (unexpectedly != 0)  |
 | pi_n(S^n) = Z | Identity map generates              |
 +------------+----------------------------------------+

 Deep fact: pi_3(S^2) = Z != 0

   This means a 3-sphere can be nontrivially mapped onto a 2-sphere.
   This map is called the Hopf fibration: S^3 -> S^2 with fiber S^1.
   Each point of S^2 is a circle in S^3.

     S^3 = union (of circles),  indexed by points of S^2

   These circles are linked to each other. Every pair — like chain links.
   Therefore the map cannot be "untangled" to a constant.

   Physics:
   * pi_2 — classification of magnetic monopoles
   * pi_3 — classification of instantons
   * Hopf fibration — related to electron spin

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Homology — the algebra of "holes" in a space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Intuition: Homology answers "what holes are there in the space?"

   H_0 counts connected components (how many separate pieces)
   H_1 counts tunnels (holes through which a rope can be threaded)
   H_2 counts cavities (enclosed voids, like inside a ball)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Key idea: cycle vs boundary
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Cycle = a closed contour (start coincides with end)
 Boundary = a contour that bounds some region

 Example on a plane with a hole:

        +-----------------+          +-----------------+
        |    +---+        |          |                 |
        |   /     \   g   |          |    +---+        |
        |  |   *   |<-----|          |   /     \   d   |
        |   \     /       |          |  |       |<-----|
        |    +---+        |          |   \     /       |
        +-----------------+          |    +---+        |
        g goes around hole *         +-----------------+
                                     d does not go around hole

   g is a cycle but not a boundary  d is a cycle and a boundary
   (cannot "fill" — the hole is there) (can be filled with a disk)

   H_1 = {cycles} / {boundaries} = "cycles that cannot be filled"

   For the plane with a hole: H_1 = Z (one generator — going around)
   For the ordinary plane: H_1 = 0 (all cycles can be filled)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Construction: from gluing to algebra
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The idea of homology is to translate geometry into linear algebra.
 The space is cut into simple pieces, the pieces are written as formal
 sums, and the question "is there a hole?" becomes "is a system of
 linear equations solvable?"

 Step 1: Cutting into simplices

 A simplex is the minimal "brick" of each dimension:

   * 0-simplex = point          *
   * 1-simplex = segment        *---*
   * 2-simplex = triangle       triangle
   * 3-simplex = tetrahedron    tetrahedron

 Any "decent" space can be cut into simplices glued along entire faces.
 The result is a simplicial complex.

 Step 2: Orientation — where the signs come from

 For the algebra to work, simplices must be oriented:

   * Point: sign + or -
   * Segment: a chosen direction (start -> end)
   * Triangle: direction of traversal (clockwise or counterclockwise)
   * Tetrahedron: consistent orientation of faces

 Orientation is not decoration but necessity. Without it, signs cannot
 be defined and the entire construction falls apart. Reversing orientation
 is equivalent to multiplying by -1.

 Key property: when gluing two oriented triangles along a shared edge,
 this edge enters each triangle with opposite orientations — and cancels
 in the sum. All internal edges vanish; only the outer boundary remains.

 Step 3: Chains — formal sums

 A chain of dimension k is a formal sum of oriented k-simplices
 with integer coefficients:

   c = n_1*sigma_1 + n_2*sigma_2 + ... + n_m*sigma_m,  n_i in Z

 Coefficient n_i = -1 means the same simplex with reversed orientation.
 The set of all k-chains is denoted C_k. It is an abelian group.

 Step 4: Boundary operator d

 The operator d takes the oriented boundary:

   d(segment AB) = B - A  (end minus start)

   d(triangle ABC) = BC + CA + AB  (traversal along the boundary)

           A
          / \           d(triangle) = AB + BC + CA
         /   \          (a closed contour)
        B-----C

   d(tetrahedron ABCD) = BCD - ACD + ABD - ABC  (four faces with signs)

 Properties of d:
   * Linearity: d(c_1 + c_2) = d(c_1) + d(c_2)
   * Dimension lowering: d: C_k -> C_{k-1}
   * dd = 0 (the boundary of a boundary is empty)

 The last is not magic but a consequence of orientation. Check:

   d(d(triangle ABC)) = d(AB + BC + CA)
                      = (B-A) + (C-B) + (A-C)
                      = 0  ok

 Each vertex appears exactly twice: as the start of one edge and the end
 of another. Signs are opposite — everything cancels.
 This works in any dimension: d_{k-1} . d_k = 0.

 Step 5: Chain complex and homology

 We have a sequence of groups and operators:

   ... -> C_{k+1} ->d_{k+1} C_k ->d_k C_{k-1} -> ...

 From dd = 0 it follows: Im(d_{k+1}) sub ker(d_k). Every boundary is a cycle.
 Question: is the converse true? Is every cycle a boundary?

 If yes — no holes. If no — the "extra" cycles are holes.

   Z_k = ker(d_k)    — cycles (chains without boundary)
   B_k = Im(d_{k+1}) — boundaries (chains that are someone's boundary)

   H_k = Z_k / B_k   — the k-th homology group

 H_k measures the gap between "being a cycle" and "being a boundary."
 If H_k = 0 — all k-cycles are boundaries (no k-holes).
 If H_k != 0 — there are cycles that cannot be filled.

 Connection to linear algebra: d_k is a matrix (huge, sparse, almost all
 entries 0, the rest +/-1). Cycles = kernel of matrix. Boundaries = image
 of another matrix. Homology = kernel/image. The entire problem reduces to
 linear algebra, even though the matrices are gigantic.

 Example: torus T^2

   H_1(T^2) = Z + Z — two independent generators:
   [meridian] and [parallel]. Any cycle on the torus is homologous to
   n*[meridian] + m*[parallel] for integers n, m.

   The meridian is a cycle (closed) but not a boundary: it cannot be
   "filled" with a disk while staying on the torus surface.

 Examples:
 +-----------------+----+-----+----+------------------+
 | Space           | H_0| H_1 | H_2| Interpretation   |
 +-----------------+----+-----+----+------------------+
 | Point           | Z  | 0   | 0  | 1 piece, no holes|
 | Circle S^1      | Z  | Z   | 0  | 1 tunnel         |
 | Sphere S^2      | Z  | 0   | Z  | 1 cavity         |
 | Torus T^2       | Z  | Z+Z | Z  | 2 tunnels,1 cav. |
 | Solid torus     | Z  | Z   | 0  | 1 tunnel         |
 +-----------------+----+-----+----+------------------+

 Connection to Euler characteristic:

   chi = rank(H_0) - rank(H_1) + rank(H_2) - ...

   For the sphere: chi = 1 - 0 + 1 = 2  ok
   For the torus:  chi = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0  ok

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Cohomology — the dual view through integration
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Homology: "what closed surfaces exist in the space?"
 Cohomology: "what forms can be integrated over these surfaces?"

 Intuition:

   Let there be a 1-form omega (like df = "gradient").
   The integral int_gamma omega depends only on the class of gamma in H_1.

   If gamma_1 ~ gamma_2 (homologous), then int_{gamma_1} omega =
   int_{gamma_2} omega.

 Closed vs exact form:

   Closed form: d(omega) = 0
   Exact form: omega = df for some function f

   Exact => closed (because d^2 = 0)
   But not conversely. On spaces with holes there are closed but
   not exact forms.

 Example — dtheta on the circle:

   The form dtheta on S^1 is closed (d(dtheta) = 0).
   But int_{S^1} dtheta = 2pi != 0.

   If dtheta = df, then int_{S^1} dtheta = f(end) - f(start) = 0.
   Contradiction. So dtheta is not exact on S^1.

 De Rham cohomology:

   H^k_dR(M) = {closed k-forms} / {exact k-forms}
             = "forms that cannot be represented as df"

 Pairing (de Rham theorem):

   <omega, gamma> = int_gamma omega  — integral of form over cycle

   This gives an isomorphism: H^k_dR(M) iso H_k(M; R)*

   Cohomology = "linear functionals on homology"
   (duality in action)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 CW-complexes — a constructor for spaces
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Idea: Build spaces from simple pieces — cells (disks).
       Like LEGO: start with points, attach segments, then disks, etc.

 Cells:
   0-cell = point              *
   1-cell = open interval      *-------*  (endpoints are 0-cells)
   2-cell = open disk          O  (boundary consists of 1-cells)
   n-cell = open n-disk        (boundary consists of (n-1)-cells)

 "Attaching" — what it means:

   Take an n-disk D^n and map its boundary dD^n = S^{n-1}
   onto the already-built skeleton X^{(n-1)}.

   New space = X^{(n-1)} cup_f D^n  (gluing via f: S^{n-1} -> X^{(n-1)})

 Example: how to build the sphere S^2

   Method 1 (minimal):

     Step 0: One 0-cell (point) *
     Step 2: Attach a 2-cell, entire boundary goes to that point

             *  ---------->   O
          (point)         (entire boundary   (result = S^2)
                           collapsed
                           to point)

     S^2 = 1 zero-cell + 1 two-cell

 Example: how to build the torus T^2

     Torus = square with opposite sides glued:

        a --------> a              After gluing:
        |           |                    +-------+
      b |           | b                 /         \
        |           |                  |  +-----+  |
        v           v                  |  |     |  |
        a --------> a                   \ +-----+ /
                                         +--------+

     T^2 = 1 point + 2 loops (a and b) + 1 square
         = 1*(0-cell) + 2*(1-cell) + 1*(2-cell)

 Why CW-complexes:

   1. Easy to compute homology: H_n depends only on n-cells and (n+/-1)-cells
   2. Euler characteristic: chi = (# 0-cells) - (# 1-cells) + (# 2-cells)
   3. Many spaces have simple cell structures

   For S^2: chi = 1 - 0 + 1 = 2  ok
   For T^2: chi = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0  ok

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Homotopy groups of spheres — why this is hard and interesting
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Question: In how many essentially different ways can one sphere
           be mapped onto another?

 pi_n(S^m) = classes of maps S^n -> S^m (up to deformation)

 Simple cases (intuition works):

   pi_1(S^1) = Z:  A loop on the circle can be wound 0, 1, 2, ... times.
                   The winding number is an integer.

   pi_1(S^2) = 0:  Any loop on the sphere can be contracted
                   (no "hole" to catch on).

   pi_n(S^n) = Z:  A sphere can "wrap" itself an integer number of times.
                   (Degree of the map)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Surprise: pi_3(S^2) = Z != 0
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   One might think: the 3-sphere is "bigger" than the 2-sphere. Any map
   S^3 -> S^2 should compress the extra dimension; everything should contract.

   But no. There is a nontrivial map — the Hopf fibration:

     h: S^3 -> S^2,  where the preimage of each point is a circle S^1

   Imagine: S^3 is "ruled" by non-intersecting circles, and each circle
   corresponds to a point on S^2.

     S^3 = union (of circles),  indexed by points of S^2

   These circles are linked to each other. Every pair — like chain links.
   Therefore the map cannot be "unraveled" to a constant.

   Physics: The Hopf fibration describes the topology of electron spin.

 Table of groups pi_{n+k}(S^n):
 +-----+---+----+------+--------+-----+-----+
 | k\n | 1 | 2  | 3    | 4      | 5   | inf |
 +-----+---+----+------+--------+-----+-----+
 | 0   | Z | Z  | Z    | Z      | Z   | Z   |
 | 1   | 0 | Z  | Z_2  | Z_2    | Z_2 | Z_2 |
 | 2   | 0 |Z_2 | Z_2  | Z_2    | Z_2 | Z_2 |
 | 3   | 0 |Z_2 | Z_12 | Z+Z_12 |Z_24 | Z_24|
 +-----+---+----+------+--------+-----+-----+

   Column inf — stable groups: for n >= k+2 the answer is unchanged.

 Why such strange numbers (Z_12, Z_24)?

   This is a deep result. Where does 24 come from? Connections to:
   * 24 = dimension of the Leech lattice (coding theory)
   * 24 divisors of modular forms (number theory)
   * 24 in Riemann's formula zeta(-1) = -1/12 (via 2*12 = 24)

   Homotopy groups of spheres are a window into deep connections
   across different areas of mathematics.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Stabilization and suspension — why the table "settles down"
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Suspension Sigma: take a space X and "suspend" it:

   Sigma X = (X x [0,1]) / (X x {0} ~ point, X x {1} ~ point)
           = "two cones glued along X"

   Example: Sigma S^0 = S^1  (two cones over two points = circle)
            Sigma S^1 = S^2  (two cones over circle = sphere)
            Sigma S^n = S^{n+1}

 Freudenthal's theorem:

   Suspension induces a homomorphism: Sigma: pi_k(S^n) -> pi_{k+1}(S^{n+1})

   For n >= k+2 this homomorphism is an isomorphism.

   Therefore in the table: pi_3(S^2) -> pi_4(S^3) -> pi_5(S^4) -> ...
   starting with pi_4(S^3), the group stabilizes = Z_2.

 Stable groups pi_k^s:

   pi_k^s = lim pi_{n+k}(S^n)  as n -> inf

   These are the "true" invariants, cleansed of dimensional effects.
   Computing pi_k^s is one of the main tasks of algebraic topology.

 Tools for computation:

   * Hurewicz theorem: connects pi_n and H_n for "nice" spaces
   * Exact sequences: connect pi for fibrations F -> E -> B
   * Spectral sequences: systematic computation method
   * Eilenberg-MacLane spaces K(G,n): standard building blocks

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Euler characteristic — a number determining the shape
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Definition (for polyhedra):

   chi = V - E + F

   V = number of vertices
   E = number of edges
   F = number of faces

 Examples:
 +-----------+----+----+----+---+
 | Figure    | V  | E  | F  |chi|
 +-----------+----+----+----+---+
 | Tetrahedron| 4 | 6  | 4  | 2 |
 | Cube      | 8  | 12 | 6  | 2 |
 | Octahedron| 6  | 12 | 8  | 2 |
 | Dodecahedr| 20 | 30 | 12 | 2 |
 | Icosahedron| 12| 30 | 20 | 2 |
 +-----------+----+----+----+---+

 Euler's theorem: For any convex polyhedron, chi = 2.

 Moreover: chi is a topological invariant. It depends only on the "shape"
 of the surface, not on the partition into faces.

 Euler characteristic of surfaces:
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Surface               |  chi  | Explanation                           |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Sphere S^2            |  2    | "Ball": no holes                      |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Torus T^2 (doughnut)  |  0    | One "hole"                            |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Double torus          | -2    | Two "holes"                           |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | g-torus (genus g)     | 2-2g  | g "holes"                             |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Projective plane RP^2 |  1    | Non-orientable                        |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+
 | Klein bottle          |  0    | Non-orientable, chi=0 like the torus, |
 |                       |       | but different pi_1                    |
 +-----------------------+-------+---------------------------------------+

 Formula: chi = 2 - 2g (for orientable surfaces of genus g)

 What is genus g?
   Genus = "number of holes" in the surface.
   Sphere: g=0 (no holes). Torus: g=1 (one hole, like a doughnut).
   A g-torus is obtained from a sphere by "attaching g handles."
   Formally: g = (2 - chi)/2 for orientable closed surfaces.

 Connection to other invariants:

   chi = sum (-1)^n * rank(H_n)     (via homology groups)

 Applications:

   * Hairy ball theorem: on S^2 (chi=2) one cannot comb a hedgehog
     (a vector field must have zeros)
   * On the torus (chi=0) combing is possible.
   * Gauss-Bonnet formula: int int K dA = 2*pi*chi
     (link between curvature and topology)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Classification of compact surfaces
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Connected sum M # N (operation on surfaces):
   1. Cut a small disk from M and N
   2. Glue M and N along the boundaries of those disks
   Result: a new surface. Example: torus = S^2 # (handle).

 Theorem (classification):
 Every connected compact surface is homeomorphic to exactly one of:

   Orientable:
   * Sphere S^2 (genus 0)
   * Torus T^2 (genus 1)
   * Double torus (genus 2)
   * ... g-fold torus (genus g)

   Non-orientable:
   * Projective plane RP^2
   * Klein bottle
   * ... (connected sums with RP^2)

 Orientability — can "left" and "right" be distinguished?

   On a sphere or torus: Yes — going around, right stays right.

   On a Moebius strip: No — going around, right becomes left.

       Moebius strip:

       Take a strip of paper,       +------------------+
       twist by 180 degrees and     |   ->  ->  ->  -> | <- one side
       glue the ends.               +------------------+
                                         | glue with twist
       Result: a surface             +------------------+
       with one side.                |   <-  ->  ->  <- | <- same side
                                     +------------------+

   Klein bottle = Moebius strip "sealed" into a tube
   (cannot be embedded in R^3 without self-intersection)

 Invariants distinguishing surfaces:
 +----------------+---+--------+---------------+
 | Surface        |chi| pi_1   | Orientable?   |
 +----------------+---+--------+---------------+
 | Sphere S^2     | 2 | {e}    | Yes           |
 | Torus T^2      | 0 | Z x Z  | Yes           |
 | Klein bottle   | 0 | other  | No            |
 | RP^2           | 1 | Z/2    | No            |
 +----------------+---+--------+---------------+

 Important: chi and pi_1 together completely classify compact surfaces.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Table of invariants — how to tell spaces apart
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 The idea of an invariant
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: how to prove that two spaces are different?
   Doughnut != sphere — this is "visible," but a rigorous argument is needed.

 Solution: associate to a space a number or group.
   If the numbers differ -> the spaces are certainly different.

 Invariant = something that does not change under deformation
 (without tearing or gluing).


 Main invariants with explanations
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
| Invariant         | What it means in simple words                           |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| pi_0 (connected.) | How many pieces? Whether the object breaks into parts.  |
|                   | pi_0(* *) = 2 (two points), pi_0(O) = 1 (one circle)   |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| pi_1 (fund. grp.) | Which loops cannot be contracted to a point?            |
|                   | On a plane — all loops contract: pi_1 = 0               |
|                   | On a circle — the loop around doesn't contract: pi_1=Z  |
|                   | On a doughnut — two independent loops: pi_1 = Z x Z     |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| chi (Euler)       | The formula V - E + F for a polygonal partition.        |
|                   | Sphere: chi = 2 (any partition)                         |
|                   | Doughnut: chi = 0                                       |
|                   | Surface with g holes: chi = 2 - 2g                      |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| Orientability     | Are there "two sides"?                                  |
|                   | Sphere, doughnut — YES (can paint in two colors)        |
|                   | Moebius strip, Klein bottle — NO (one side)             |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| Dimension         | How many numbers are needed to specify a point?         |
|                   | Line: dim=1, plane: dim=2, space: dim=3                 |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+
|                   |                                                         |
| Compactness       | Bounded and closed?                                     |
|                   | Sphere — compact (finite), R — not (infinite)           |
|                   |                                                         |
+-------------------+---------------------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 How to use
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Invariant differs  ->  spaces are certainly different.
   chi(sphere) = 2 != 0 = chi(doughnut)  ->  sphere not-iso doughnut ok

 Invariants coincide  ->  not yet certain they are the same.
   Must check other invariants or construct an explicit isomorphism.

 Example: How to prove that mug = doughnut?
   chi = 0 for both, pi_1 = Z for both, both orientable.
   But this is not a proof. One must construct the deformation.


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Summary: central concepts of topology
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Hierarchy of concepts
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Set X
       |
       | + topology tau (family of "open" sets)
       v
   Topological space (X, tau)
       |
       +---> Open/closed sets
       |     Boundary, interior, closure
       |
       +---> Continuous maps f: X -> Y
       |     (preimage of open is open)
       |
       +---> Homeomorphism (topological equivalence)
       |     (continuous bijection with continuous inverse)
       |
       +---> Connectedness (cannot split into two open sets)
       |
       +---> Compactness (finite subcovers)
       |
       +---> Invariants (pi_1, H_n, chi, ...)
             for distinguishing spaces

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connection to other structures
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

                    Topology (closeness)
                         |
            + metric     |
            (distance)   |
                         v
                Metric space
                         |
       + linear structure|
         + norm (||x||,  |
       d(x,y) = ||x-y||)v
              Normed space
                         |
          + completeness |
        (all limits      |
         exist)          v
               Banach space
                         |
        + inner product  |
        (||x||^2=<x,x>) v
               Hilbert space

 Each level inherits the topology from the previous one
 but adds additional structure.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Practical consequences
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 For the engineer / applied scientist:

 * Continuity of functions — key to numerical methods
   (close inputs give close outputs = stability)

 * Compactness — guarantees existence of solutions
   (extrema exist, sequences converge)

 * Connectedness — "integrity" of a system
   (can one transition from one state to another?)

 * Topological invariants — the "shape" of the parameter space
   (how many "holes" are in the admissible region?)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Full nesting scheme of spaces
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Each level adds structure and restricts the class of spaces:

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                         |
 |   SET                                                                   |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + topology tau                                                  |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   TOPOLOGICAL SPACE                                                     |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + axiom T_2 (Hausdorff)                                         |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   Hausdorff space (uniqueness of limits)                                |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + metrizability (topology generated by a metric)                |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   Metric space (notion of distance)                                     |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + completeness (all Cauchy sequences converge)                  |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   COMPLETE METRIC SPACE                                                 |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + linear structure + norm                                       |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   Banach space (complete normed)                                        |
 |       |                                                                 |
 |       | + norm from inner product                                       |
 |       v                                                                 |
 |   Hilbert space (complete with inner product)                           |
 |                                                                         |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Examples at each level:
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Level              | Examples                               |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Topological        | R^n, any set with any topology          |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Hausdorff          | R^n, manifolds, but not indiscrete      |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Metric             | R^n, C[0,1], l^p, anything with metric |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Complete metric    | R^n, C[0,1], l^p, but not Q, not (0,1) |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Banach             | R^n, C[0,1], l^p (p>=1), L^p           |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+
 | Hilbert            | R^n, L^2, l^2, but not C[0,1], not l^1 |
 +--------------------+----------------------------------------+

 Note: L^p is the space of functions with finite int |f|^p. The full
 definition requires the Lebesgue integral. In R^n, Riemann suffices.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 What is a norm
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 A norm ||.|| on a vector space V is a "vector length":

   * ||x|| >= 0, and ||x|| = 0 iff x = 0
   * ||alpha*x|| = |alpha|*||x||      (scaling)
   * ||x + y|| <= ||x|| + ||y||       (triangle inequality)

 A norm generates a metric: d(x,y) = ||x - y||
 Examples: ||x||_2 = sqrt(sum x_i^2) in R^n,  ||f||_2 = sqrt(int |f|^2) in L^2

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Metrizability — how to prove a space is (not) metrizable
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Definition:
   A topological space (X, tau) is called metrizable if there exists
   a metric d on X generating the topology tau.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Necessary conditions for metrizability (if violated — not metrizable)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 1. Hausdorff (T_2):
    A metric space is always T_2.
    => If the space is not Hausdorff — it is not metrizable.

 2. First countability:
    In a metric space every point has a countable neighborhood base
    (balls of radius 1/n).
    => If there is no countable neighborhood base — not metrizable.

 3. Sequentiality:
    In a metric space, closedness is equivalent to sequential closedness.
    => If there is a sequentially closed but not closed set — not metrizable.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Sufficient conditions (metrizability theorems)
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Two countability axioms:

   * First (A_1): every point has a countable neighborhood base

   * Second (A_2): the entire topology has a countable base
     (any open set = union of elements from a countable family)

   A_2 => A_1, but not conversely.

 Urysohn's theorem:
   Regular (T_3) + Second countability => metrizable.

 Nagata-Smirnov theorem:
   T_3 + sigma-locally finite base => metrizable.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Strategies for proving non-metrizability
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Strategy 1: Violation of T_2
   Show that two points cannot be separated by disjoint neighborhoods.

 Strategy 2: No countable neighborhood base
   Find a point where no countable system of neighborhoods is a base.

 Strategy 3: Sequential argument
   Find a set that is sequentially closed but not topologically closed.

 Strategy 4: Compactness + uncountability
   In a compact metric space every open cover has a countable subcover.
   => If compact but no countable subcover — not metrizable.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Classical examples
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | Space                    | Metrizable? | Why                           |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | R^n (standard topology)  | YES         | Euclidean metric              |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | R (Zariski topology)     | NO          | Not T_2: closed = finite sets |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | R (Sorgenfrey line)      | NO          | Separable but no countable    |
 |                          |             | base (T_2 but not 2nd count.) |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | [0,1]^[0,1] (Tychonoff)  | NO          | No countable neighborhood base|
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | beta*N (Stone-Cech)      | NO          | Uncountable discrete subspace |
 |                          |             | in a compact set              |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+
 | omega_1 (first uncountable| NO         | Sequentially compact          |
 | ordinal)                 |             | but not compact               |
 +--------------------------+-------------+-------------------------------+

 Step-by-step example: Prove that [0,1]^R is not metrizable

   1. This is an uncountable product of compacts => compact (Tychonoff).
   2. Assume it is metrizable.
   3. Then it has a countable neighborhood base at each point.
   4. Consider x = (0, 0, 0, ...) — zero in all coordinates.
   5. A basic neighborhood depends on finitely many coordinates.
   6. Countable base => involves a countable set of coordinates.
   7. But there are uncountably many coordinates => contradiction.

 Conclusion: [0,1]^R is not metrizable because it has no countable
 neighborhood base.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Applied example: connectedness of a pipeline network
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: A city heating network. Need to understand what happens in an accident.

            Boiler house
               *===========*===========* District A
               |           |           |
               |           |           |
               *===========*===========* District B
               |           |           |
               |           |           |
               *===========*===========* District C

 Topological viewpoint:

 1. Connectedness:
    The network is connected = from any point one can reach any other.
    If an accident cuts the network into two parts -> the network
    becomes disconnected.

 2. Number of independent cycles = first Betti number beta_1:

    beta_1 = E - V + 1     (for a connected graph)
    E = number of edges (pipelines)
    V = number of vertices (nodes)

    For our network: V = 9, E = 12  =>  beta_1 = 12 - 9 + 1 = 4

    Meaning: 4 pipes can be shut off without losing connectivity
    (with the right choice).

 3. Practical conclusion:

    * beta_1 = 0: tree network, any accident disconnects some consumers
    * beta_1 > 0: ring network, backup paths exist
    * The larger beta_1, the more reliable the network (but costlier)


-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Topological equivalence in heat exchangers:
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Two heat exchangers with the same flow topology work the same way.

   Tube-in-tube:      ===============     Counterflow: efficiency ~100%
                      ---------------

   Plate:             +===+ +===+        Counterflow with mixing
                      |   | |   |
                      +===+ +===+        efficiency ~95%

   Mixing:            liquids             Co-current flow (topologically
                      mix together        different) — efficiency ~50%

 The flow topology (counterflow/co-current/crossflow) determines
 the theoretical efficiency limit of heat exchange.


Topology gives the notion of closeness but does not say how to measure
and compute. To work with a space quantitatively — add vectors, solve
equations, find projections — additional structure is needed.

Linear algebra adds to a space the operations of addition and scalar
multiplication. This makes computation possible — and this is the
language of nearly all of physics.


===============================================================================
Linear algebra — the language of linear transformations
===============================================================================

Linear algebra adds flatness to a space. Now the space is not merely
"connected" (topology) — it is flat, without curvature. One can draw a
straight line, add two vectors, stretch a vector by a factor of two.

In "object-observer" terms, linear algebra is where the observer first
truly appears.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
A basis is the observer's choice. Coordinates are its language.
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

One and the same vector v exists independently of the basis. But to
record it as numbers (3, 4, 5), one must choose a basis. Another observer
in a different basis writes the same vector as (1, 2, 6). The vector has
not changed — the notation has.

The change-of-basis matrix is a dictionary for translating between the
languages of two observers. The formula A' = P^{-1}AP says: "here is
how the same operator A looks through the eyes of the new observer."

What is invariant (does not depend on the observer):
  * Rank of the matrix — how many independent directions it engages
  * Determinant — by what factor the volume changes
  * Trace — the sum of eigenvalues
  * Characteristic polynomial — the "DNA" of the operator

These invariants are genuine properties of the object. The specific
numbers in the matrix are merely a notation depending on the basis.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Linear algebra as a viewpoint on space
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Linear algebra sees in a space two operations:
   * Addition of vectors (points can be added)
   * Scalar multiplication (vectors can be stretched/compressed)

 This is more structure than topology (only closeness),
 but less than a metric (specific distances).

 Key fact: Almost all spaces in physics and engineering are vector spaces.
   * Forces can be added
   * Velocities can be added
   * Quantum-mechanical states can be added

Linear algebra is the second most important structure after sets. It describes
everything that can be "added" and "stretched": vectors, functions, matrices.

Why this is the central topic:
  * Quantum mechanics lives in vector spaces
  * Nonlinear problems are linearized (the derivative)
  * Data are vectors in R^n
  * Linear systems are solved explicitly

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Place in the overall picture
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Linear algebra is the study of vector spaces and linear maps between them.

   +--------------------------------+
   | Hierarchy of transformations   |
   +--------------------------------+
   |                                |
   | Arbitrary maps                 |
   | v  preserve continuity         |
   | Continuous (topology)          |
   | v  preserve smoothness         |
   | Smooth (analysis, manifolds)   |
   | v  preserve linear structure   |
   | Linear  <- we are here         |
   | v  preserve angles and lengths |
   | Orthogonal (group O(n))        |
   | v  preserve orientation        |
   | Rotations (group SO(n))        |
   |                                |
   +--------------------------------+


 Vector space — formal definition

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Vector space                                          |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                   |
 | A vector space over a field F is a set V with two operations:     |
 | addition (+) and scalar multiplication (.), such that:            |
 |                                                                   |
 | Addition axioms (V is an abelian group under +):                  |
 | (V1) u + v = v + u                    (commutativity)             |
 | (V2) (u + v) + w = u + (v + w)        (associativity)             |
 | (V3) exists 0 in V: v + 0 = v         (identity element)         |
 | (V4) forall v exists (-v): v + (-v) = 0 (inverse element)        |
 |                                                                   |
 | Scalar multiplication axioms:                                     |
 | (V5) alpha(beta*v) = (alpha*beta)v    (associativity)             |
 | (V6) 1.v = v                          (field identity)            |
 |                                                                   |
 | Distributivity axioms:                                            |
 | (V7) alpha(u + v) = alpha*u + alpha*v (over vectors)              |
 | (V8) (alpha + beta)v = alpha*v + beta*v (over scalars)            |
 |                                                                   |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Characteristic property: A vector space admits:
   * Adding objects (parallelogram rule)
   * Stretching/compressing objects (scalar multiplication)
   * and these operations behave "well" (consistently)


 Examples of vector spaces
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
| Space           | Elements                    | Dimension     | Comment     |
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
|                 |                             |               |             |
| R^n             | (x_1, x_2,...,x_n), x_i in R| n             | Standard    |
|                 |                             |               | example     |
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
|                 |                             |               |             |
| C([a,b])        | Continuous functions f: R->R | inf           | Functions   |
|                 | (f+g)(x) = f(x)+g(x)       |               | are vectors |
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
|                 |                             |               |             |
| R[x]_{<=n}      | Polynomials of degree <= n  | n + 1         | Basis:      |
|                 | a_0 + a_1*x +...+ a_n*x^n  |               | 1, x, x^2,..|
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
|                 |                             |               |             |
| M_{mxn}(R)      | Matrices of size m x n      | m*n           | Basis: E_ij |
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+
|                 |                             |               |             |
| C^n             | (z_1,...,z_n), z_i in C      | n (over C)    | Or 2n over R|
+-----------------+-----------------------------+---------------+-------------+

 Counterexample: What is not a vector space

 +-----------------------+-----------------------------------------------+
 | {(x,y) in R^2 : x>=0} | (1,0) in V, but (-1)*(1,0) = (-1,0) not in V |
 | (right half-plane)     | Not closed under scalar multiplication.       |
 +-----------------------+-----------------------------------------------+


 Linear independence and basis

 +----------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Linear combination                           |
 +----------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                          |
 | A linear combination of vectors v_1,...,v_k is           |
 |                                                          |
 | alpha_1*v_1 + alpha_2*v_2 +...+ alpha_k*v_k,            |
 |   where alpha_i in F (scalars)                           |
 +----------------------------------------------------------+

 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Linear independence                                  |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                  |
 | Vectors v_1,...,v_k are linearly independent if from              |
 |                                                                  |
 | alpha_1*v_1 + alpha_2*v_2 +...+ alpha_k*v_k = 0                 |
 |                                                                  |
 | it follows that alpha_1 = alpha_2 = ... = alpha_k = 0.           |
 |                                                                  |
 | In other words: the only way to get 0 is to take all alpha_i = 0.|
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Basis                                                 |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                   |
 | A basis of V is a set {e_1,...,e_n} that:                         |
 |                                                                   |
 | (1) Is linearly independent                                      |
 | (2) Spans V (any v in V can be written as v = sum alpha_i*e_i)   |
 |                                                                   |
 | Equivalently: a basis is a minimal spanning set,                  |
 | or a maximal linearly independent set.                            |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+

 +--------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Dimension theorem                                                  |
 +--------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                    |
 | All bases of a given space contain the same number of vectors.     |
 | This number is called the dimension of the space: dim(V).          |
 +--------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Two types of bases in infinite-dimensional spaces

 +---------------+-----------------------------+------------------------+
 | Type          | Definition                  | Example                |
 +---------------+-----------------------------+------------------------+
 |               |                             |                        |
 | Hamel basis   | v = sum alpha_i*e_i         | For C[0,1] such a     |
 | (algebraic)   | (finite sum)                | basis is uncountable.  |
 |               | Every vector is a finite    | Cannot be explicitly   |
 |               | linear combination          | constructed.           |
 +---------------+-----------------------------+------------------------+
 |               |                             |                        |
 | Schauder basis| v = sum alpha_i*e_i         | {1, x, x^2,...} for   |
 | (topological) | (infinite convergent series)| analytic functions     |
 |               | Requires a topology.        | {sin nx, cos nx} — L^2|
 +---------------+-----------------------------+------------------------+


 Linear map — preserving the structure

 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Linear map                                           |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                  |
 | A map T: V -> W is called linear if:                             |
 |                                                                  |
 | (L1) T(u + v) = T(u) + T(v)    (preserves addition)             |
 | (L2) T(alpha*v) = alpha*T(v)   (preserves scalar multiplication) |
 |                                                                  |
 | Equivalently (in one formula):                                   |
 |                                                                  |
 | T(alpha*u + beta*v) = alpha*T(u) + beta*T(v)                     |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Key fact: T is completely determined by its values on a basis.
   If we know T(e_1),...,T(e_n), then T(v) = T(sum alpha_i*e_i) =
   sum alpha_i*T(e_i)


 Matrix — a linear map in coordinates

 +=================================================================+
 | The main mistake engineers make: a matrix is not a tensor.      |
 +=================================================================+
 |                                                                 |
 | A matrix is a table of numbers, a coordinate record of          |
 | something in a basis. The same 3x3 matrix can represent:        |
 |                                                                 |
 | * Linear operator T: V -> V    (type (1,1) tensor)              |
 | * Bilinear form B: V x V -> R  (type (0,2) tensor)              |
 | * Quadratic form Q(v)          (type (0,2) tensor)              |
 | * Just a set of coefficients   (not a tensor at all)            |
 |                                                                 |
 | without specifying what it is — a matrix is meaningless under   |
 | change of basis. Different objects transform by different laws:  |
 |                                                                 |
 | A' = P^{-1}AP  (operator)  vs  A' = P^T A P  (form)            |
 |                                                                 |
 | Confusion here is the source of half the errors in mechanics.   |
 +=================================================================+

 Key idea: Matrix = record of T in chosen bases.
           Changed the basis -> the matrix changed, but T is the same.

 Construction: T: V -> W, basis {e_j} in V, basis {f_i} in W
   T(e_j) = sum_i a_{ij} f_i  ->  j-th column of A = coordinates of T(e_j)

 +-------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Object            | Transformation law             |
 +-------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Linear operator   | A' = P^{-1}AP                  |
 | T: V -> V         | (matrix similarity)            |
 +-------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Bilinear form     | A' = P^T A P                   |
 | B: V x V -> R     | (matrix congruence)            |
 +-------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Metric g_{ij}     | g'_{ij} = (dx^k/dx'^i)         |
 | (type (0,2) tens.)| (dx^l/dx'^j) g_{kl}           |
 +-------------------+--------------------------------+

 Three meanings of matrix-vector multiplication w = Av:

 Meaning 1: active transformation — the vector actually changes.
   Was vector v, became a different vector w = Av.
   Physically: rotated, stretched, deformed the object itself.

 Meaning 2: passive transformation — same vector, coordinates change.
   Was vector v with coordinates (3,4) in old basis.
   Same vector has coordinates (1.5,4) in new basis.
   Physically: nothing changed, just recalculated numbers.

 Meaning 3: matrix as physical law — linking different quantities.
   This is the main application in physics and engineering.
   The matrix describes how one vector quantity generates another.

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Thermal conductivity matrix  q = -lambda . grad(T)                |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | grad(T) — temperature gradient (vector, K/m)                      |
 | q — heat flux (vector, W/m^2)                                     |
 | lambda — thermal conductivity matrix                              |
 |                                                                   |
 | In isotropic material (metal, water):                             |
 | lambda_{ij} = lambda * delta_{ij}                                 |
 | Heat flows along the gradient                                     |
 |                                                                   |
 | In anisotropic material (wood, crystal, layered insulation):      |
 | Heat can flow not along the gradient.                             |
 | Heat one side — heat escapes sideways.                            |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------+

 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Stress matrix  sigma                                          |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 | n — normal to area element (vector, direction)                |
 | F = sigma . n — force per unit area (vector, N/m^2)           |
 | sigma — 3x3 stress matrix                                    |
 |                                                               |
 | sigma says: "if the area element is directed as n,            |
 | then the force on it will be F"                               |
 | This is not one force — it is a rule for all possible areas.  |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+

 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Inertia matrix  I                                                |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | omega — angular velocity (vector)                                |
 | L = I . omega — angular momentum (vector)                        |
 | I — 3x3 inertia matrix                                          |
 |                                                                  |
 | For symmetric body (ball, cube): L || omega                      |
 | For asymmetric body (dumbbell at angle): L and omega not parallel|
 | Spin around one axis — momentum points in another direction.     |
 | (That is why wheels are balanced — removing off-diagonal I)      |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Kernel and image — the main subspaces

 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definitions                                                   |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                               |
 | For a linear T: V -> W:                                       |
 |                                                               |
 | kernel:  ker(T) = {v in V : T(v) = 0}  (what maps to zero)   |
 | image:   Im(T) = {T(v) : v in V}       (where we land)       |
 |                                                               |
 | Both are subspaces (closed under + and scalar mult.).         |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+

 +--------------------------------------------+
 | Rank-Nullity theorem                       |
 +--------------------------------------------+
 |                                            |
 | dim(V) = dim(ker T) + dim(Im T)            |
 |              nullity       rank             |
 |                                            |
 | "Dimension of domain = losses + results"   |
 +--------------------------------------------+

 Example: Projection T: R^3 -> R^3, T(x,y,z) = (x,y,0)

   ker(T) = {(0,0,z) : z in R} = z-axis         dim(ker T) = 1
   Im(T)  = {(x,y,0) : x,y in R} = xy-plane      dim(Im T) = 2

   Check: 3 = 1 + 2 ok

 Connection to systems of equations:

 +---------+-------------------------------------------+
 | Concept | Meaning                                   |
 +---------+-------------------------------------------+
 | ker(A)  | Solutions of Ax = 0 (homogeneous system)  |
 | Im(A)   | Column space (span of columns of A)       |
 | rank(A) | Number of nonzero rows in echelon form    |
 +---------+-------------------------------------------+

 +----------------------+-----------------------------------------+
 | Property of T        | Condition                               |
 +----------------------+-----------------------------------------+
 | Injective (one-to-one)| ker(T) = {0}                           |
 | Surjective (onto)    | Im(T) = W                               |
 | Bijective (isomorph.)| ker={0} and Im=W iff dim V=dim W, det!=0|
 +----------------------+-----------------------------------------+


Kernel and image — a universal structure

 The (ker, Im) structure is present for any morphism between structures:

+----------------+--------------+-----------------+-------------------+
| Domain         | Operator     | Ker             | Im                |
+----------------+--------------+-----------------+-------------------+
| Linear algebra | T: V -> W    | ker T sub V     | Im T sub W        |
| Group theory   | phi: G -> H  | ker phi <| G    | Im phi <= H       |
| Diff. forms    | d: Om^k->k+1| ker d (closed)  | Im d (exact)      |
| Diff. equations| L: C^inf     | ker L (solutions)| Im L (reachable) |
| QM (physics)   | H-hat - E   | ker(H-E)=states | spectrum={E:ker!=0}|
+----------------+--------------+-----------------+-------------------+

 Cohomology: H^k = ker(d_k) / Im(d_{k-1})

 Eigenvalues and eigenvectors

 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition                                                    |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                               |
 | A nonzero vector v is called an eigenvector of T (or A) if    |
 |                                                               |
 | T(v) = lambda*v   (or Av = lambda*v)                          |
 |                                                               |
 | The number lambda is called the eigenvalue.                   |
 +---------------------------------------------------------------+

 Geometric meaning of eigenvalues:

 +--------------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
 | Value lambda | Geometric action     | Example                        |
 +--------------+----------------------+--------------------------------+
 | lambda > 1   | Stretching           | lambda=2: doubling             |
 | 0 < lambda <1| Compression          | lambda=0.5: halving            |
 | lambda = 1   | No change            | Direction preserved            |
 | lambda = 0   | Collapse (to 0)      | Projection kills this dir.     |
 | lambda < 0   | Reflection + scaling | lambda=-1: pure reflection     |
 | |lambda| = 1 | Isometry/reflection  | Preserves length               |
 +--------------+----------------------+--------------------------------+

 How to find:

   Av = lambda*v  iff  (A - lambda*I)v = 0  iff  v in ker(A - lambda*I)

   For ker != {0}, need det(A - lambda*I) = 0.

   Characteristic polynomial: p(lambda) = det(A - lambda*I)
   Eigenvalues = roots of p(lambda).

 Invariants (do not depend on the basis):

   det(A) = lambda_1 * lambda_2 * ... * lambda_n
   tr(A) = lambda_1 + lambda_2 + ... + lambda_n

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Diagonalization — choosing the "right" basis
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Diagonalization theorem                                                 |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                         |
 | A matrix A is diagonalizable iff there exists a basis of eigenvectors.  |
 |                                                                         |
 | Then A = P D P^{-1}, where:                                             |
 |   P = [v_1 | v_2 | ... | v_n] — matrix of eigenvectors                 |
 |   D = diag(lambda_1,...,lambda_n) — diagonal of eigenvalues             |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Why it matters:

 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+
 | Problem                  | How it helps                               |
 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+
 | Matrix powers A^n        | P D^n P^{-1}, D^n = diag(lambda_i^n)      |
 | Differential equations   | e^{At} computed via the diagonal           |
 | Stability analysis       | Behavior as t->inf depends on |lambda_i|  |
 +--------------------------+--------------------------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Connection to groups
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Invertible matrices form the group GL(n) — general linear group

   +-----------+------------------+---------------------------------+
   | Subgroup  | Condition        | What it preserves               |
   +-----------+------------------+---------------------------------+
   | GL(n)     | det(A) != 0      | Linear structure                |
   | SL(n)     | det(A) = 1       | + volume                        |
   | O(n)      | A^T A = I        | + lengths and angles            |
   | SO(n)     | A^T A = I, det=1 | + orientation                   |
   | U(n)      | A* A = I (compl.)| Hermitian inner product         |
   | SU(n)     | A* A = I, det=1  | + volume (quantum mechanics)    |
   +-----------+------------------+---------------------------------+

 det: GL(n) -> R* is a group homomorphism
 ker(det) = SL(n) — kernel of the homomorphism

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Matrix exponential — the key to systems of ODEs
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Definition:
 +---------------------------------------------+
 |                                             |
 | e^A = I + A + A^2/2! + A^3/3! + ...        |
 |     = sum A^n/n!                            |
 |                                             |
 | (the series always converges for any A)     |
 +---------------------------------------------+

 Why it matters:
   The linear ODE system  x' = Ax  has solution  x(t) = e^{At} . x(0)

 If A is diagonalizable:

   A = P D P^{-1}

   e^A = P . diag(e^{lambda_1},...,e^{lambda_n}) . P^{-1}

 Properties:
   * e^0 = I
   * e^{A+B} = e^A . e^B  only if AB = BA
   * (e^A)^{-1} = e^{-A}
   * d/dt e^{At} = A . e^{At}
   * det(e^A) = e^{tr(A)}

 Example for thermal physics:

   System of n rooms with temperatures T_1, T_2, ..., T_n:
   T' = A . T, where A is the thermal conductivity matrix

   Solution: T(t) = e^{At} . T(0)

   Eigenvalues of A (all negative) -> decay rates
   Eigenvectors of A -> "modes" of the system (which rooms heat together)


 SVD — singular value decomposition (the king of decompositions)


 Problem: Diagonalization A = PDP^{-1} works only for square matrices,
          and even then not all (need a full set of eigenvectors).

 Solution: SVD works for any m x n matrix!

 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Theorem (SVD): Any matrix A in R^{m x n} decomposes as              |
 |                                                                      |
 | A = U Sigma V^T                                                      |
 |                                                                      |
 | where:                                                               |
 | * U in R^{mxm} — orthogonal, columns = left singular vectors        |
 | * Sigma in R^{mxn} — diagonal, sigma_1 >= sigma_2 >= ... >= 0       |
 | * V in R^{nxn} — orthogonal, columns = right singular vectors       |
 +----------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Geometric meaning:
   Any linear transformation = rotation U x stretching Sigma x rotation V^T

 Connection to eigenvalues:
   * sigma_i^2 = eigenvalues of A^T A (or A A^T)
   * Columns of V = eigenvectors of A^T A
   * Columns of U = eigenvectors of A A^T

 Applications (why SVD is "king"):
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Problem         | How SVD is used                              |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Data compression| A ~ sum_r sigma_i u_i v_i^T (keep first r)  |
 | (images)        | Error = sigma_{r+1} (Eckart-Young theorem)   |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | PCA             | Principal components = right sing. vectors V |
 | (data analysis) | (for centered data)                          |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Pseudoinverse   | A^+ = V Sigma^+ U^T                         |
 | (least squares) | Solution Ax ~ b: x = A^+ b                  |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Matrix rank     | rank(A) = number of nonzero sigma_i          |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Matrix norm     | ||A||_2 = sigma_1 (max singular value)       |
 |                 | ||A||_F = sqrt(sum sigma_i^2) (Frobenius)    |
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+
 | Condition number| cond(A) = sigma_1/sigma_n (error sensitivity)|
 +-----------------+----------------------------------------------+

 Applications
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
| Domain              | How linear algebra is used                               |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     |                                                          |
| Diff. equations     | y' = Ay -> solution y(t) = e^{At} y_0                    |
| (stability)         | Re(lambda)>0: growth, Re(lambda)<0: decay, =0: oscill.  |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     |                                                          |
| Quantum mechanics   | Observables = Hermitian operators (A = A*)               |
|                     | Eigenvalues = measurement outcomes                       |
|                     | Eigenvectors = states                                    |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     |                                                          |
| Data analysis (PCA) | Covariance matrix C = (1/n) X^T X                       |
|                     | Eigenvectors = principal components                      |
|                     | Eigenvalues = variance along directions                  |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     |                                                          |
| Graphs and networks | PageRank = principal eigenvector                         |
|                     | Spectral clustering = eigenvectors of Laplacian          |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+
|                     |                                                          |
| Mechanics           | Natural frequencies = sqrt(lambda)                       |
|                     | Vibration modes = eigenvectors                           |
+---------------------+----------------------------------------------------------+

 One object — three views
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| Algebra                | Geometry            | Analysis               |
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| Matrix A               | Transformation      | Linear system Ax = b   |
| (table of numbers)     | of space            |                        |
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| det(A)                 | Volume change       | Solvability condition  |
| (a number)             | factor + orientation| (det!=0 iff unique sol)|
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| Eigenvector v, Av=lv   | Invariant direction | Solution of            |
|                        | (does not rotate)   | (A - lambda*I)v = 0   |
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| Eigenvalue lambda      | Stretching factor   | Root of characteristic |
|                        | along v             | polynomial det(A-lI)=0|
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+
| Diagonalization        | Choosing the "right"| Separation of          |
| A = P D P^{-1}        | basis (of eigenvec.)| variables              |
+------------------------+---------------------+------------------------+

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Applied example: thermal balance of a heating system
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Problem: A building with 4 rooms. Known heat losses and heat transfer
 between rooms. Find steady-state temperatures.

 Balance equations (for each room):

   Q_i = alpha_i(T_i - T_out) + sum_j k_{ij}(T_i - T_j)
   "Supply = losses outside + exchange with neighbors"

 This is a linear system. In matrix form:  K . T = Q

 Numerical example:
   alpha_i = 100 W/K (heat losses), k_{ij} = 50 W/K (exchange)
   T_out = 0 deg C, Q_1 = 3000 W (heating only in room 1)

   Matrix K:
   +                     +
   | 200  -50    0   -50 |
   | -50  200  -50    0  |
   |   0  -50  200  -50  |
   | -50    0  -50  200  |
   +                     +

   Solution: T = K^{-1} . Q
   T_1 = 20 deg C, T_2 = 10 deg C, T_3 = 5 deg C, T_4 = 10 deg C

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Eigenvalues — relaxation modes
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 If heating is turned off, temperatures will fall.
 How exactly? Solution: T(t) = sum c_i . v_i . e^{-lambda_i * t}

   lambda_i = eigenvalues of K  (relaxation rates)
   v_i = eigenvectors of K      (modes — the "shape" of cooling)

   * Smallest lambda_1 -> slowest mode (entire building together)
   * Largest lambda_n -> fastest mode (equalization between rooms)

 Moral: Linear algebra is the language of thermal calculations.
 Conductivity matrix + eigenvalues = complete understanding of the system.

We have learned to add vectors and multiply them by numbers. But how to
multiply vectors by each other? It turns out there is no single answer —
different problems require different products. This is the key to
understanding tensors.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Vector products — different questions, different answers
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why so many types of multiplication
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Numbers: 2 x 3 = 6. One way of multiplying.

 Vectors: What do we want to know about two vectors?

   * How co-directed are they?           -> Scalar .    -> number
   * What is perpendicular to both?      -> Cross x     -> vector
   * All combinations of components?     -> Tensor (x)  -> matrix
   * What area do they span?             -> Exterior ^  -> bivector

 Different questions require different operations.

+-----------------+-----------+-----------------------------------------------+
| Product         | Result    | Question / geometric meaning                  |
+-----------------+-----------+-----------------------------------------------+
| u . v           | number    | "How co-directed?" = |u||v|cos theta          |
| u x v           | vector    | "What is perp. to both?" (only R^3 and R^7)  |
| u (x) v         | matrix    | "All component combos?" (u(x)v)_{ij} = u_i v_j|
| u ^ v           | bivector  | "What area do they span?" (any dimension)     |
+-----------------+-----------+-----------------------------------------------+

 Main distinction: inner vs outer product

 +-----------------+---------------------------+-------------------------+
 |                 | Inner (., contraction)    | Outer ((x), ^)          |
 +-----------------+---------------------------+-------------------------+
 | Rank change     | DECREASES                 | INCREASES               |
 |                 | vector x vector -> scalar | vector x vector -> matrix|
 +-----------------+---------------------------+-------------------------+
 | Meaning         | CO-DIRECTEDNESS           | NEW OBJECT              |
 |                 | "how parallel"            | in higher-dimensional   |
 |                 |                           | space                   |
 +-----------------+---------------------------+-------------------------+

 Remember: Inner "compresses," outer "expands."

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Why the cross product exists only in R^3 and R^7
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 This is a deep result connected to division algebras over R:

   dim 1:  R (real numbers)
   dim 2:  C (complex numbers)
