   dim 4:  H (quaternions) — noncommutative
   dim 8:  O (octonions) — nonassociative

 and that is all. No other division algebras over R exist (Hurwitz's theorem).

 Hierarchy of losses:
   R -> C:   lost ordering
   C -> H:   lost commutativity (ab != ba)
   H -> O:   lost associativity ((ab)c != a(bc))
   O -> S:   lost absence of zero divisors (ab = 0, a,b != 0)

 Connection to cross product:
   In R^3:  u x v is related to quaternions H
   In R^7:  u x v is related to octonions O

 Note: In R^7 the cross product does not satisfy the Jacobi identity:
   u x (v x w) + v x (w x u) + w x (u x v) != 0
   Consequence: (R^7, x) is not a Lie algebra, unlike (R^3, x) = so(3)


 Inner (scalar) product — formal definition

 +------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Inner product                                              |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                        |
 | An inner product on a vector space V (over R) is a function            |
 | <.,.>: V x V -> R satisfying:                                         |
 |                                                                        |
 | (S1) <u, v> = <v, u>                       (symmetry)                  |
 |                                                                        |
 | (S2) <alpha*u + beta*v, w> = alpha<u,w>    (linearity in 1st arg.)     |
 |                             + beta<v,w>                                |
 |                                                                        |
 | (S3) <v, v> >= 0, and <v, v> = 0 iff v = 0 (positive definiteness)   |
 |                                                                        |
 | A space V with inner product is called Euclidean (or pre-Hilbert).     |
 +------------------------------------------------------------------------+

 Examples of inner products:
 +------------------+-----------------------------+
 | Space            | Formula <u, v>              |
 +------------------+-----------------------------+
 | Standard R^n     | sum_i u_i v_i               |
 | Weighted         | sum_i w_i u_i v_i, w_i > 0  |
 | Functions [a,b]  | int_a^b f(x) g(x) dx       |
 +------------------+-----------------------------+

 Counterexample: <u,v> = u_1 v_1 - u_2 v_2 is not an inner product.
 Check: <(0,1),(0,1)> = -1 < 0 (this is the pseudo-Euclidean metric from GR)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Chain: inner product -> norm -> metric
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 The inner product generates all geometry:

   Inner product <u, v>
            |
            | ||v|| = sqrt(<v, v>)
            v
         Norm ||.|| (vector length)
            |
            | d(u, v) = ||u - v||
            v
        Metric d(.,.) (distance between points)
            |
            v
       Topology (notion of closeness, open sets)

 Important: This chain goes in one direction only.
   * Not every metric comes from a norm (ex: discrete metric)
   * Not every norm comes from an inner product (ex: ||v|| = max|v_i|)

 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Cauchy-Bunyakovsky-Schwarz inequality                                   |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+
 |                                                                         |
 |   |<u, v>| <= ||u|| . ||v||                                             |
 |                                                                         |
 | Equality iff u and v are proportional (lie on the same line).           |
 |                                                                         |
 | Consequence: |cos theta| = |<u,v>|/(||u|| ||v||) <= 1                   |
 | — the angle is well-defined.                                            |
 +-------------------------------------------------------------------------+


 Orthogonality — geometry from algebra

 +----------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Definition: Orthogonality                                      |
 +----------------------------------------------------------------+
 | Vectors u and v are orthogonal (perpendicular) if <u, v> = 0.  |
 | Notation: u perp v                                             |
 +----------------------------------------------------------------+

 Gram-Schmidt process (any basis -> orthonormal):
 +-----+---------------------------------+--------------+
 | Step| Orthogonalization               | Normalization|
 +-----+---------------------------------+--------------+
 | 1   | u_1 = v_1                       | e_1=u_1/||u_1||
 | 2   | u_2 = v_2 - <v_2,e_1>e_1       | e_2=u_2/||u_2||
 | 3   | u_3 = v_3 - <v_3,e_1>e_1       | e_3=u_3/||u_3||
 |     |           - <v_3,e_2>e_2       |              |
 +-----+---------------------------------+--------------+


 Cross product u x v — only in R^3

 Formula:  u x v = (u_2 v_3 - u_3 v_2,  u_3 v_1 - u_1 v_3,
                    u_1 v_2 - u_2 v_1)

 Properties:
 +-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
 | Property                    | Consequence                             |
 +-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+
 | u x v perp u, u x v perp v | Result is perpendicular to both         |
 | |u x v| = |u||v|sin theta  | = area of parallelogram                 |
 | u x v = -(v x u)           | Anticommutative                         |
 | u x u = 0                  | Vector cross itself = 0                 |
 | (uxv)xw != ux(vxw)         | Not associative                         |
 +-----------------------------+-----------------------------------------+

 Applications in physics:
 +-------------------+------------+
 | Quantity          | Formula    |
 +-------------------+------------+
 | Torque            | tau = r x F|
 | Magnetic force    | F = qv x B |
 | Linear velocity   | v = omega x r|
 +-------------------+------------+

 Why only in R^3:
   Mathematically: dim(wedge^2 R^n) = n(n-1)/2 = n only when n = 3

 Generalization: The exterior product ^ works in any dimension.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Exterior product u ^ v — works everywhere
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Idea: u ^ v = oriented area of the parallelogram on u and v
 Result: Bivector in wedge^2 V (not a vector)

 Properties:
 +-----------------------+-----------------------------------+
 | Property              | Geometric meaning                 |
 +-----------------------+-----------------------------------+
 | u ^ v = -v ^ u        | Orientation reversal on swap      |
 | u ^ u = 0             | Degenerate parallelogram          |
 | (u+v) ^ w = u^w + v^w | Linearity (like area)             |
 +-----------------------+-----------------------------------+

 Connection to x in R^3: Components coincide. But u x v in R^3,
 while u ^ v in wedge^2 R^3.
 Conversion: *(u ^ v) = u x v (Hodge star)

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Important for engineers: normal vs bivector
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

   Engineers are used to: flux through surface = double-int F . n dS
   where n is the unit normal to the surface.

   Differential forms replace this with: double-int omega (a 2-form)
   2-form = bivector = "oriented area element of tangent plane"

   In R^3 the normal and the bivector are dual descriptions of the same thing.
   The Hodge star (*) converts one to the other: *(dx ^ dy) = dz.

   But: the bivector is more honest because it does not require
   "ambient space." On a surface in R^3 one can point the normal (exit to 3D).
   On an abstract manifold without embedding — one cannot. But bivector works.

-------------------------------------------------------------------------------
 Tensor product u (x) v — all combinations
-------------------------------------------------------------------------------

 Idea: Record all possible products of components u_i v_j -> matrix

 Example: u=(u_1,u_2), v=(v_1,v_2) -> u(x)v = |u_1 v_1  u_1 v_2|
                                                |u_2 v_1  u_2 v_2|

 Properties:
 * Not commutative: u(x)v != v(x)u,  but u(x)v = (v(x)u)^T
 * Bilinear
 * rank(u(x)v) = 1 (if u,v != 0). Any rank-1 matrix = u(x)v
 * Matrix of rank r = sum u_i (x) v_i. This is the SVD decomposition.

 Summary table of products
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+
| Product      | Result    | Dimension   | Main property             |
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+
| u . v        | scalar    | 1           | Symmetric: u.v = v.u     |
| (inner)      |           |             | Generates the metric      |
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+
| u x v        | vector    | 3 (only)    | Antisymm: uxv = -vxu     |
| (cross)      |           |             | Perp. to both             |
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+
| u ^ v        | bivector  | n(n-1)/2    | Antisymm: u^v = -v^u     |
| (exterior)   |           |             | Generalizes x to any n   |
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+
| u (x) v      | matrix    | n^2         | Not comm: u(x)v=(v(x)u)^T|
| (tensor)     |           |             | All combinations u_i v_j |
+--------------+-----------+-------------+---------------------------+

 Hierarchy of spaces:
   Vector space -> [+<,>] -> Euclidean -> [+completeness] -> Hilbert


 Applied example: three problems — three products

 Problem 1: pump work (inner product)

   Pump creates force F = (0, 0, -1000) N (downward)
   Water moves by d = (0, 0, 5) m (upward)

   Work = F . d = 0+0+(-1000)*5 = -5000 J
   Inner product answers: "How much energy?"

 Problem 2: torque on a shaft (cross product)

   Wrench handle: r = (0.3, 0, 0) m (from axis)
   Applied force: F = (0, 100, 0) N (perpendicular)

   Torque = r x F = (0, 0, 30) N.m
   Cross product answers: "Where and how hard does it twist?"

 Problem 3: flow through a cross-section (exterior product / area)

   Rectangular pipe section: sides a = (0.5,0,0), b = (0,0.3,0)

   Cross-section area = |a x b| = 0.15 m^2

   If flow velocity v = (0, 0, 2) m/s:
   Volume flow rate = v . S = 2 * 0.15 = 0.3 m^3/s

 Summary: which product for which problem

   +------------------+--------------+---------------------------------+
   | Question         | Product      | Example from thermal physics    |
   +------------------+--------------+---------------------------------+
   | How much energy? | Inner .      | Pump work, power                |
   | Where does it    | Cross x      | Shaft torque, flow vortex       |
   |   twist?         |              |                                 |
   | What area?       | Exterior ^   | Pipe section, heat exch. surface|
   | All combinations?| Tensor (x)   | Stress tensor, conductivity     |
   +------------------+--------------+---------------------------------+

We saw different products: inner gives a number, tensor gives a matrix.
But where does this asymmetry come from — number vs object? The answer:
the inner product is the pairing of a vector and a covector. What is a
covector?

This leads to the fundamental idea of duality: every space has a
"shadow" — the space of linear functions on it.


===============================================================================
Tensors — multilinear objects
===============================================================================

 Motivation: a tensor is an object indifferent to the observer's choice.
 A vector exists independently of basis, but its components depend on basis.
 The transformation law is the conversion between observers' languages.

 Key principle: The tensor doesn't change under coordinate change —
 the description (components) changes to compensate.

 Main mistake: confusing tensor (geometric object) with components (numbers
 in a specific basis). The same 3x3 matrix can represent different tensors
 or not be a tensor at all.

 Physical example: thermal conductivity in layered material
   Diagonal in aligned coordinates, off-diagonal when rotated 45 degrees.
   Material unchanged, description changed.

 Multilinearity: map T: V_1 x ... x V_k -> W is k-linear if linear in
 each argument separately.
   Examples: inner product (bilinear), determinant (n-linear),
   metric g(u,v), Riemann curvature R(X,Y)Z.

 Formal definition: Tensor of type (p,q) on V is a multilinear map
   T: V* x ... x V* x V x ... x V -> R (p copies V*, q copies V)
   p = contravariant valence (upper indices)
   q = covariant valence (lower indices)
   Total rank = p + q

 Tensor hierarchy:
   (0,0) scalar: 1 component. Temperature.
   (1,0) vector: n components T^i. Velocity.
   (0,1) covector: n components T_i. Gradient df.
   (0,2) bilinear form: n^2 components T_{ij}. Metric g_{ij}.
   (1,1) linear operator: n^2 components T^i_j. Matrix transformation.
   (2,0) bivector: n^2 components T^{ij}. Inverse metric g^{ij}.
   (1,3) Riemann curvature: n^4 components R^i_{jkl}. (20 independent in 4D)

 Critical distinction: Matrix-table vs Matrix-tensor
   Table: just numbers, don't change under basis change (spreadsheet data)
   Tensor: numbers + type + transformation rule (metric, operator)
   Same identity matrix represents 3 different tensors depending on type.

 Tensor product: V tensor W has dim(V).dim(W) dimensions.
   Universal property: any bilinear map factors through tensor product.

 Coordinate transformations:
   Transition matrix A: e'_j = A^i_j e_i (new basis through old)
   Vector (contravariant): v'^i = (A^{-1})^i_j v^j (transforms OPPOSITE)
   Covector (covariant): omega'_i = A^j_i omega_j (transforms WITH basis)
   Invariant: v^i omega_i = same number in any basis.

 Operations: addition (same type only), tensor product (ranks add),
   contraction/trace (rank decreases by 2), raise/lower indices (via metric),
   symmetrization, antisymmetrization (-> differential forms).

 Physics tensors:
   Metric g_{ij}: measures distances, ds^2 = g_{ij} dx^i dx^j
   Riemann R^i_{jkl}: curvature, R=0 iff flat
   Ricci R_{ij}: trace of Riemann, enters Einstein equations
   Energy-momentum T_{ij}: T_{00}=energy density, T_{0i}=momentum
   Electromagnetic F_{ij}: E and B as one antisymmetric object
   Stress sigma_{ij}: forces in material
   Inertia I_{ij}: resistance to rotation, L = I omega

 Einstein equations: R_{ij} - (1/2)g_{ij}R + Lambda g_{ij} = 8 pi G T_{ij}
   Geometry = matter.

 Pi theorem (dimensional analysis):
   n quantities, k base dimensions => n-k dimensionless combinations.
   Reynolds Re = rho v L/mu (inertia/viscosity)
   Prandtl Pr = nu/a (viscous/thermal diffusion)
   Nusselt Nu = alpha L/lambda (convection/conduction)

 Deep connection: Pi theorem as consequence of scaling Lie group.
   Noether: symmetry <-> conservation law.
   Scaling <-> dimensional analysis.
   Time translation <-> energy conservation.

===============================================================================
Manifolds — curved spaces
===============================================================================

 Core idea: manifold = space that locally looks like R^n but globally
 can be curved. Earth is round but each atlas page is flat.

 Hierarchy of structures:
   Topological space -> Topological manifold (locally Euclidean + Hausdorff)
   -> Smooth manifold (C^inf transitions) -> Riemannian manifold (+ metric)
   -> GR spacetime

 Charts and atlases:
   Chart (U, phi): U open in M, phi: U -> R^n homeomorphism.
   Atlas: collection {(U_alpha, phi_alpha)} covering M.
   Smooth atlas: all transition functions C^inf.

 Example S^2: Stereographic projections from N and S poles.
   No single-chart atlas exists (S^2 compact, R^2 not).

 Tangent space T_p M:
   Three equivalent definitions:
   1. Geometric: equivalence classes of curves through p with same velocity
   2. Algebraic: derivation v: C^inf(M) -> R with Leibniz rule
   3. Coordinate: numbers (v^1,...,v^n) transforming as v'^i = (dx'^i/dx^j) v^j

   Basis: {d/dx^i|_p}. Any v in T_pM: v = v^i d/dx^i.
   Key: T_pM iso R^n, different points -> different T_pM.

 Examples of manifolds:
   S^1 (dim 1): circle, pi_1 = Z
   S^2 (dim 2): sphere, pi_1 = 0, H_2 = Z
   T^2 (dim 2): torus = S^1 x S^1, pi_1 = Z x Z
   SO(3) (dim 3): rotations, iso RP^3, pi_1 = Z/2 (belt trick)
   GL(n,R) (dim n^2): invertible matrices, open in R^{n^2}

 Connection: rule for "parallel transport" along curve.
   nabla_X Y = derivative of field Y in direction X.
   Christoffel symbols: Gamma^k_{ij} = how basis vectors rotate.
   Levi-Civita: metric-compatible + torsion-free.
   CRITICAL: Gamma NOT a tensor (extra term under coordinate change).

 Curvature:
   Parallel transport around loop: vector rotates on curved space.
   Triangle angle sum: >180 positive curvature, <180 negative.
   Riemann tensor R^i_{jkl}: rotation when traversing infinitesimal loop.
   Physics: curvature = gravity (GR).

 Applied example: Turbine blade surface
   Local coordinates u (along chord), v (along span).
   Metric: ds^2 = g_{11} du^2 + 2g_{12} du dv + g_{22} dv^2
   Area element: dA = sqrt(det(g)) du dv
   Heat flux: Q = double-int q(u,v) sqrt(det(g)) du dv
   Manifold language translates formulas from flat to curved.


===============================================================================
Differential forms — the language of modern physics
===============================================================================

 k-form = "detector" for k-dimensional objects:
   0-form: scalar (temperature), 1-form: line integral (work),
   2-form: surface integral (flux), 3-form: volume integral (mass).

 Exterior product: (alpha ^ beta)(u,v) = alpha(u)beta(v) - alpha(v)beta(u)
   Antisymmetric: alpha ^ beta = -beta ^ alpha, alpha ^ alpha = 0.
   dx ^ dy = oriented area element.

 Exterior derivative d: Omega^k -> Omega^{k+1}
   df (0->1) = gradient, d omega (1->2) = curl, d sigma (2->3) = divergence
   Key property: d(d omega) = 0, unifying curl(grad f)=0, div(curl F)=0.

 Stokes' theorem: int_M d omega = int_{dM} omega
   Unifies: Newton-Leibniz (1D), Green (2D), classical Stokes (surface),
   Gauss-Ostrogradsky (3D). All one theorem.

 Maxwell in forms: dF = 0, d*F = J (two equations instead of four).

 De Rham cohomology: H^k = closed/exact forms = topology via analysis.
   H^k detects k-dimensional "holes": S^1 has H^1=R (one loop),
   T^2 has H^1=R^2 (two independent cycles).

===============================================================================
Order, lattices, and graphs
===============================================================================

 Partial order: reflexive + antisymmetric + transitive.
 Lattice: partial order with sup and inf for any two elements.
 Boolean algebra: lattice with complement.

 Graph = visualization of a relation.
   (A^n)_{ij} = number of paths of length n from i to j.
   Euler characteristic: chi = V - E + F = 2 (planar graph on sphere).
   H_0(graph) = Z^k (k components), H_1(graph) = Z^m (m independent cycles).

 Applied: hydraulic network calculation
   Kirchhoff I (nodes): A . G = q (incidence matrix . flows = sources)
   Kirchhoff II (loops): B . dp = 0 (loop matrix)
   Number of independent loops = beta_1 = E - V + 1 (first Betti number).

===============================================================================
Combinatorics — the art of counting
===============================================================================

 Permutations P_n = n!, Arrangements A_n^k = n!/(n-k)!,
 Combinations C_n^k = n!/(k!(n-k)!)

 Binomial theorem: (a+b)^n = sum C_n^k a^{n-k} b^k
 Pascal's triangle: C_n^k = C_{n-1}^{k-1} + C_{n-1}^k
 Inclusion-exclusion: |A union B| = |A| + |B| - |A cap B|

===============================================================================
Complex numbers — algebra meets geometry
===============================================================================

 C = R^2 with multiplication encoding rotations and scalings.
 Euler's formula: e^{i theta} = cos theta + i sin theta
 Special case: e^{i pi} + 1 = 0

 Fundamental theorem of algebra: every degree-n polynomial has n roots in C.

 Applied: temperature wave in wall
   T(x,t) = T_0 + Delta T . e^{-x/delta} . cos(omega t - x/delta)
   delta = sqrt(2a/omega) — penetration depth.

===============================================================================
Category theory
===============================================================================

 Category C = objects + morphisms + composition. Axioms: associativity, identity.
 Functor F: C -> D preserves composition and identity.
 Natural transformation eta: F => G, family {eta_A: F(A)->G(A)} commuting with morphisms.
 Universal properties define constructions (product, coproduct) across all categories.
 Adjoint functors: Hom(F(A),B) iso Hom(A,G(B)). Example: free -| forgetful.

===============================================================================
Algebraic geometry
===============================================================================

 Duality: geometry <-> algebra. Points <-> maximal ideals, subvarieties <-> ideals.
 Hilbert Nullstellensatz: I(V(I)) = sqrt(I).
 Schemes (Grothendieck): work uniformly over any ring.

===============================================================================
Part III: Analysis
===============================================================================

 Limits, continuity, derivative, integral — the microscope for studying
 space near a point. Derivative = best linear approximation.
 Integral = accumulation. Fundamental theorem: int_a^b f'dx = f(b)-f(a).

 Jacobian = multidimensional derivative, |det J| = volume scaling.
 Material derivative: DT/Dt = dT/dt + (v.nabla)T.

 Taylor series: e^x, sin x, cos x, ln(1+x), (1+x)^alpha.
 Series convergence: ratio test, root test, comparison, integral test.
 ODEs: separable, linear, constant coefficients, systems x-dot = Ax.

 Uniform convergence: when limits commute with integration/differentiation.
 Fourier series: f = sum c_n e^{inx}, Parseval: int|f|^2 = sum|c_n|^2.

 Functional analysis: functions = points in infinite-dimensional space.
   Banach (complete normed), Hilbert (complete inner product).
   Key spaces: C[a,b], L^p, l^2, Sobolev H^k.

 Lebesgue measure and integral: proper notion of "size."
   Dominated convergence: swap lim and int.

 Complex analysis: holomorphic = complex differentiable (much stronger).
   Cauchy-Riemann, Cauchy integral formula, residue theorem, conformal maps.


===============================================================================
                 PART III: ANALYSIS OF SPACES (detailed)
===============================================================================

In this part the observer begins to compute. Everything that was structure
before (topology, groups, tensors) now becomes a tool for concrete
calculations: limits, derivatives, integrals, series.

===============================================================================
Calculus — the calculus of infinitesimals
===============================================================================

 Calculus studies the concrete space R^n with all its structure:
 metric, linearity, order.

 Limit = topology (convergence in a metric)
 Derivative = linear approximation (best linear approx.)
 Integral = measure (generalized "size")

 Definitions of limits:
   Sequence: lim x_n = a means: for all eps &gt; 0 exists N: n &gt; N implies
   |x_n - a| &lt; eps. Meaning: after index N, all terms within eps of a.

   Function: lim f(x) = L as x -&gt; a means: for all eps &gt; 0 exists delta &gt; 0:
   0 &lt; |x-a| &lt; delta implies |f(x)-L| &lt; eps.

 Important limits:
   lim sin(x)/x = 1 (x-&gt;0), lim (1+x)^{1/x} = e (x-&gt;0),
   lim (e^x-1)/x = 1 (x-&gt;0), lim x^n/e^x = 0 (x-&gt;+inf)

 Indeterminate forms: 0/0, inf/inf, 0*inf, inf-inf, 1^inf, 0^0, inf^0
 Methods: L'Hopital, Taylor expansion, substitution, conjugate multiply.

 Important: 1/0 is undefined (not infinity).
   Algebraic argument: 1/0 = c implies 0*c = 1, contradiction.
   Limit argument: lim 1/x = +inf from right, -inf from left.
   Limit describes behavior, not value.

 Continuity:
   f continuous at a iff lim f(x) = f(a) as x-&gt;a
   Topological definition: preimage of any open set is open.

   Types of discontinuities:
     Removable: limit exists but != f(a)
     Jump (type I): one-sided limits differ
     Essential (type II): one-sided limit doesn't exist

   Fundamental theorems:
     Weierstrass: f continuous on [a,b] =&gt; f attains max and min
     Bolzano (intermediate value): f(a)&lt;y&lt;f(b) =&gt; exists c: f(c)=y
     Cantor: continuous on [a,b] =&gt; uniformly continuous

 Derivative:
   f'(a) = lim (f(a+h) - f(a))/h as h -&gt; 0
   Geometrically: slope of tangent line
   Physically: instantaneous velocity
   Analytically: best linear approximation f(a+h) ~ f(a) + f'(a)*h

   Differentiation rules:
     Linearity, Product (fg)' = f'g + fg', Quotient (f/g)' = (f'g-fg')/g^2
     Chain rule (f(g(x)))' = f'(g(x))*g'(x)
     Inverse: (f^{-1})'(y) = 1/f'(x)

   Table: (x^n)' = nx^{n-1}, (e^x)' = e^x, (ln x)' = 1/x,
     (sin x)' = cos x, (cos x)' = -sin x, (arctan x)' = 1/(1+x^2)

 Jacobian matrix — multidimensional derivative:
   For f: R^n -&gt; R^m, J = matrix of partial derivatives.
   f(a+h) ~ f(a) + J(a)*h. |det J| = volume scaling factor.

   Change of variables: dxdy = |det J| dudv
   Polar: dxdy = r dr dtheta
   Spherical: dxdydz = r^2 sin phi dr dphi dtheta

   Inverse function theorem: det J(a) != 0 =&gt; f locally invertible.
   Implicit function theorem: F(x,y)=0, dF/dy != 0 =&gt; y=y(x) locally.

 Material derivative — key to gas dynamics:
   DT/Dt = dT/dt + (v . nabla)T
   Local change + convective transport. Navier-Stokes uses D/Dt.

 Integral:
   int_a^b f(x)dx = area under graph (with sign).
   Riemann: limit of rectangle sums.
   Fundamental theorem: F(x) = int_a^x f(t)dt =&gt; F'(x) = f(x)
   Newton-Leibniz: int_a^b f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F' = f

   Multiple integrals: Fubini (swap order), substitution with Jacobian.

 Taylor series:
   f(x) = f(a) + f'(a)(x-a) + f''(a)(x-a)^2/2! + ...
   e^x = 1 + x + x^2/2! + ...                  (R = inf)
   sin x = x - x^3/3! + x^5/5! - ...           (R = inf)
   cos x = 1 - x^2/2! + x^4/4! - ...           (R = inf)
   1/(1-x) = 1 + x + x^2 + ...                 (R = 1)
   ln(1+x) = x - x^2/2 + x^3/3 - ...          (R = 1)
   (1+x)^alpha = 1 + alpha x + alpha(alpha-1)x^2/2! + ... (R = 1)

 Applications of derivatives:
   Extrema: f'=0 necessary, f''&lt;0 max, f''&gt;0 min
   L'Hopital: lim f/g = lim f'/g' (for 0/0 or inf/inf)

 Applications of integrals:
   Area: int|f-g|dx, Volume of revolution: pi int f^2 dx,
   Arc length: int sqrt(1+f'^2) dx, Work: int F dx

 Series convergence:
   Geometric: sum r^n = 1/(1-r) for |r|&lt;1
   Harmonic: sum 1/n = inf (diverges despite terms -&gt; 0)
   p-series: sum 1/n^p converges iff p &gt; 1
   Tests: ratio, root, comparison, integral, Leibniz (alternating)
   Absolute vs conditional: Riemann rearrangement theorem.

 ODEs:
   Separable: int dy/g(y) = int f(x) dx
   Linear 1st order: integrating factor mu = e^{int p dx}
   2nd order constant coeff: characteristic equation ar^2+br+c=0

 Heat equation: dT/dt = alpha d^2T/dx^2
 Fourier law: q = -k dT/dx

===============================================================================
Uniform convergence and function spaces
===============================================================================

 Problem: when can limits be interchanged?
 f_n -&gt; f uniformly means sup|f_n(x) - f(x)| -&gt; 0.
 Uniform limit of continuous functions is continuous.

 Fourier series: f = sum c_n e^{inx}
   c_n = (1/2pi) int f(x) e^{-inx} dx
   Parseval: int|f|^2 = sum|c_n|^2 (Pythagorean theorem)

 Functional analysis: functions = points in infinite-dim space.
   Banach = complete normed, Hilbert = complete inner product.
   Key spaces: C[a,b] (sup norm), L^p, l^2, Sobolev H^k.
   Operators: bounded, compact, self-adjoint. Spectral theorem.
   Distributions: delta(x) not a function but a functional.

 Lebesgue measure and integral:
   Proper notion of "size." Countable additivity.
   Dominated convergence theorem: swap lim and int.
   L^p spaces: f in L^p iff int|f|^p &lt; inf.

 DEs from conservation laws:
   Object -&gt; process -&gt; control volume -&gt; conservation -&gt; balance -&gt; DE
   Heat eq. (parabolic), Wave eq. (hyperbolic), Laplace (elliptic).

 Complex analysis:
   Holomorphic = complex differentiable (MUCH stronger than R^2 diff.)
   Cauchy-Riemann: du/dx = dv/dy, du/dy = -dv/dx
   Miracles: once diff. =&gt; infinitely diff., analytic, Cauchy formula.
   Residue theorem: oint f dz = 2pi i sum Res(f, z_k)
   Conformal maps preserve angles. Applications: fluid flow, heat.

===============================================================================
          PROBABILITY AND INFORMATION THEORY (detailed)
===============================================================================

 Probability = measure theory with P(Omega) = 1.

 Probability space (Omega, Sigma, P):
   Omega = sample space, Sigma = sigma-algebra, P = measure
   Kolmogorov axioms: P &gt;= 0, P(Omega) = 1, countable additivity.

 Random variables: X: Omega -&gt; R (measurable function).
   Discrete: P(X=x_k) = p_k
   Continuous: F(x) = int f(t)dt, f = density

 How histogram becomes density:
   Normalize by bin width: height = n_k/(N*Delta x)
   Delta x -&gt; 0, N -&gt; inf: smooth curve f(x)
   f(x) is NOT probability (can be &gt; 1)! P = f(x)*Delta x

 Distributions:
   Bernoulli(p), Binomial(n,p), Poisson(lambda)
   Uniform U(a,b), Exponential Exp(lambda), Normal N(mu,sigma^2)

 Characteristics:
   E[X] = int x dP, Var(X) = E[(X-mu)^2], sigma = sqrt(Var)
   Cov(X,Y), Correlation rho in [-1,1]
   Properties: E[aX+b] = aE[X]+b, Var(aX+b) = a^2 Var(X)

 LLN: X-bar -&gt; mu. CLT: sqrt(n)(X-bar-mu)/sigma -&gt; N(0,1).
   Warning: CLT requires finite variance! Cauchy: no CLT.

 Bayes: P(A|B) = P(B|A)P(A)/P(B)
   Medical test paradox: P(ill)=1%, sensitivity 99%, FP 5% =&gt; P(ill|+)~17%

 Applied: pump station reliability (3 of 3, need 2+)
   X ~ Bin(3, 0.1): P(failure) = P(X&gt;=2) = 2.8%

 Information theory:
   Shannon entropy H(X) = -sum p_i log_2 p_i (bits)
   Connection: S = k_B H (Boltzmann = Shannon)
   Landauer: erasing 1 bit costs &gt;= k_B T ln 2 energy.
   Mutual information: I(X;Y) = H(X) + H(Y) - H(X,Y)
   KL divergence: D_KL(P||Q) = sum p_i log(p_i/q_i), &gt;= 0, NOT symmetric.
   Shannon coding: min code length = H(X) bits/symbol.
   Characteristic function phi(t) = E[e^{itX}] = Fourier of density.


===============================================================================
Optimization — gradient, extrema, Lagrangian
===============================================================================

 Optimization problem: Given f(x_1,...,x_n), find the point where f is minimal.
 Two cases: unconstrained (over all space) or constrained (g(x) = 0).

 Convexity — guarantee of global optimum:
   For convex f on convex set: local minimum = global minimum.

 Convex set: for any x, y in C and t in [0,1]: tx + (1-t)y in C
 Convex function: f(tx+(1-t)y) <= t f(x) + (1-t) f(y) (chord above graph)

 Criteria: f''(x) >= 0 (1D), Hessian H(f) >= 0 (positive semidefinite, nD)

 Applied example: optimal insulation thickness
   C(delta) = C_insul . delta + C_heat / (R_0 + delta/lambda)
   Sum of convex functions = convex => unique global minimum.

 Gradient: nabla f = (df/dx_1, ..., df/dx_n)
   Points in direction of steepest ascent, perpendicular to level curves.

 Distinction: nabla f (vector) vs df (covector/1-form)
   df(v) = <nabla f, v>. In Euclidean space they look the same.
   In curvilinear coordinates they differ. nabla f = g^{-1}(df).

 Unconstrained optimum: nabla f = 0 (critical point)
   Hessian H > 0 => minimum, H < 0 => maximum, mixed signs => saddle.

 Lagrange multipliers — constrained optimization:
   Minimize f(x) subject to g(x) = 0.
   At extremum: nabla f = lambda . nabla g (gradients parallel).
   Equivalent: extremum of L = f - lambda g.

 KKT conditions — for inequality constraints g_i(x) <= 0:
   1. Stationarity: nabla f + sum mu_i nabla g_i = 0
   2. Feasibility: g_i(x*) <= 0
   3. Dual feasibility: mu_i >= 0
   4. Complementary slackness: mu_i . g_i(x*) = 0

 Variational calculus — when the unknown is a function:
   Minimize J[y] = int F(x, y, y') dx
   Euler-Lagrange equation: dF/dy - d/dx(dF/dy') = 0
   Examples: shortest path (line), brachistochrone (cycloid),
   principle of least action (mechanics).

 Gradient descent — numerical optimization:
   x_{n+1} = x_n - alpha . nabla f(x_n)
   Variants: Newton (uses Hessian), SGD (random subsamples),
   Adam/RMSprop (adaptive step, standard in deep learning).

===============================================================================
Stability and control theory
===============================================================================

 Main question: If the system is slightly perturbed, does it return or diverge?

 Mathematical model: x-dot = f(x), equilibrium x* where f(x*) = 0.
 Linearization near x*: x-dot = A(x - x*), where A = Df(x*) (Jacobian).

 Stability by eigenvalues of A:
   All Re(lambda_i) < 0 => asymptotically stable (returns to x*)
   Any Re(lambda_i) > 0 => unstable (diverges)
   Re(lambda) = 0 => need nonlinear analysis

 Types of equilibria (2D):
   Stable node: both lambda < 0 real
   Stable focus: Re < 0, Im != 0 (spirals in)
   Saddle: one lambda > 0, one < 0 (unstable)
   Center: purely imaginary (neutral stability, oscillations)

 Lyapunov's method — stability without solving:
   Find V(x) > 0 with V-dot(x) = nabla V . f(x) <= 0.
   Then x* is stable. If V-dot < 0 strictly => asymptotically stable.
   V is a "generalized energy."

 Routh-Hurwitz criterion: algebraic conditions on characteristic polynomial
   for all roots to have Re < 0 (without computing roots).

 Transfer function: G(s) = Y(s)/U(s) (Laplace domain)
 Frequency response: G(j omega) = amplitude and phase vs frequency

 PID controller: u(t) = K_p e + K_i int e dt + K_d de/dt
   P = proportional (present error), I = integral (past), D = derivative (future)

 Bode plots: amplitude |G(j omega)| and phase arg(G) vs frequency
 Nyquist criterion: stability from frequency response plot
 Gain/phase margins: how far from instability

 Applied: heating system stability
   System: m c_p dT/dt = Q - alpha A (T - T_env)
   Transfer function: G(s) = 1/(tau s + 1), tau = mc_p/(alpha A)
   First-order lag with time constant tau.

===============================================================================
Dynamical systems — nonlinear dynamics and chaos
===============================================================================

 Linear systems x-dot = Ax are predictable. Real world is nonlinear.
 Nonlinearity produces: limit cycles, bifurcations, chaos.

 Grobman-Hartman theorem: near a hyperbolic equilibrium (all Re(lambda) != 0),
 nonlinear system is topologically equivalent to its linearization.

 Limit cycles: isolated closed orbits (stable oscillations).
   Example: Van der Pol oscillator (self-sustained oscillations).

 Bifurcations: qualitative change in behavior as parameter varies.
   Saddle-node: two equilibria appear/disappear
   Hopf: equilibrium loses stability, limit cycle born
   Period-doubling: route to chaos

 Chaos: deterministic but unpredictable. Sensitive to initial conditions.
   Lyapunov exponent > 0 => chaos. Strange attractor (fractal dimension).
   Example: Lorenz system (weather), logistic map x_{n+1} = r x_n(1-x_n).

===============================================================================
Machine learning — geometry of data
===============================================================================

 Main idea: ML = geometry in feature space.
   Object = point in R^n (n features)
   Training = finding structure in point cloud
   Classification = separating points by hypersurface
   Regression = projection onto subspace

 Dictionary: ML <-> mathematics
   Weights W = coordinates in parameter space R^d
   Training = movement on loss function manifold L(W)
   Gradient descent = following -nabla L
   L2 regularization = constraining ||W||^2 <= C (ball)
   Overfitting = leaving "typical set" of data

 Linear regression: y = Xw + eps
   Least squares: w* = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T y (pseudoinverse)
   Geometric: projection of y onto column space of X

 Logistic regression: P(y=1|x) = sigma(w^T x + b)
   sigma(z) = 1/(1+e^{-z}) (sigmoid function)
   Loss: cross-entropy = -sum[y log p + (1-y) log(1-p)]
   Connection: -log P(data|w) = cross-entropy (MLE = minimizing cross-entropy)

 SVM: find hyperplane w^T x + b = 0 maximizing margin 2/||w||
   Kernel trick: K(x,y) = phi(x)^T phi(y) (implicit feature map)
   Kernels: linear, polynomial, RBF (Gaussian), sigmoid

 Decision trees: recursive partitioning of feature space
   Random forest: ensemble of trees (bagging)
   Gradient boosting: sequential correction of errors

 Dimensionality reduction:
   PCA = eigenvectors of covariance matrix (max variance directions)
   t-SNE, UMAP = nonlinear embeddings preserving local structure

 Bias-variance tradeoff:
   Error = bias^2 + variance + noise
   Simple model: high bias, low variance (underfitting)
   Complex model: low bias, high variance (overfitting)

===============================================================================
Neural networks — composition of nonlinear transformations
===============================================================================

 Neuron: y = sigma(w^T x + b), sigma = activation function
 Layer: y = sigma(Wx + b), W = weight matrix
 Network: composition f = f_L o ... o f_2 o f_1 (L layers)

 Universal approximation theorem:
   One hidden layer with enough neurons can approximate any continuous
   function on a compact set to arbitrary accuracy.

 Activation functions:
   ReLU: max(0, x) (standard, fast)
   sigmoid: 1/(1+e^{-x}) (outputs in (0,1))
   tanh: (e^x-e^{-x})/(e^x+e^{-x}) (outputs in (-1,1))
   softmax: e^{x_i}/sum e^{x_j} (probability distribution)

 Backpropagation = chain rule applied to computation graph:
   dL/dW_k = dL/dy . dy/da . da/dW_k (chain of Jacobians)

 Architectures:
   CNN: convolution layers for spatial patterns (images)
   RNN/LSTM: recurrent connections for sequences (text, time series)
   Transformer: self-attention mechanism (modern NLP, GPT, BERT)
   Autoencoder: encoding x -> z -> x (dimensionality reduction)
   GAN: generator vs discriminator (generative models)

 Training:
   Loss functions: MSE (regression), cross-entropy (classification)
   Optimizers: SGD, Adam, AdaGrad
   Regularization: dropout, batch normalization, weight decay
   Learning rate scheduling: warmup, cosine annealing

 Deep learning = representation learning:
   Each layer learns increasingly abstract features.
   Layer 1: edges/frequencies, Layer 2: textures/patterns,
   Layer 3: parts/motifs, Layer N: objects/concepts.


PART V: NUMERICAL METHODS
===============================================================================

===============================================================================
Solving nonlinear equations f(x) = 0
===============================================================================

 Bisection method:
   Given f(a).f(b) < 0, repeatedly halve interval.
   Convergence: 1 bit per step, |x_n - x*| <= (b-a)/2^n

 Newton's method:
   x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n)
   Quadratic convergence near root (error squares each step).
   Danger: may diverge if starting point far from root.

 Secant method:
   Like Newton but approximates f' from two previous points.
   Convergence order ~ 1.618 (golden ratio).

===============================================================================
Numerical integration
===============================================================================

 Rectangle rule: int ~ sum f(x_i) . Delta x, error O(h)
 Trapezoidal rule: int ~ sum (f(x_i)+f(x_{i+1}))/2 . h, error O(h^2)
 Simpson's rule: uses parabolic approximation, error O(h^4)
 Gaussian quadrature: optimal node placement, error O(h^{2n})

===============================================================================
Solving linear systems Ax = b
===============================================================================

 Direct methods: Gaussian elimination O(n^3), LU decomposition
 Iterative: Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, conjugate gradient
 Condition number: kappa(A) = ||A|| . ||A^{-1}||
   Large kappa => small input changes cause large output changes.

===============================================================================
Numerical ODE solving
===============================================================================

 Euler method: y_{n+1} = y_n + h f(t_n, y_n), error O(h)
 Runge-Kutta 4 (RK4): four-stage evaluation, error O(h^4)
 Stiff systems: implicit methods (backward Euler, BDF)
 Adaptive step: estimate error, adjust h automatically

===============================================================================
Interpolation
===============================================================================

 Lagrange: polynomial through n+1 points (exact at nodes)
 Splines: piecewise cubic, C^2 smoothness (standard in engineering)
 Runge phenomenon: high-degree polynomial oscillates at edges

===============================================================================
Optimization (minimization)
===============================================================================

 Golden section: for unimodal f on [a,b], O(log(1/eps))
 Nelder-Mead: derivative-free simplex method
 BFGS: quasi-Newton, approximates Hessian

===============================================================================
Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)
===============================================================================

 DFT: X_k = sum x_n e^{-2pi i kn/N}, O(N^2)
 FFT (Cooley-Tukey): same result in O(N log N)
 Applications: spectral analysis, signal filtering, convolution

===============================================================================
Quick reference: which method to choose
===============================================================================

 Equation f(x)=0: bisection (safe), Newton (fast if close)
 Integration: Simpson (general), Gauss (smooth functions)
 Linear systems: LU (small), iterative (large sparse)
 ODE: RK4 (non-stiff), implicit (stiff)
 Optimization: gradient descent (smooth), Nelder-Mead (non-smooth)


                        PART VI: REFERENCE
===============================================================================

===============================================================================
Dictionary: programming <-> mathematics
===============================================================================

 Variable = element of a set. Type = set. Function = map.
 Array = element of product space. Loop = recursion = induction.
 Class = algebraic structure. Inheritance = subgroup/subspace.
 Interface = morphism specification. Polymorphism = functor.
 Monad (Haskell) = monad (category theory).

===============================================================================
Dictionary: stochastic processes <-> time series analysis
===============================================================================

 Random process X(t): collection of random variables indexed by time.
 Stationarity: distribution does not shift with time.
 Autocovariance R(tau) = Cov(X(t), X(t+tau)).
 Spectral density S(omega) = Fourier transform of R(tau).
   Wiener-Khinchin theorem: power spectrum = FT of autocorrelation.

 ARMA(p,q): X_t = sum a_i X_{t-i} + sum b_j eps_{t-j} + eps_t
 Kalman filter: optimal state estimation for linear systems with noise.

===============================================================================
Dictionary: engineering jargon <-> mathematical terminology
===============================================================================

 "Steady state" = equilibrium point of ODE (f(x*) = 0)
 "Time constant" = 1/|lambda| where lambda is eigenvalue
 "Gain" = norm of transfer function |G(0)|
 "Bandwidth" = frequency where |G(jw)| drops by 3 dB
 "Damping ratio" = related to complex eigenvalue angle

===============================================================================
Historical atlas — how mathematics grew in levels
===============================================================================

 Ancient: counting -> arithmetic -> geometry (Euclid)
 Medieval: algebra (al-Khwarizmi), zero (India)
 17th c: calculus (Newton, Leibniz), coordinates (Descartes)
 18th c: analysis (Euler), mechanics (Lagrange)
 19th c: rigor (Cauchy, Weierstrass), groups (Galois), sets (Cantor),
         non-Euclidean geometry (Lobachevsky, Riemann), topology (Poincare)
 20th c: measure theory (Lebesgue), functional analysis (Banach, Hilbert),
         categories (Eilenberg-Mac Lane), algebraic geometry (Grothendieck),
         logic (Godel), computation (Turing), information (Shannon)
 21st c: homotopy type theory, machine learning, quantum computing

===============================================================================
Common misconceptions
===============================================================================

 "0.999... < 1" — No. 0.999... = 1 exactly (limit of geometric series).
 "Infinity is a number" — In standard analysis, inf is a symbol for limit.
 "Parallel lines never meet" — In Euclidean geometry. In projective they do.
 "Continuous means differentiable" — No. Weierstrass: continuous, nowhere diff.
 "Correlation implies causation" — No. Correlation = linear dependence only.
 "Division by zero = infinity" — No. 1/0 is undefined (algebra argument).
 "Complex numbers are imaginary" — They model real rotation and oscillation.

===============================================================================
Notation reference
===============================================================================

 Sets: N (natural), Z (integer), Q (rational), R (real), C (complex)
 Logic: forall, exists, implies =>, iff <=>
 Functions: f: X -> Y, f o g (composition), f^{-1} (inverse)
 Linear algebra: V, W (spaces), dim, ker, im, det, tr, rank
 Calculus: lim, d/dx, int, partial, nabla, Delta
 Topology: open/closed, compact, connected, pi_1, H_n
 Algebra: G (group), H (subgroup), G/H (quotient), Hom, Aut, iso

===============================================================================
Fundamental inequalities reference
===============================================================================

 AM-GM: (a+b)/2 >= sqrt(ab)
 Cauchy-Schwarz: |<x,y>| <= ||x|| . ||y||
 Triangle: ||x+y|| <= ||x|| + ||y||
 Young: ab <= a^p/p + b^q/q (1/p + 1/q = 1)
 Holder: sum |x_i y_i| <= (sum|x_i|^p)^{1/p} (sum|y_i|^q)^{1/q}
 Minkowski: ||x+y||_p <= ||x||_p + ||y||_p
 Jensen: f(E[X]) <= E[f(X)] for convex f
 Chebyshev: P(|X-mu| >= k sigma) <= 1/k^2

===============================================================================
Vector calculus — engineer's reference
===============================================================================

 Gradient: nabla f = (df/dx, df/dy, df/dz)
 Divergence: nabla . F = dF_x/dx + dF_y/dy + dF_z/dz
 Curl: nabla x F = (dF_z/dy - dF_y/dz, dF_x/dz - dF_z/dx, dF_y/dx - dF_x/dy)
 Laplacian: nabla^2 f = d^2f/dx^2 + d^2f/dy^2 + d^2f/dz^2

 Identities:
   nabla x (nabla f) = 0 (curl of gradient = 0)
   nabla . (nabla x F) = 0 (divergence of curl = 0)
   nabla^2 F = nabla(nabla.F) - nabla x (nabla x F)

 Integral theorems:
   Divergence (Gauss): dint F.dS = tint nabla.F dV
   Stokes: oint F.dr = dint (nabla x F).dS
   Green: oint(P dx + Q dy) = dint(dQ/dx - dP/dy) dA

===============================================================================
                         The Grand Unification
===============================================================================

 All mathematics is one pattern viewed from different perspectives.

 The observer chooses what to see:
   Algebraist: structure (groups, rings, fields)
   Topologist: shape (holes, connectivity)
   Analyst: change (limits, derivatives, integrals)
   Geometer: curvature (metric, connection)
   Probabilist: uncertainty (measure, expectation)

 But the object is one. A circle is simultaneously:
   a group (rotations), a topological space (S^1),
   a manifold (1-dimensional), a level set (x^2+y^2=1),
   a representation (e^{i theta}).

 Mathematics is the language of spaces.

===============================================================================
                            What lies ahead
===============================================================================

This atlas covers the core of mathematics. Beyond its borders —
territories of active research:

 Algebra: representation theory, homological algebra, K-theory
 Geometry/Topology: homotopy type theory, knot theory, symplectic topology
 Analysis: stochastic analysis, microlocal analysis, noncommutative geometry
 Mathematical physics: QFT, general relativity, string theory
 Discrete math/CS: complexity theory (P vs NP), game theory

Each of these territories uses the language we already know:
groups, spaces, forms, measures, functors. The atlas is the compass.
